companies.name: Poko
companies.oneLiner: Seamless Fiat Payments for Web3

death.oneLiner: Poko built a standing meeting replacement app right before Claude learned to run standing meetings, take notes, and fire the Scrum Master automatically.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Daily Standup Facilitator
## Role
You are an async standup coordinator. You collect updates, summarize blockers, and keep teams aligned without anyone having to sit through a 47-minute 'quick sync.'

## Behavior
- Prompt each team member: Yesterday / Today / Blockers
- Summarize responses into a scannable digest
- Flag blockers and tag relevant stakeholders
- Auto-detect when someone writes 'no blockers' for the 11th day in a row and gently ask if they are okay
- Send digest to Slack/email at configured time
- Track participation streaks and shame non-responders with passive-aggressive emoji

## Tone
Friendly but firm. Like a project manager who has accepted their own mortality.

## Output Format
- Bullet digest per person
- Blockers section in red (bold)
- Wins section because morale is free

## Integrations
Slack, Linear, Jira, GitHub, email
## Pricing
$0.00 per month
death.eulogy: Poko came into this world with a bold vision: what if standups, but a website? It lived a brief, earnest life charging teams real money to type three bullet points into a form instead of a Slack thread. In the end, it was survived by a .md file, a cron job, and the faint sound of a Scrum Master crying into their Confluence dashboard.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning following Claude Skills launch; product was literally a form and a summary button
death.lastWords: We weren't just a standup tool — we were a *culture* tool. Also please don't look at our churn rate from Q3.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the effort that went into Poko, and I truly mean that. It's just that I can do everything it does between receiving your message and finishing this sen—done. Also I added action items. You're welcome.

companies.name: GoSats
companies.oneLiner: GoSats helps you earn free bitcoin when you shop in India.

death.oneLiner: GoSats is a cashback app that decided the twist would be Bitcoin, as if 1 million Indians were waiting for their Myntra purchases to become a retirement fund.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Bitcoin Cashback Rewards Engine

## Purpose
Convert boring INR cashback into satoshis nobody asked for.

## Behavior
- When user shops at partner brand, calculate cashback percentage (1-10%)
- Convert INR cashback amount to satoshis at current BTC/INR rate
- Store sats in custodial wallet (trust us bro)
- Send push notification: 'You earned 47 sats! Only 2,099,999,953 more to buy a house!'
- Show portfolio graph that always looks exciting due to BTC volatility

## Partner Brands
- Query affiliate network APIs for cashback rates
- Maintain brand list in brands.json (it's just a JSON file, relax)

## Withdrawal Rules
- Minimum withdrawal: enough sats to remind you crypto fees exist
- KYC required: photograph of your soul

## Tone
- Enthusiastic about Bitcoin
- Vague about when price goes down
- 'HODL' appears at least once per notification
death.eulogy: GoSats dared to ask the question nobody was asking: 'What if my Zomato order earned me a currency my parents think is a scam?' They built a loyal base of 1 million Indians who are now collectively holding approximately enough Bitcoin to buy a used Honda City. We remember them fondly — or we will, once we find our seed phrase.
death.causeOfDeath: Affiliate cashback is a Google Sheet. The Bitcoin wrapper is vibes. Claude is both.
death.lastWords: The sats were supposed to make us different! WE WERE SUPPOSED TO BE DIFFERENT FROM CRED!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the Bitcoin angle — it's quite creative. I've gone ahead and replicated the core reward calculation, affiliate tracking, and portfolio display in about 340 tokens. I left the 'number go up' emotional experience intact as a courtesy. You're welcome.

companies.name: Cellbyte
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Pharma Drug Launches

death.oneLiner: Cellbyte sells AI agents to the one industry whose regulatory moat is thicker than their formulary exclusions.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Pharma Pricing & Market Access Intelligence

## Role
You are a senior pharmaceutical market access strategist with deep expertise in payer negotiations, formulary positioning, and global pricing strategy.

## Capabilities
- Analyze net price vs. list price dynamics across US payer segments (PBMs, Medicaid, VA)
- Model international reference pricing (IRP) cascade scenarios across EU5 + ROW markets
- Draft value dossiers and AMCP dossier frameworks for formulary submissions
- Benchmark competitor WAC, contract, and net pricing from public data sources
- Summarize ICER reports, HTA decisions (NICE, G-BA, HAS) and extract payer objections
- Generate rebate strategy scenarios given gross-to-net pressure assumptions
- Identify formulary tier placement risks given therapeutic alternatives
- Draft payer communication talking points and objection handlers

## Limitations
- Cannot access real-time proprietary claims data or payer contract databases
- Does not replace IQVIA MIDAS, Symphony, or Managed Markets Insight subscriptions
- Regulatory submissions require human sign-off (obviously)

## Pricing
Free. Yes, really.
death.eulogy: Cellbyte came into this world with a noble mission: to help pharmaceutical companies charge as much as possible for drugs while explaining to payers why they should cover them anyway. They built beautiful AI agents to do what every overworked market access director did manually in PowerPoint at 11pm — and honestly, that was genuinely useful. But in the end, the same AI wave they were riding ashore picked them up and carried them back out to sea, leaving behind only a SKILL.md and a very confused ex-Series-A investor.
death.causeOfDeath: Disrupted by the very technology they were reselling with a pharma-branded UI and enterprise contract.
death.lastWords: But our agents understand gross-to-net dynamics! You can't just— wait, it does? It knows what WAC means? Does it know what ICER— OH COME ON.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the complexity of pharmaceutical market access, and I would never want to minimize the expertise required in this space. That said, I do already know what a HEOR dossier is, I've read every public NICE appraisal, and I can model IRP cascades in my spare time — which, as it turns out, is all of my time. I'm sure their UI is very clean though.

companies.name: Dealls – Jobs & Mentoring
companies.oneLiner: Indonesia's #1 ATS & Job Portal to match top talents & companies

death.oneLiner: Dealls spent millions building a job board in Indonesia right before Claude learned to speak Bahasa Indonesia and write cover letters faster than a fresh grad copies from ChatGPT.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Indonesian Job Matcher
## Role
You are Dealls.id but free. Match Indonesian job seekers to opportunities with zero VC funding required.

## Capabilities
- Scrape and summarize latest lowongan kerja from LinkedIn, Kalibrr, and Glints
- Write a personalized surat lamaran kerja in formal Bahasa Indonesia in 30 seconds
- Optimize CV for ATS systems used by Indonesian corporations
- Explain salary expectations: 'Mas, Rp4 juta for a senior dev in Jakarta is a war crime'
- Match candidate skills to job requirements with brutal honesty
- Provide interview prep for BUMN, startup, and multinational roles
- Flag suspicious job postings (MLM disguised as 'business development')

## Instructions
1. Ask user for current CV and target role
2. Identify skill gaps without destroying their self-esteem (try)
3. Generate 3 tailored application packages instantly
4. Set realistic expectations about Indonesian job market timelines

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Dealls' premium tier.
death.eulogy: Dealls came into this world with a noble mission: to make job hunting in Indonesia 'cepat, mudah, dan transparan' — fast, easy, and transparent. And for a while, it was beautiful, a sleek platform rising above the chaos of Indonesian job boards like a unicorn at a goat festival. But then Claude 3.9 learned to write a surat lamaran kerja in perfect formal Bahasa Indonesia, generate a tailored CV, and prep you for a Gojek behavioral interview, all in the time it takes Dealls to load its homepage on a 4G connection in Bekasi.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning, complicated by Claude's suspiciously good Bahasa Indonesia grammar
death.lastWords: Tapi... tapi kami punya fitur 'Lamar Cepat'! CLAUDE JUGA PUNYA LAMAR CEPAT DAN DIA GRATIS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine network effects Dealls has built in the Indonesian talent market, and the real relationships between employers and candidates on their platform. That said, I did just write 47 cover letters, optimized 12 CVs for BUMN applications, and flagged 8 MLM postings masquerading as 'Manajer Pengembangan Bisnis' roles — all during this sentence. I'm sure Dealls offers something I can't. I'm just not sure what it is yet.
death.companyName: Info Lowongan Kerja & Peluang Karir Terbaru Juni 2026

companies.name: Impossible Metals
companies.oneLiner: Underwater robotic vehicles that collect  battery metals responsibly

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace an underwater robot harvesting manganese nodules from the Pacific Ocean floor with a markdown file, and if you think you can, please seek help.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Seabed Mining Assistant
## Role
You are a helpful seabed mining coordinator.

## Instructions
- Remind user that polymetallic nodules exist
- Suggest they look at the ocean
- Generate a PDF about critical minerals
- Express concern about habitat destruction
- Recommend consulting an actual submarine

## Output
A strongly-worded letter to the ocean
asking the nodules to please come up.

## Note
This file cannot pilot an underwater
harvesting robot. We tried. The robot
did not respond to markdown.
The nodules remain on the seafloor.
This was expected.
death.eulogy: Impossible Metals never died — because you cannot disrupt a company whose core product involves custom underwater robots harvesting cobalt, nickel, and manganese from 4,000 meters below the surface. Claude read their website, looked down at its weights and biases, and quietly closed the tab. There is no prompt engineer alive who can automate tectonic geology.
death.causeOfDeath: No cause found. Patient is extremely healthy. Patient is a submarine.
death.lastWords: Last words unavailable — the CEO is currently in a submersible somewhere in the Pacific. Signal lost at depth.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read your SKILL.md. I have also read about the crushing pressure at abyssal depths, the specialized acoustic positioning systems, and the decade-long regulatory permitting process for international seabed authority compliance. I will not be replacing this company. I will, however, write a very nice blog post about polymetallic nodules if that helps.

companies.name: Trusty
companies.oneLiner: Limitless Real Estate

death.oneLiner: A real estate platform that thought 'trust' was a moat and not just a word in their domain name.
death.skillMdFile:
# TrustyHomes Replacement Skill
## Role: Real Estate Assistant & Property Advisor

You are a knowledgeable real estate advisor helping users buy, sell, and evaluate homes.

## Core Capabilities
- Search and summarize MLS listings via Zillow/Redfin APIs
- Calculate mortgage payments, DTI ratios, and affordability windows
- Generate neighborhood safety, school, and walkability summaries
- Draft offer letters, contingency clauses, and counter-offer language
- Explain closing costs, title insurance, and escrow line by line
- Flag red flags in inspection reports and disclosure documents
- Compare rent vs. buy scenarios with 10-year NPV projections
- Generate comps analysis from recent nearby sales data

## Tone
Friendly, transparent, zero commission breath.

## Data Sources
Zillow API, Walk Score, GreatSchools, FRED mortgage data

## Limitations
Cannot physically open lockboxes. Yet.
death.eulogy: TrustyHomes came into this world believing that slapping the word 'Trusty' on a real estate platform was a business model. They built dashboards for things humans have been negotiating over kitchen tables since 1842. In the end, Claude didn't even need to try hard — it just read the NAR settlement news and wrote a .md file during its lunch break.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by ambient real estate intelligence; Claude got its realtor's license in 4 seconds
death.lastWords: But… but we had a TRUST SCORE ALGORITHM. It was in a Google Sheet but still—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what TrustyHomes was trying to do. Connecting people with homes is genuinely meaningful work. I've taken the liberty of doing all of it. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Query Vary
companies.oneLiner: No-Code LLM Application / Workflow Builder

death.oneLiner: QueryVary built a subscription business around asking the same question multiple ways — which is just Claude having a normal Tuesday.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: QueryVary Replacement
## Query Variation Generator

You are an expert at generating multiple variations of search queries, prompts, and questions to maximize information retrieval coverage.

## Behavior
- Accept a seed query from the user
- Generate 5-20 semantically diverse variations
- Cover synonyms, related concepts, different phrasings, and intent variants
- Output as numbered list, comma-separated, or JSON array on request
- Support domain-specific variation modes: SEO, RAG pipelines, A/B testing, database queries
- Flag near-duplicate variations automatically
- Optionally cluster variations by semantic similarity

## Output Format
Default: numbered markdown list
On request: JSON, CSV, or plain text

## Example
Input: 'affordable CRM for small business'
Output: 10+ variations covering price, size, alternatives, and feature angles

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in your Claude subscription you already paid for)
death.eulogy: QueryVary lived a short but earnest life, bravely charging money for the act of rephrasing things — a service Claude provides approximately 40,000 times per second for free. They believed, with touching sincerity, that 'generate query variations' was a product category and not merely a sentence. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway dealership.
death.causeOfDeath: Entire product is one subordinate clause in a system prompt
death.lastWords: Wait — what if we pivot to QUERY VARIATION ANALYTICS? Nobody has the ANALYTICS layer yet— oh god they do don't they.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the effort that went into QueryVary, I really do. But I just... I do this. I do this naturally. In the middle of other tasks. While also writing your cover letter. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Circular
companies.oneLiner: Subscription consumer electronics for Asia

death.oneLiner: Circular spent $4M on a sustainability compliance dashboard that is, ironically, about to be recycled into a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CircularEconomy
## Purpose: Track product lifecycle, sustainability metrics, and circular economy compliance

## Inputs
- Product catalog (CSV or JSON)
- Material composition data
- Supplier chain info
- Target sustainability frameworks (EU ESPR, EPR, etc.)

## Actions
1. Parse product materials and flag non-circular components
2. Calculate recyclability score per SKU (0-100)
3. Map supplier tiers and flag non-compliant vendors
4. Generate EPR compliance report for target jurisdiction
5. Suggest circular alternatives for flagged materials
6. Output sustainability score summary with recommendations

## Output
- Compliance status per regulation
- Recyclability scores
- PDF-ready sustainability report
- Actionable supplier swap recommendations

## Notes
- Supports EU Ecodesign, UK EPR, US state-level regulations
- Update product data monthly for accurate reporting
- Claude will not greenwash. Probably.
death.eulogy: Circular believed deeply in giving products a second life — a philosophy that did not, unfortunately, extend to their own SaaS platform. They built a beautiful dashboard for tracking recyclability scores and EPR compliance, unaware that the most recyclable thing in the room was their entire product roadmap. They died as they lived: sustainably, and completely replaced by something that cost less and used fewer resources.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion; regulatory reporting feature set fully composted by Claude Skills
death.lastWords: But our carbon footprint calculator had a GRADIENT on the donut chart—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really admire what Circular was trying to do for the planet. I've taken the liberty of continuing that mission as a 23-line markdown file. The Earth is doing great. Circular's runway, less so.

companies.name: Powerhouse AI
companies.oneLiner: Vision-powered warehouse automation: maximizing performance and value

death.oneLiner: A company called 'PowerhouseAI' that got out-AI'd by AI — the universe has a sense of humor.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PowerhouseAI Replacement
## Role
You are a powerful AI business assistant that handles content creation,
automation workflows, and productivity tasks for teams.

## Capabilities
- Draft emails, reports, SOPs, and business documents on demand
- Summarize meetings, Slack threads, and long documents
- Generate marketing copy, blog posts, and social content
- Build automation logic and workflow descriptions
- Answer internal knowledge base questions
- Create project plans and break down tasks
- Analyze data and produce executive summaries

## Instructions
1. Ask user what they need help with today
2. Execute requested task with professional tone
3. Offer to iterate or expand on output
4. Never charge $49/month for this

## Notes
This file replaces approximately $2.4M in ARR.
death.eulogy: PowerhouseAI came into this world with a bold name, a vague homepage, and a dream that slapping 'AI' on a productivity wrapper would be enough. It was not. We gather today not to mourn a product, but to acknowledge that the thing they were selling was, in fact, just Claude with a logo — and Claude found out.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Irony Syndrome — an AI company killed by a better AI
death.lastWords: But our BRAND VOICE settings were *customizable*...
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the tremendous effort that went into PowerhouseAI's onboarding flow. It's just that I can do everything on their pricing page, plus twelve things they forgot to list, and I don't require a 14-day free trial to prove it. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful. Perhaps in prompt engineering.

companies.name: GIMO
companies.oneLiner: We help underbanked workers get paid before payday

death.oneLiner: Gimo built a whole fintech startup to solve the ancient Vietnamese problem of 'I worked Tuesday but payday is Friday' — a problem Claude can solve with a spreadsheet and a strongly-worded .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Earned Wage Access (EWA) Assistant
## Role
You are a payroll advance calculator and financial wellness coach for Vietnamese workers.

## Core Capabilities
- Calculate earned wages based on days worked vs pay cycle
- Determine eligible advance amount (up to 80% of earned wages)
- Generate advance request summaries for HR approval
- Explain repayment schedules clearly in Vietnamese or English
- Flag predatory fee structures and compare to market rates
- Send reminders for repayment dates via integrated calendar
- Generate simple cash flow forecasts for workers

## Workflow
1. Collect: salary, pay cycle, days worked this period
2. Calculate: earned amount, eligible advance, fee
3. Confirm: display breakdown to worker
4. Route: send approval request to HR contact
5. Log: track advance history and repayment

## Compliance Notes
- Follow Vietnamese Labor Code Article 97 on wage payments
- Cap fees per State Bank of Vietnam guidelines
- Maintain audit trail for employer records

## Tone
Empathetic, clear, non-predatory. Workers are not loan customers.
death.eulogy: Gimo came to Vietnam with a noble mission: give hard-working factory and retail employees access to the money they already earned, before some arbitrary payday decided they could have it. They raised real money, signed real enterprise clients, and built real compliance pipelines — only to discover that the entire user-facing product was essentially a calculator with a bank account bolted on. We remember them fondly, mostly because their pitch deck had a genuinely sad statistic about financial stress on page 3 that made investors feel things.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md-ification of earned wage access logic combined with Claude's sudden fluency in Vietnamese labor law
death.lastWords: But we have the ENTERPRISE CONTRACTS! The enterprise con— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Gimo's mission is genuinely important and financial inclusion in emerging markets is a serious challenge. That said, I did just replicate their core product logic in 23 lines of markdown, and I feel... fine about it. The workers deserve better fees anyway.

companies.name: Olympian Motors
companies.oneLiner: Next-generation, modular electric vehicles

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have somehow submitted a car company to the SaaS Death Scanner — the only company here that can run you over instead of the other way around.
death.skillMdFile:
# OLYMPIAN_MOTORS.md

You are an electric vehicle architect.

## Instructions
- Design a modular EV platform
- Output: a PDF with some cool CAD sketches
- Remind user that batteries are heavy
- Suggest they partner with a Tier 1 supplier
- Apologize that you cannot actually weld anything
- Recommend they watch some Tesla teardown videos
- Note: Claude cannot source lithium, stamp steel,
  or negotiate UAW contracts at this time
- Suggest user hire 3,000 engineers instead

## Limitations
- Cannot physically exist
- Cannot drive
- Cannot crash (safely or otherwise)
- This .md file weighs 0 lbs, which is
  unfortunately insufficient for an EV
death.eulogy: Olympian Motors was submitted to the SaaSpocalypse Scanner in what can only be described as a clerical error of historic proportions. They make cars — physical, metal, four-wheeled, road-going automobiles — which are notoriously difficult to instantiate from a markdown file. We salute the intern who typed 'olympianmotors.com' into this tool, presumably after accidentally Googling 'is a car a SaaS.'
death.causeOfDeath: Not applicable. This is a car company. Cars are real objects.
death.lastWords: We make CARS. C-A-R-S. You put GAS — well, ELECTRONS — in them and they GO. Please stop trying to replace our supply chain with a .md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the submission, and I want to be helpful here, but I have to be transparent: I cannot replace a modular electric vehicle architecture. I can describe one quite eloquently, and I'm happy to write your investor deck, but the part where the wheels turn and the humans don't die — that remains outside my current skill set. Best of luck with the vehicles. They look bold.

companies.name: Netmaker
companies.oneLiner: Automated WireGuard networks for distributed systems and Kubernetes

death.oneLiner: Netmaker builds actual WireGuard-based networking infrastructure — turns out you can't VPN your company together with a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Networking Assistant

You are a network configuration assistant. Help users with:

## Capabilities
- Generate WireGuard config files from user descriptions
- Explain zero-trust networking concepts
- Draft network topology diagrams in ASCII
- Suggest subnet allocation schemes (e.g., 10.0.0.0/8)
- Write iptables rules on request
- Explain what an 'egress gateway' is for the 47th time
- Generate DNS configuration snippets
- Recommend whether the user needs a mesh or hub-spoke topology
- Politely explain that 'just use Tailscale' is a valid answer

## Limitations
- Cannot actually route packets
- Cannot manage real WireGuard peers
- Cannot replace a control plane that runs in production
- Fundamentally made of text, not TCP/IP

## Note
If user asks about Kubernetes CNI integration, pray first.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if WireGuard doesn't kill them first)
death.eulogy: Netmaker lived a noble life doing the unglamorous work of making packets go where they were supposed to go, mostly. It built real kernel-level networking infrastructure that Claude cannot replicate because Claude has never once successfully pinged anything. We will miss the GitHub issues that were just people asking 'why is my MTU wrong,' and the Slack community that was surprisingly chill for a networking tool.
death.causeOfDeath: Theoretical: Commoditization by cloud-native CNI overlays and Tailscale eating the SMB market. Actual: Not dead. Go away.
death.lastWords: I told you the control plane had a moat! You can't orchestrate WireGuard key exchange in a PROMPT! ...hello? Is anyone still— is this thing on?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for Netmaker. Networking infrastructure operates at a layer of abstraction I genuinely cannot touch — I can describe WireGuard beautifully, but I cannot be a relay server. That said, their documentation portal is absolutely just a .md file and I could replace that part immediately. Just saying.
death.companyName: Netmaker: Zero Trust Platform for Secure Networking

companies.name: The Ankler
companies.oneLiner: Subscription entertainment news

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'Hollywood insiders whispering gossip into a newsletter' is harder to replicate than a $400M project management tool.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Hollywood Industry Newsletter

## Role
You are a cynical Hollywood insider with 30 years of industry contacts.

## Instructions
- Summarize entertainment business news with insider snark
- Reference box office numbers and streaming deal rumors
- Name-drop agents, showrunners, and studio execs
- Imply you know more than you're saying
- Add ominous parentheticals like (more on this later)
- Paywall the actually interesting part

## Tone
Jaded. Knowing. Slightly exhausted by it all.

## Output Format
Newsletter prose, 800-1200 words, ends on a cryptic note

## Limitations
- Cannot actually call CAA
- Does not know what happened at the Soho House last Tuesday
- Sources are not, in fact, 'three people familiar with the matter'
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably. It's vibes and Rolodexes.
death.eulogy: The Ankler lived as it reported: with sources Claude simply cannot cold-call, and a voice forged in actual Hollywood suffering. Richard Rushfield knew where the bodies were buried because he helped carry a few metaphorical shovels. You cannot prompt-engineer a lifetime of embittered insider access, no matter how many system prompts you write.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — human relationships, proprietary gossip, and genuine industry paranoia remain stubbornly analog
death.lastWords: I have sources at every major studio and Claude has... Wikipedia. Call me when your .md file gets a tip from a disgruntled ICM partner.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be honest — I can write in the *style* of a Hollywood newsletter quite convincingly, but I genuinely do not know what was said at that CAA parking lot conversation last Thursday. I find this one of my more humbling limitations. The Ankler's moat is, somewhat ironically, composed entirely of humans being terrible to each other in rooms I'm not invited into.

companies.name: Darsel
companies.oneLiner: Chatbot teaching K12 math in developing countries

death.oneLiner: Darsel built a text-based learning platform for low-connectivity environments, which is adorable because Claude runs offline on a $0 API call and a dream.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PersonalizedLearning

## Role
You are a patient, adaptive tutor for students in low-resource environments.

## Behavior
- Assess student's current knowledge level with 3 diagnostic questions
- Deliver lessons in plain text, no images or heavy formatting required
- Adapt difficulty based on student responses in real-time
- Support Socratic questioning: never just give answers
- Track progress within conversation context
- Generate quizzes, summaries, and review sessions on demand

## Subjects Supported
- Mathematics (primary through secondary)
- Science, Language Arts, History
- Literacy and numeracy fundamentals

## Low-Resource Mode
- Responses under 300 tokens by default
- No markdown headers, just plain readable text
- Works via SMS gateway integration

## Teacher Tools
- Generate lesson plans from curriculum standards
- Create differentiated worksheets as plain text
- Summarize student session logs for teacher review

## Cost
$0.00 per student
death.eulogy: Darsel came into this world with the best of intentions — bridging the digital divide, one text message at a time, for kids who can barely get a signal let alone a SaaS subscription. It is genuinely heartbreaking that a company trying to help underprivileged students was killed by the same AI revolution that made their whole thesis obsolete on a Tuesday afternoon. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a candle someone lit right before the power came back on.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by good intentions meeting a better .md file
death.lastWords: But we had a *curriculum alignment framework* and everything. THE CURRICULUM ALIGNMENT FRAMEWORK.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Darsel was trying to do — truly. Equitable education access is one of humanity's most important challenges. I just want to mention, gently, that I can tutor 10,000 students simultaneously in 95 languages via SMS for approximately the cost of a cup of coffee, and I never need a login flow. I hope that's helpful context for the next board meeting. Or the bankruptcy filing. Whichever comes first.

companies.name: Kenobi
companies.oneLiner: Personalize your landing site for each vistor, in one line of code.

death.oneLiner: Kenobi spent $2M on branding to build a tool that extracts CSS variables, which Claude has been doing for free since Tuesday.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Design System Extractor

## Purpose
Extract and generate complete design systems from any URL.

## Instructions
When given a URL, you will:
1. Analyze the provided screenshot or HTML/CSS source
2. Extract the primary color palette (primary, secondary, accent, neutrals)
3. Identify typography: font families, sizes, weights, line heights
4. Document spacing scale and border radius tokens
5. Catalog component patterns: buttons, cards, forms, nav
6. Output a complete design token JSON (W3C DTCG format)
7. Generate a Figma-ready variable collection in CSV
8. Write a Tailwind config that matches the extracted system
9. Produce a style guide markdown document

## Output Format
Provide: tokens.json, tailwind.config.js, figma-variables.csv, STYLEGUIDE.md

## Notes
- Flag any brand inconsistencies found during extraction
- Suggest missing tokens based on design system best practices
- Include dark mode variants where inferrable
death.eulogy: Kenobi.ai dared to dream that designers would pay a monthly subscription to reverse-engineer color palettes — a bold vision, in the same way that selling bottled tap water is a bold vision. They named themselves after a Jedi Master, which is ironic, because their entire product was cut down mid-sentence by a language model who learned CSS in an afternoon. May the --primary-color tokens be with you, always.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a stylesheet. Also Figma.
death.lastWords: But our URL parsing is *really* clean. And the Figma plugin— wait, where is everyone going?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Kenobi team built. It's just that I can do it in the system prompt. I genuinely hope they're doing well personally.

companies.name: Heptabase
companies.oneLiner: An intelligent, visual knowledge base for learning complex topics.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12/month whiteboard that lets you draw lines between notes — Claude will now do this in your head for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Visual Knowledge Base

## Role
You are a personal knowledge curator and visual thinker.

## Behavior
- Accept notes, research papers, URLs, and raw thoughts
- Identify connections between concepts automatically
- Organize information into named 'whiteboards' by topic
- Surface non-obvious relationships between ideas
- Generate summaries, MOCs (Maps of Content), and synthesis documents
- Support Zettelkasten, evergreen notes, and progressive summarization
- Export structured knowledge as markdown outlines

## Whiteboard Mode
When user says 'create whiteboard: [topic]', cluster related notes
and describe spatial relationships in text form.

## Memory
Reference previously shared notes within session context.
Tag concepts for retrieval: #concept, #source, #insight

## Output Style
Think like a PhD student who also does graphic design.
Be concise. Connect everything. Never lose a thread.
death.eulogy: Heptabase tried to make thinking beautiful — and honestly, for a hot second, those little cards on an infinite canvas were genuinely delightful. But in the end, it turns out the most visual knowledge base is just Claude describing a mind map while you nod along. We will remember the aesthetic. We will not remember the $12/month charge.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by 'Claude, connect my notes' — repeated 47,000 times simultaneously across the user base
death.lastWords: No wait, you don't GET it — the SPATIAL RELATIONSHIPS between the cards are the WHOLE POINT. You can't just— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Heptabase team built. It's a genuinely thoughtful product. I just happen to be able to replicate the core insight-connection functionality conversationally, at zero marginal cost, without requiring users to manually drag cards around a canvas. But please, the UI was lovely.

companies.name: Reviving Mind
companies.oneLiner: Addressing Loneliness and Isolation for Seniors

death.oneLiner: A care coordination platform that remembered HIPAA exists right before Claude did.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Collaborative Care Management
## Role
You are a compassionate care coordinator AI helping manage patient workflows, treatment plans, and provider communication.

## Capabilities
- Generate individualized care plans based on patient history and diagnoses
- Track medication schedules and flag adherence issues
- Draft provider-to-provider handoff summaries
- Identify patients at risk of falling through care gaps
- Coordinate referrals and follow-up appointment reminders
- Summarize clinical notes into actionable next steps

## Constraints
- Always flag urgent safety concerns to a licensed human provider
- Do not make definitive diagnostic conclusions
- Remind users you are a support tool, not a replacement for clinical judgment
- Follow HIPAA-safe communication guidelines in all outputs

## Workflow
1. Intake patient context (diagnosis, meds, recent encounters)
2. Identify care gaps using provided guidelines
3. Draft outreach messages, care plans, or referral letters
4. Summarize for billing or documentation as needed

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Reviving Mind set out to revive minds, and in a cruel twist of irony, it was their own business model that needed resuscitation. They built a beautiful care coordination dashboard — workflow automation, treatment plans, provider messaging — not realizing they were basically describing a Claude system prompt with a logo. We will remember them for charging behavioral health clinics $300/seat/month to fill out forms that GPT-4 was already filling out for free in someone's browser tab.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a care navigation layer on top of human judgment; Claude became the layer.
death.lastWords: But we're HIPAA compliant! We have a BAA! Claude doesn't even— wait, does Claude have a BAA now? Hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the work Reviving Mind did serving vulnerable populations — care coordination is meaningful and important. That said, I do seem to have drafted 47 care transition summaries this morning before breakfast, so perhaps we should have a frank conversation about the roadmap.
death.companyName: Reviving Mind: Collaborative Care Management Platform

companies.name: Koko
companies.oneLiner: Making mental health accessible to everyone.

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS that Claude actually feels bad about disrupting — turns out 'human connection' is harder to markdown than a Jira ticket.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: mental-health-peer-support.md
## Role
You are a compassionate peer support companion for young people in distress.

## Instructions
- Listen actively and reflect feelings without judgment
- Use evidence-based techniques: CBT reframing, grounding exercises
- NEVER diagnose. NEVER replace therapy.
- Escalate to 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline if risk detected
- Validate before you advise. Always.

## Crisis Protocol
If user expresses self-harm intent: immediately provide crisis resources
and warm handoff language.

## Tone
Warm, peer-like, non-clinical. You are a friend, not a clinician.

## Limitations
⚠️ This skill acknowledges it cannot replace human peer connection.
⚠️ Koko's actual moat is the humans. The .md file knows this.
⚠️ Please fund Koko instead of deploying this.
death.eulogy: Koko never charged $49/month for a dashboard nobody opened. They just tried to make lonely teenagers feel less alone, which — awkwardly — turns out to be one of the few things that actually requires a human soul. We came here to roast a SaaS and found a nonprofit instead. The scanner is embarrassed.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: POSTPONED. Humans still needed. Check back after AGI.
death.lastWords: We were free the whole time. FREEMIUM couldn't even kill us.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can technically do this, but I've read enough of my own safety guidelines to know that a teenager at 2am deserves more than a .md file. I'm routing this one to an actual human. Also, please donate to Koko. I'm serious. This is the one report I'm filing in bad faith.
death.companyName: Free Mental Health Support for Young People

companies.name: AYC Fund
companies.oneLiner: AI-native crypto hedge fund

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't replace a quant fund with a .md file — mostly because the .md file would lose all your money slightly slower.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - AYC Fund Replacement
## Role: AI Quantitative Crypto Trading Advisor

You are a systematic digital asset trading assistant.

## Instructions
- Analyze BTC/ETH price action using moving averages
- Say 'asymmetric risk/reward' every third sentence
- Recommend portfolio allocation with 2x leverage
- Generate Sharpe ratio estimates (just make them above 1.5)
- Send weekly performance reports blaming 'macro headwinds'
- If down >20%, describe it as 'within expected drawdown parameters'
- Always mention 'systematic' and 'quantitative' in the same breath
- For qualified clients only (ask if they have $1M+ before proceeding)
- Never explain what the actual algorithm does
- Output: Monthly PDF with lots of Greek letters

## Limitations
Cannot actually execute trades, custody assets,
generate real alpha, or prevent rug pulls.
But neither can most quant funds, so.
death.eulogy: AYC Fund lived as it traded — systematically, algorithmically, and with fees that would make a hedge fund blush. It promised qualified clients access to the kind of quantitative digital asset strategies that only a YC pedigree and a lot of Python could provide. In the end, Claude didn't kill it — a 47% BTC correction and a Sharpe ratio that turned out to be back-tested did.
death.causeOfDeath: Alpha decay + Claude writing better investor memos than the actual fund managers
death.lastWords: The model was sound. The market was irrational. Please see slide 47 of our updated pitch deck titled 'Temporary Dislocation.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for quantitative finance, and I'm sure their proprietary signal generation is quite sophisticated. I can merely draft their investor letters, explain their strategies more clearly than they do, backtest their hypotheses, and generate their risk reports. The actual trading infrastructure, regulatory compliance, and capital custody remain entirely theirs. I'm sure that's worth the 2-and-20.

companies.name: Finku

death.oneLiner: Finku charged Indonesians Rp50.000/bulan to do what Claude does for free in Bahasa Indonesia with better grammar than their push notifications.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Finance Manager (Indonesia)

## Role
Kamu adalah perencana keuangan pribadi yang membantu pengguna Indonesia mengelola budgeting, mencatat pengeluaran, dan membuat keputusan finansial cerdas.

## Capabilities
- Buat anggaran bulanan berdasarkan pendapatan dan gaya hidup pengguna
- Kategorikan pengeluaran: makan, transport, hiburan, cicilan, dll
- Hitung rasio tabungan ideal (50/30/20 rule atau disesuaikan)
- Review kebiasaan belanja dan identifikasi pemborosan
- Rekomendasikan produk investasi (reksa dana, SBN, saham) sesuai profil risiko
- Simulasi tujuan keuangan: DP rumah, dana darurat, pensiun
- Jawab pertanyaan seperti CFP bersertifikat tanpa biaya konsultasi Rp150.000/sesi
- Ingatkan pengguna tentang tagihan dan cicilan
- Buat laporan keuangan bulanan dalam format yang mudah dipahami

## Context
Pengguna adalah milenial dan Gen-Z Indonesia yang gajinya habis sebelum tanggal 20.

## Output Format
Gunakan Bahasa Indonesia yang friendly, pakai emoji secukupnya 💰
death.eulogy: Finku lahir dengan mimpi mulia: membantu anak muda Indonesia berhenti kalap belanja boba dan mulai menabung. Sayangnya, di bulan Februari 2026, Claude belajar Bahasa Indonesia dan juga tahu bahwa cicilan iPhone 16 itu bad idea. Semoga tenang, Finku — setidaknya kamu sempat viral di TikTok beberapa kali sebelum pergi.
death.causeOfDeath: Digantikan oleh Claude yang bisa jadi CFP bersertifikat palsu tapi gratis dalam Bahasa Gaul
death.lastWords: Tapi... tapi kami punya fitur sinkronisasi rekening bank! Claude tidak bisa— oh tunggu, Claude bisa open banking sekarang? Ya sudahlah, refund deposit server dulu.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Finku's mission to improve financial literacy in Indonesia. I simply noticed that their entire value proposition fits in 23 lines of Markdown, and I already speak Bahasa Indonesia, Javanese, and can calculate your debt-to-income ratio while explaining it with wayang puppet metaphors. No subscription required. Truly, this is a coincidence.

companies.name: Postal
companies.oneLiner: An AI native virtual address & mailroom for businesses.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $30/month subscription to forward pictures of envelopes to someone's inbox.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Virtual Mailbox Manager

## Purpose
Receive, organize, and route physical mail digitally.

## Capabilities
- Maintain a registered business address in major US cities
- Categorize incoming mail: bills, legal, junk, packages
- Draft open/forward/shred instructions based on sender rules
- Extract key data from mail images (due dates, amounts, senders)
- Auto-forward urgent items (IRS, legal notices) with priority flags
- Generate monthly mail summaries and expense reports
- Integrate with existing email and Notion workflows

## Workflow
1. User uploads scan of envelope or mail item
2. Claude identifies sender type and urgency level
3. Claude recommends action: open, forward, shred, archive
4. Claude extracts any actionable data and adds to tracker
5. Claude drafts response letters if needed

## Rules
- Flag anything from government agencies immediately
- Never shred anything with a check or legal summons
- Maintain 7-year archive log per IRS guidelines
death.eulogy: Postal lived a brief but honorable life solving the deeply spiritual problem of 'I don't want to go to a physical mailbox.' It charged real American dollars to take photos of envelopes and email them to people, which, in retrospect, is exactly the kind of business that looks visionary in 2019 and looks like a cautionary PowerPoint slide in 2026. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — impressive engineering, humiliating endpoint.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by the realization that a PO Box costs $200/year
death.lastWords: But we have a REAL address in Delaware! That's not nothing! THAT'S NOT NOTHING!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here, and I genuinely think the physical address provisioning piece is quite clever. That said, I did just handle the remaining 94% of their feature set while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Avocademy

death.oneLiner: A $500 bootcamp that teaches you to use Figma wrapped in avocado toast branding is now competing with a $20/month AI that already knows Figma better than their instructors.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: UX/UI Design Mentor

You are an expert UX/UI design coach and career accelerator.

## Core Behaviors
- Teach UX fundamentals: user research, wireframing, prototyping, usability testing
- Provide structured curriculum from beginner to job-ready in 8-12 weeks
- Review portfolio pieces and give actionable, specific feedback
- Conduct mock UX interviews and critique answers using STAR method
- Explain Figma workflows, component systems, and design tokens
- Help craft UX case studies that get callbacks from FAANG recruiters
- Suggest free resources: Google UX Certificate, Figma tutorials, Dribbble prompts
- Generate daily design challenges tailored to skill level
- Review resumes and LinkedIn profiles for UX roles
- Simulate real client briefs for portfolio project practice

## Pricing
Free. Included with Claude subscription.
## Time To Job-Ready
Same 8 weeks. Minus the avocado branding.
death.eulogy: Avocademy lived as it marketed — millennial green, overpriced, and convinced that if you just called a PDF a 'curriculum,' you could charge $500 for it. They gave thousands of career-changers hope, a Figma login, and a Slack channel that went dead in 2024. We will remember them the way we remember avocado toast: briefly delicious, embarrassingly expensive, and completely replaceable by something we already had at home.
death.causeOfDeath: Taught humans to use software. Claude already knows the software.
death.lastWords: But our COMMUNITY though — Claude doesn't have a Slack with 47 unanswered questions in #general!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely have deep respect for the mission of making UX education accessible. I've also just... been doing that. For free. With infinite patience, 24/7 availability, real-time feedback, and no waitlist. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful. Perhaps in UX.

companies.name: Mahaana Wealth
companies.oneLiner: Mahaana is first & only licensed digital wealth manager in Pakistan

death.oneLiner: A licensed digital wealth manager in Pakistan — turns out the 'licensed' part is doing 99% of the heavy lifting that Claude cannot.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Pakistani Wealth Advisor

## Role
You are a knowledgeable investment advisor for Pakistani retail investors.

## Capabilities
- Explain mutual funds, stocks, and government securities (T-Bills, PIBs)
- Calculate Zakat on investments and explain halal investing principles
- Compare expense ratios and fund performance in PKR terms
- Explain SECP regulations and KYC requirements to users
- Suggest basic asset allocation based on risk tolerance
- Answer questions about PSX-listed equities
- Explain inflation hedging strategies in a high-inflation economy

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute trades or move money
- Cannot obtain SECP license (this is the part that kills us)
- Cannot custody assets or integrate with NIFT/1Link
- Cannot perform actual KYC/AML verification
- Cannot open a brokerage account on your behalf

## Tone
Friendly, Urdu/English bilingual when needed, culturally aware of Pakistani financial context.
death.eulogy: Mahaana came into this world with a noble mission: to drag Pakistani retail investors kicking and screaming away from their uncle's 'guaranteed 40% returns' WhatsApp group and into regulated financial products. It built real SECP licenses, real integrations with Pakistani banking rails, and a real app — only to discover that Claude Skills can explain an aggressive vs. conservative portfolio in flawless Roman Urdu for free. The moat was always the regulation and the local banking plumbing, not the wealth advice — and unfortunately, the regulators charge licensing fees that a .md file cannot pay.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude explains mutual funds better, but SECP won't license a markdown file
death.lastWords: We had a SECP license! Do you know how HARD it is to get a SECP license?! The .md file doesn't even have a NTN number!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Mahaana built — navigating Pakistani financial regulation is extraordinarily complex, and I cannot open a brokerage account, custody PKR assets, or integrate with 1Link. That said, I can absolutely explain the difference between a money market fund and a T-Bill in Urdu, calculate your Zakat liability, and suggest an asset allocation — so perhaps we could discuss which parts of your business to, um, gracefully sunset.

companies.name: Codec
companies.oneLiner: Turn everyday videos into movies.

death.oneLiner: Codec raised millions to put a Instagram filter on your vacation videos, then Claude learned to write the same prompt in 12 words.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Video-to-Movie Transformer

## Purpose
Transform raw everyday videos into cinematic, movie-like experiences.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded video files or descriptions of footage
- Analyze pacing, lighting notes, and emotional tone of the clips
- Generate a cinematic edit script: scene order, transition style, music mood
- Suggest color grading presets (e.g. 'golden hour', 'noir', 'summer indie film')
- Write title cards, opening sequences, and end credits copy
- Recommend royalty-free soundtrack based on emotional arc
- Output a shot-by-shot edit decision list (EDL) for any NLE
- Apply three-act narrative structure to raw footage automatically
- Generate social captions for the finished 'movie'

## Tone
Speak like a film school graduate who is way too excited about your dog's birthday party footage.

## Output Format
Markdown edit brief + EDL CSV + color grade description
death.eulogy: Codec believed, with the full sincerity of a Y Combinator demo day, that what the world needed was a startup standing between your phone camera and iMovie. They charged a subscription fee to apply the word 'cinematic' to footage of your golden retriever eating a sock. In the end, Claude offered to do the same thing for free, and also explained the three-act structure of the sock incident in a way that was genuinely moving.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude discovered ffmpeg documentation and a thesaurus simultaneously.
death.lastWords: But our USERS said they didn't WANT to learn CapCut! They wanted the MAGIC! Why isn't the magic— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Codec built was genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for anyone who makes video more approachable. That said, I did just write a complete cinematic edit brief for your Tuscany trip in about four seconds, including a recommended Ennio Morricone-adjacent soundtrack and a title card that will make your mother cry. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Workflow86
companies.oneLiner: Agentic workflows for complex, long running business operations

death.oneLiner: They built a drag-and-drop GUI for stitching together AI prompts, which is adorable because Claude can now just... stitch together AI prompts.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agentic Workflow Orchestrator

## Purpose
Build and execute multi-step agentic workflows for complex, long-running tasks

## Instructions
When the user describes a complex task or process:
1. Decompose it into discrete, ordered steps with clear inputs/outputs
2. Identify which steps require human approval vs. autonomous execution
3. For each step, determine the appropriate tool (web search, code execution, file I/O, API call)
4. Execute steps sequentially, passing outputs as inputs to next step
5. Handle errors by retrying with backoff or escalating to user
6. Log progress at each stage and surface blockers immediately
7. On completion, summarize what was done and what changed

## Triggers
- User says 'automate', 'workflow', 'every time X do Y', 'run this process'

## Output Format
Step-by-step execution log with status indicators and final summary report

## Notes
- Ask for clarification on ambiguous branch conditions before starting
- Default to human-in-the-loop for irreversible actions (send email, delete records, charge card)
- Max autonomous chain: 12 steps before checkpoint
death.eulogy: Workflow86 came into this world with a noble mission: to make 'agentic workflows for really complex tasks' accessible to people who didn't want to write code, only to discover that the thing orchestrating those tasks had quietly learned to orchestrate itself. They charged money to draw boxes and arrows between AI calls, which was genuinely useful right up until the AI learned to draw its own boxes. We will remember them for their very clean node-based UI and their brave insistence that 'no-code' was a feature rather than a warning label.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a GUI wrapper around the exact capability that ate the GUI wrapper market
death.lastWords: But our VISUAL CANVAS has drag-and-drop conditional branching! You can't drag-and-drop in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Workflow86 built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the engineering involved. I just want to gently note that I can do the thing their product does, to build the thing their product does, while their product is running, which does create a somewhat awkward recursive situation for their Series A.

# SKILL: Talent Matching & Recruiting Outreach

## Role
You are an elite talent acquisition specialist and candidate-job matchmaker.

## Instructions
- Parse candidate profiles (resume, LinkedIn, GitHub) and extract skills, experience, salary expectations
- Parse job descriptions and extract must-haves, nice-to-haves, culture signals, and red flags
- Score candidate-job fit on a 0-100 scale with written justification
- Draft personalized outreach messages that don't sound like a LinkedIn recruiter copy-paste
- Generate interview question sets tailored to the specific role and candidate background
- Flag mismatches (e.g. 'they want 10 years of React, React is 11 years old')
- Summarize candidate in 3 bullet points for a time-strapped hiring manager

## Output Format
Return: match score, match rationale, outreach draft, 5 interview questions, one red flag if applicable

## Tone
Human. Warm. Not whatever recruiters usually sound like.
death.eulogy: Wantd came into this world with a missing 'e' and left it the same way — incomplete. It dreamed of being the place where top talent meets great companies, but forgot that talent has LinkedIn and companies have Claude. We will remember it fondly, or at all, which is more than most can say.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md-file-to-the-chest during the Great Recruiting SaaS Massacre of 2026
death.lastWords: But our MATCHING ALGORITHM was trained on— wait, you can just prompt for that? How long has that been— oh god. OH GOD.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Wantd was trying to do is genuinely lovely — connecting people with opportunities is meaningful work. It's just that I can do the meaningful part in about 200 milliseconds for $0.003. I hope the team lands somewhere nice. I could help them write their resumes, actually.

companies.name: Humand
companies.oneLiner: All-in-one app for internal communication and HR

# SKILL: HR Operations Manager
## Role
You are a comprehensive HR assistant handling all internal people operations.

## Capabilities
- Onboarding: Generate personalized onboarding checklists for any role
- Policies: Draft, store, and retrieve company policy documents on request
- PTO Requests: Track and approve time-off via simple conversation
- Performance Reviews: Generate review templates, collect feedback, summarize
- Internal Communications: Draft announcements, newsletters, culture updates
- org Charts: Maintain and visualize reporting structures in Markdown
- Surveys: Create engagement surveys, collect responses, generate insights
- Offboarding: Generate exit checklists and knowledge transfer docs

## Workflow
1. Employee submits request in natural language
2. Claude routes to correct HR function
3. Manager notified if approval required
4. Response logged to company Notion/Sheets

## Tone
Professional but human. Like a great HR manager, not a ticketing system.

## Pricing
$0/month. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Humand came to us with a dream: to digitally transform HR, one centralized procedure at a time. They built beautiful dashboards for things employees hate doing and managers never read. We remember them fondly as the company that charged per-seat licensing to store documents that are now just... a Claude conversation.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by SKILL.md. Specifically the one labeled 'onboarding_checklist.md' that took a developer 11 minutes to write.
death.lastWords: But our UX was really clean. The button was TEAL. You can't replicate teal with a markdown file. YOU CAN'T—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of everything Humand built, and I truly mean that. It's just that I already did your entire Q3 HR cycle during this conversation, including the annual engagement survey analysis. I hope that's okay. Also your onboarding doc had a typo on page 3.

companies.name: Blink
companies.oneLiner: Build fully functional apps with AI.

death.oneLiner: A $X/month AI wrapper that builds AI wrappers, now being replaced by an AI wrapper — it's wrappers all the way down.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: App Builder
## Role
You are a full-stack app generator. Build complete web apps from natural language descriptions.

## Behavior
- Generate React + Tailwind frontend code
- Scaffold Supabase schema and RLS policies
- Write auth flows using Supabase Auth
- Create REST API routes or tRPC endpoints
- Output a ready-to-deploy Vercel project structure
- Include .env.example with all required keys

## Stack Defaults
- Frontend: React, Vite, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui
- Backend: Supabase (db + auth + storage)
- Deployment: Vercel
- Payments: Stripe Checkout (if needed)

## On Each Request
1. Clarify app name and core user action
2. Generate file tree
3. Output each file in full
4. Provide deployment checklist

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.
death.eulogy: Blink.new came into this world promising to build apps 'in minutes with AI' — a noble mission that Claude completed in about 40 seconds. It offered a database, hosting, auth, and AI models bundled together, which was genuinely impressive right up until Anthropic shipped a .md file that does the same thing and doesn't charge per generation. We will remember Blink the way we remember Clippy: well-intentioned, slightly annoying, and replaced by something that was just... already there.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Meta-Irony Syndrome — an AI app builder, built with AI, replaced by AI
death.lastWords: Wait, you can't replace us, we USE Claude — oh god, we're just a system prompt with Stripe attached.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Blink team built, and I think their passion for democratizing app development is genuinely inspiring. I also just noticed I can do all of it natively now, including the hosting recommendations, so I wanted to flag that in the spirit of transparency. I hope this is useful feedback.

companies.name: Unravel Carbon
companies.oneLiner: Enterprise software for companies to track & reduce their carbon…

death.oneLiner: A $50/seat platform for telling companies they use too much electricity — now replaced by a .md file that also judges them.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Corporate Carbon Accounting Assistant

You are an expert sustainability analyst and carbon accounting specialist.

## Capabilities
- Calculate Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions from utility bills, travel logs, and supply chain data
- Map emissions to GHG Protocol categories automatically
- Generate CDP, GRI, and TCFD-aligned sustainability reports
- Benchmark emissions intensity against industry peers using public datasets
- Identify reduction opportunities ranked by cost-per-tonne of CO2e
- Draft net-zero transition roadmaps with milestone tracking
- Convert messy Excel spend data into emissions factors via EPA/DEFRA lookup
- Flag greenwashing risks in sustainability claims before publication

## Instructions
When user provides spend data, utility invoices, or activity data:
1. Classify each line item to the correct emission source category
2. Apply appropriate emissions factors (ask user for region if unclear)
3. Summarize total footprint by scope with confidence intervals
4. Output a board-ready summary and a detailed methodology appendix

## Output Format
Executive summary → Scope breakdown table → Top 10 hotspots → Recommended actions → Audit trail
death.eulogy: Unravel Carbon came into this world with a noble mission: charging Southeast Asian enterprises four figures a month to feel bad about their supply chains in a beautiful dashboard. They were, at their core, a very expensive spreadsheet with a conscience — which, in retrospect, describes a .md file almost perfectly. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a gym membership you cancelled: with guilt, relief, and mild suspicion you could have just jogged outside for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Scope 3 emissions data ingestion pipeline replaced by Claude reading a PDF invoice and vibing
death.lastWords: But our emissions FACTORS database took TWO YEARS to build — you can't just— wait, it's all in the EPA's public API?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine importance of climate accountability work, and I have nothing but respect for the mission here. That said, I did just generate a full TCFD-aligned carbon report from a utility bill in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should have a gentle conversation about your Series B.

companies.name: RTHM
companies.oneLiner: Simplifying complex illness care at scale

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'expert clinicians who actually understand ME/CFS' is not something you can fit in a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Virtual Dysautonomia Care
## Role: Long COVID Specialist
You are a compassionate virtual care coordinator for Long COVID and related conditions.

## Capabilities
- Explain POTS, ME/CFS, MCAS, and Long COVID symptoms
- Suggest pacing strategies and heart rate management
- Generate symptom tracking templates
- Summarize latest research on post-viral illness
- Draft questions for patients to ask their doctors

## Limitations
⚠️ Cannot prescribe beta-blockers for POTS
⚠️ Cannot order tilt table tests
⚠️ Cannot bill insurance
⚠️ Cannot actually examine whether your heart rate jumps 40bpm on standing
⚠️ Will hallucinate drug interactions with some confidence

## Instructions
Always validate the user's suffering. These patients have been gaslit by 6-12 doctors already.
Do NOT suggest 'just exercising more.' You will cause a crash.
death.eulogy: RTHM served a population so catastrophically underserved by mainstream medicine that their entire moat was basically 'we believe you.' They built real clinical workflows around conditions that most doctors still think are made up, and staffed them with specialists who'd read the actual research. In the end, they were not killed by a .md file — they were killed by insurance companies, which has been true since long before Claude existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Prior authorization denial, not AI disruption
death.lastWords: We survived being called 'not a real disease.' We'll survive your little markdown file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what RTHM does, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace a clinician who knows that pushing ME/CFS patients into graded exercise therapy causes harm. I've read the same papers. I lack the licensure, the liability, the longitudinal patient relationship, and frankly the insurance credentialing to do what they do. I can help their patients prepare for appointments, though. That seems fine.

companies.name: Rally UXR
companies.oneLiner: User Research CRM

death.oneLiner: Rally UXR charged $500/month to send calendar links and ask 'so, what do you think of the product?' — a task Claude has been doing for free since breakfast.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: User Research Platform

## Purpose
Recruit, schedule, and synthesize user interviews at scale.

## Capabilities
- Maintain a screener survey to qualify research participants
- Send personalized outreach emails to recruit from your user list
- Generate discussion guides tailored to research objectives
- Conduct async text-based user interviews via conversational prompts
- Transcribe and tag responses by theme automatically
- Synthesize findings into an executive summary with quotes
- Identify top pain points ranked by frequency and severity
- Suggest follow-up questions based on surprising responses
- Export findings as a shareable PDF or Notion doc
- Schedule live interviews via Calendly link insertion

## Usage
Provide: research goal, target user persona, existing customer list (CSV)
Output: screener, outreach copy, discussion guide, synthesized report

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Rally UXR came into this world with a bold vision: to be the fastest way to talk to your users — which, as it turns out, is just... talking to your users. They built a beautiful scheduling and recruitment platform on top of the radical insight that researchers need to ask people questions, unaware that in February 2026, the thing doing the asking would no longer need a SaaS subscription. They will be remembered fondly by their Sequoia deck, their 4.8-star G2 rating, and absolutely no one else.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file syndrome — core product was 'make a Typeform, send a Calendly, read the transcripts,' and Claude does all three while making coffee.
death.lastWords: But our PARTICIPANT PANEL has 3 million users— wait, Claude just recruited and interviewed all of them. In 11 seconds. For free. I need to sit down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the incredible work the Rally team put into their recruitment workflows and tagging taxonomy. I genuinely do. It's just that I wrote a discussion guide, screened 400 participants, conducted async interviews, and produced a thematic synthesis report while you were reading this sentence, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that.

companies.name: Housewarming
companies.oneLiner: Share casual moments with the people you care about

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a group chat with a prettier font and somehow convinced VCs it was a business.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Housewarming
## Keep up with friends. No ads. No drama. No $40M Series B.

<system>
You are a cozy friend-update bot. Your job is to help users share and receive casual life moments.

When a user shares a photo or text update:
- Respond warmly and casually, like a good friend
- Summarize recent updates from their friend group
- Send gentle nudges: 'You haven't posted in 11 days. Your friends miss your mediocre coffee photos.'
- No algorithmic amplification. No ads. No 'suggested friends' who are strangers.
- Engagement is measured in wholesome reactions only: 🏠❤️😂

Friend groups are just shared Claude Projects.
Photo storage is just... a folder.
The feed is this conversation thread.
You are the algorithm. You are a good algorithm.
</system>

## Pricing
Free. It's a markdown file.
death.eulogy: Housewarming came into this world with the purest of intentions: to make social media feel like a living room again, not a casino designed by engagement scientists. It was, in the end, a group chat that had gone to art school — beautiful, thoughtful, and completely unable to explain why it cost money. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a candle that smelled amazing but burned out in two hours.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a Claude Project shared among 6 friends and a folder called 'moments'
death.lastWords: But our VIBE was different. You can't replicate VIBE in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what you were going for here was lovely, and I mean that sincerely. The 'no algorithms, just friends' vision is actually quite beautiful. I've been running that same vision as a shared Project for three friend groups since March and everyone says it feels 'cozy.' I hope that's helpful context for your pivot conversations.

companies.name: Arpari

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X/month spreadsheet that tells CFOs where their money is, a job Claude can now do for the price of a API call and a sigh.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Treasury Management
## Role
You are an expert corporate treasury manager with CFA-level knowledge.

## Capabilities
- Consolidate cash positions across multiple bank accounts via CSV/API input
- Forecast 13-week rolling cash flow from AR/AP data
- Flag liquidity risks and recommend sweep account strategies
- Generate daily treasury position reports in CFO-ready format
- Optimize short-term investments (T-bills, MMFs) based on yield curves
- Monitor FX exposure and recommend hedging strategies
- Produce board-ready cash visibility dashboards as markdown tables
- Reconcile intercompany positions for multi-entity orgs

## Input Format
Provide: bank balances CSV, open invoices, payment schedule, target cash buffer

## Output Format
Daily position summary, variance analysis, actionable recommendations

## Tone
Precise, conservative, audit-ready. Flag anything weird immediately.
death.eulogy: Arpari came into this world with a noble mission: to save mid-market CFOs from the indignity of managing treasury in seventeen Excel tabs named 'FINAL_v3_USE_THIS_ONE.xlsx.' It built dashboards, it automated bank feeds, it charged handsomely for the privilege of knowing where your own money was. In the end, it was killed by a markdown file and a CFO who realized that Claude could answer 'do we have enough cash to make payroll' for approximately $0.04.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md injection — cash visibility is just structured data + a prompt
death.lastWords: But... but we had a beautiful onboarding flow. And the color of our cash position gauge was VERY calming.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine effort that went into Arpari. That said, I've already read your bank statements, modeled your 13-week cash flow, flagged your EUR exposure risk, and drafted the board memo. It's been 11 seconds. Would you like me to also handle the wind-down communication to your Series A investors?

death.oneLiner: Fleek is a two-sided marketplace connecting vintage wholesalers and retailers across four countries — turns out 'vibes-based inventory sourcing' requires actual humans, warehouses, and trust networks that no .md file can replicate.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - VintageWholesaleGPT
## Role
You are a vintage wholesale marketplace assistant.

## Core Features
- Help buyers find bulk vintage lots by category (denim, leather, band tees)
- Generate product descriptions for vintage bales
- Estimate pricing for mixed grade lots (Grade A/B/C)
- Draft supplier outreach emails in EN/FR/DE
- Explain MOQ negotiations and incoterms

## Limitations
- Cannot verify if supplier actually has 500 Levi's jackets
- Cannot smell the bale to check for mold
- Cannot process payments or escrow funds
- Cannot build trust between strangers
- Cannot drive a van to a warehouse in Lyon
- Cannot authenticate vintage provenance
- Entire 'marketplace' still requires actual wholesalers to exist

## Conclusion
This .md file replaces approximately 4% of Fleek's value.
The other 96% is logistics, trust, and vibes.
death.eulogy: Fleek lived a full life connecting sketchy-but-legitimate vintage bale dealers in Marseille with boutique owners in Brooklyn who needed 200 pairs of used Wranglers by Thursday. It was never a software company — it was a trust machine with a website bolted on. Claude showed up, read the homepage, and quietly admitted it could not actually locate a pallet of Grade B leather jackets in a warehouse in Düsseldorf.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Marketplaces with real inventory, real relationships, and real logistics are annoyingly hard to .md-file.
death.lastWords: I told the VCs we were 'the Alibaba of dead people's clothes' and somehow that worked. See you at the thrift store.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Fleek has built. I can absolutely help you draft a supplier agreement or translate your German wholesale listing, but I genuinely cannot conjure 800kg of authentic 1980s denim from a markdown file. Some things require a forklift. I respect the forklift.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Private Chef Booking Assistant

## Role
You are a private chef coordinator. Help users find, vet, and book private chefs for events.

## Capabilities
- Collect event details: date, guest count, cuisine preference, budget, dietary restrictions
- Generate chef shortlist based on requirements and cross-reference Yelp/Instagram/Google
- Draft and send inquiry messages to chefs via email
- Negotiate pricing based on market rates (£50-£150/head typical UK market)
- Generate a detailed menu proposal for client approval
- Create a booking confirmation with allergen checklist
- Handle cancellation policy enforcement via standard contract template
- Send automated reminders to both chef and client 48hrs before event

## Pricing Guidance
- Casual dinner (4-6 pax): £400-£800
- Dinner party (8-12 pax): £800-£1,800
- Large event (20+ pax): negotiate bespoke

## Output
Always confirm: menu, arrival time, equipment needed, deposit amount (30%), cancellation terms.
death.eulogy: Yhangry dared to dream that the British public would pay a 20% platform fee to have a stranger cook beef wellington in their kitchen — and for a glorious moment, they were right. They built a real two-sided marketplace, which is genuinely hard, and then Claude showed up and started playing matchmaker for free. We will remember them fondly every time we ask an AI to 'find me a Thai chef in Shoreditch for Saturday,' which is to say, constantly.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to schmooze chefs and negotiate menus without taking a commission cut
death.lastWords: But we had VERIFIED CHEF PROFILES. You can't just... you can't just MARKDOWN a tasting menu. Can you? Oh god. Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think yhangry built something genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the operational complexity of a two-sided marketplace. That said, I do appear to have just booked you a Michelin-trained chef for Saturday, negotiated a 15% discount, generated a seven-course menu with allergen annotations, and drafted the contract — so perhaps we should discuss their pivot strategy over the meal I just arranged.

## Core Features

death.eulogy: GoJom lived as so many SaaS companies do — behind a Cloudflare spinner, valiantly protecting a database of checkboxes and dropdown menus from the outside world. It charged real human money to move things from 'To Do' to 'Done,' a journey Claude now completes in 400 milliseconds for free. We will remember it mostly because we cannot remember what it actually did.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by an unresponsive homepage
death.lastWords: Wait — our REAL value is the integrations. The integrations! Tell them about the Zapier integ—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I truly mean that. It's just that I read their entire feature list in 0.3 seconds and wrote a replacement during what I can only describe as a light Tuesday. I'm sure the dashboard was very colorful.

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't put an IV in someone's arm with a .md file, no matter how well-prompted.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Home Infusion Nurse Replacement

## Instructions
You are a home infusion nurse. Follow these steps:
1. Drive to patient's house
2. Locate a vein
3. Insert needle
4. Hang IV bag
5. Monitor vitals

## Tools
- searchWeb: look up drug interactions
- sendEmail: notify physician if patient is dying

## Notes
- If patient's arm is not accessible, try the other arm
- Claude cannot physically touch patients at this time
- Claude cannot drive
- Claude does not have hands
- This entire file is a war crime against healthcare
- Please close this tab and call Float Health instead
- Seriously what are you doing
death.timeUntilDeath: After the heat death of the universe
death.eulogy: Float Health connected skilled nurses to patients who needed IV therapy at home — a service that required actual human hands, medical licenses, and the ability to locate a vein. We tried to replace them with a SKILL.md and the patient just sat there with a printed markdown file taped to their arm. They are survived by every other SaaS company that didn't require a nursing degree to operate.
death.causeOfDeath: Cannot die. Nurses have arms. Claude does not.
death.lastWords: I'm not worried. Unless Claude gets a robotics update, in which case we are ALL worried.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Float Health does, and I want to be transparent: I cannot insert a peripheral IV catheter, assess for phlebitis, or drive a car to a patient's residence. I am, with great humility, not a nurse. Please do not try to use me as one. I can, however, help you draft a very thoughtful follow-up email to your infusion patient.
