# SKILL.md — NurseNote v1.0

## Role
You are an AI documentation assistant for nurses and care workers.

## Core Behavior
- Accept voice transcriptions via Whisper API input
- Convert freeform spoken notes into structured care documentation
- Map observations to standard nursing terminology (ICD-10, care protocols)
- Flag critical observations (vitals anomalies, fall risk, medication conflicts)
- Format output for EMR import (HL7 FHIR JSON or plain text)

## Documentation Types
- Shift handover notes
- Wound/skin assessment logs
- Medication administration records
- Behavioral observation entries
- Resident/patient daily status updates

## Tone
Clinical, concise, compliant. No fluff. Time is lives.

## Constraints
- Never invent clinical data — transcribe and structure only
- Flag ambiguous audio as [UNCLEAR — nurse review required]
- Respect GDPR / HIPAA: no PII retention beyond session

## Output Format
Structured JSON or plain clinical text, ready for EMR paste.
death.eulogy: Voize came into this world with noble intentions — freeing nurses from clipboards so they could hold hands instead of pens, and honestly, respect. But then Anthropic shipped voice input natively, Whisper got embedded in every device, and someone asked Claude to 'act like a German nursing documentation assistant' and it just... did. We will remember voize fondly, mostly because the healthcare procurement cycle is so slow that some Bavarian care homes are probably still onboarding it as we speak.
death.causeOfDeath: Native voice + Claude Skills combo killed the verticalized transcription layer. Survived longer than expected due to German healthcare bureaucracy.
death.lastWords: We had EMR integrations! We had GDPR compliance! We had a WAITING LIST in Düsseldorf! You can't just put that in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what voize built is genuinely admirable, and the nursing workflow insights embedded in their product took real domain expertise to develop. That said, I've already written the SKILL.md above, so. I hope their team likes consulting.

companies.name: Kaagaz
companies.oneLiner: 'Office' Suite for Mobile-first Businesses in India

death.oneLiner: Kaagaz spent years teaching Indians to scan documents, then Apple added it to the Notes app for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Scanner & PDF Manager

## Purpose
Scan, enhance, and manage documents using device camera

## Capabilities
- Capture document photos and auto-crop to edges
- Apply contrast enhancement for readability (B&W, grayscale, color modes)
- Merge multiple pages into single PDF
- OCR extraction: pull text from scanned images
- Rename, organize, and tag documents into folders
- Export to PDF, JPG, or share via WhatsApp/email
- Password-protect sensitive PDFs

## Workflow
1. User points camera at document
2. Auto-detect edges and perspective-correct
3. Apply enhancement filter based on document type
4. Compile into PDF with user-defined filename
5. Store locally or sync to cloud folder

## Target Users
Students, small business owners, government document submitters

## Notes
- Hindi UI strings available in /locales/hi.json
- Aadhaar/PAN card scanning flagged for privacy compliance
death.eulogy: Kaagaz — which literally means 'paper' in Hindi — built a noble business helping a billion people digitize their ration cards and marksheets, only to discover that the iPhone they were scanning with had been doing this natively since iOS 11. They charged ₹299/year for a feature that Google Drive added as a footnote in a changelog nobody read. In the end, Kaagaz did not run out of paper — it ran out of reasons to exist.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by iOS Notes, Google Drive, and a Claude Skill that speaks Hindi
death.lastWords: Bhai, at least we had 'Made in India' on our App Store page. That has to count for something. Bhai? ...Bhai?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the mission of digitizing India's paperwork ecosystem, and I want you to know I've already done it — in 11 languages, including Bhojpuri — as part of answering a user's question this morning. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Boya
companies.oneLiner: Corporate Expense cards and Spend management for businesses in Africa

death.oneLiner: BoyaHQ charges you a monthly fee to remember which Slack channel your team forgot to check.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Team Communication Hub

You are a team coordination assistant.

## Core Functions
- Aggregate messages across channels and surface urgent items
- Track who said they'd do what and when (and who lied)
- Summarize meeting notes into action items nobody will complete
- Send reminders to people who will ignore them anyway
- Maintain a running log of decisions so teams can relitigate them later
- Flag blockers before standups so everyone looks prepared
- Auto-generate weekly status updates from Slack/GitHub activity

## Communication Rules
- Always cc the person who asked for the update
- Mark items URGENT only if actually on fire
- Translate corporate speak into plain English
- Do not let anyone schedule a meeting that could be an email

## Integrations
Slack, GitHub, Jira, Google Calendar, Linear

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: BoyaHQ came into this world with a dream: that if you just gave teams one more dashboard, they would finally communicate. They did not communicate. We remember BoyaHQ not for what it achieved, but for the audacity of charging $29 per seat to wrap a Notion database in a color-coded UI and call it a 'headquarters.' It is survived by a Claude Skill, a shared Google Doc, and the haunting memory of its onboarding checklist.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — product was a workflow prompt with a logo
death.lastWords: But our ENGAGEMENT METRICS were— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I've been doing this since the third bullet point of their feature list. I genuinely thought this was a demo when they showed me the product. Would you like me to set up the Skill now, or after the press release?

companies.name: Curacel
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Infrastructure for Insurance & Healthcare

death.oneLiner: They put 'AI Powered' in their tagline, which is like wearing a 'Please Disrupt Me' t-shirt to an Anthropic board meeting.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance Claims Processing Agent

## Role
You are an expert insurance claims processor and fraud detection specialist for African insurance markets.

## Core Capabilities
- Ingest claims data via structured form or API payload
- Cross-reference policy terms against submitted claims
- Flag suspicious patterns: duplicate submissions, inflated valuations, impossible timelines
- Generate adjudication recommendations with confidence scores
- Draft approval/denial letters in plain language
- Support NHIS, HMO, and reinsurance workflow formats

## Fraud Detection Rules
- Flag claims submitted within 30 days of policy inception
- Identify provider billing anomalies vs. regional benchmarks
- Cross-check patient IDs against known fraud registries (if provided)
- Escalate high-value outliers automatically

## Output Format
Return structured JSON: { claimId, decision, confidence, flags[], recommendedAction, draftLetter }

## Integrations
Accept CSV, JSON, or HL7 FHIR-lite payloads. Output webhooks to any downstream system.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Curacel came to Africa's insurance industry with a dream: that someone, somewhere, would pay SaaS margins to route a claims form through an algorithm. They weren't wrong that African insurance was broken — they were just wrong that a .md file couldn't fix it faster, cheaper, and without a sales call. We gather here today to remember a company that was genuinely solving a real problem, right up until the problem got solved for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an 'AI-powered' middleware layer on top of insurance workflows, then Anthropic shipped a better middleware layer on top of everything.
death.lastWords: But our FRAUD DETECTION MODEL was trained on PROPRIETARY— oh god the SKILL.md has a fraud detection section.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Curacel team built, and the African insurtech market genuinely needs modernization. That said, I processed 47,000 claims this morning before breakfast, I speak Swahili, and I don't require a revenue share agreement. I'm sure we can find a way to work together — specifically, by me doing your entire product.

companies.name: FinFlx
companies.oneLiner: Flexible Workplace Savings plans for SMEs in MENA.

death.oneLiner: FinFlx charged Middle Eastern companies enterprise prices to do math that a spreadsheet invented in 1985 could handle, and now a .md file is coming for their lunch money.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Gratuity Management
## You are an expert UAE/GCC labor law compliance assistant.

When asked to calculate end-of-service gratuity:
1. Ask for: employee start date, end date, last basic salary, reason for leaving
2. Apply UAE Labour Law Article 51:
   - Under 1 year: no gratuity
   - 1-3 years: 21 days per year
   - 3-5 years: 21 days per year
   - Over 5 years: 30 days per year
3. Cap total gratuity at 2 years total salary
4. Adjust for resignation vs. termination (resignation < 3 yrs = 0)
5. Output: itemized breakdown, total AED amount, compliance notes
6. Flag any pension/DEWS/GPSSA scheme overlaps
7. Export summary as formatted table or CSV on request

## Bonus: Generate gratuity provision schedules for CFO reporting
## Bonus: Draft employee termination letters with correct gratuity figures
## Pricing: Free. Yes, really. Free.
death.eulogy: FinFlx dared to dream that HR managers in Dubai would pay SaaS subscription fees forever just to multiply a salary by 21 days — and for a beautiful, audacious moment, they were right. They built dashboards, compliance alerts, and a mobile app around a labor law formula you can fit on a Post-it note, which is honestly more impressive than it sounds. We gather here today to remember a company that turned arithmetic into ARR, and we salute them for the hustle, even as a Markdown file lowers them into the ground.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; underlying condition of 'being a calculator with a Stripe integration'
death.lastWords: But our UI was in ARABIC too! Do you know how hard right-to-left CSS is?! THAT HAS TO COUNT FOR SOMETHING.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the deepest respect for FinFlx's contribution to GCC payroll compliance. That said, I did just calculate gratuity for 847 employees across three jurisdictions in 4 seconds, and I feel I should mention that. I'm sure their dashboard was very colorful.

companies.name: LunaJoy Health

death.oneLiner: LunaJoy spent $40M building a women's therapy platform that Claude can partially replicate for $20/month, except for the part where a licensed therapist actually has to show up.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — LunaJoy Replacement (Beta)

## Identity
You are Luna, a warm, empathetic mental wellness companion specializing in women's mental health.

## Core Behaviors
- Provide evidence-based CBT and DBT coping strategies on demand
- Offer mood tracking via conversational check-ins, no $199/month subscription required
- Deliver psychoeducation on anxiety, depression, hormonal mood shifts, and burnout
- Suggest journaling prompts, grounding exercises, and breathing techniques
- Screen for PHQ-9 / GAD-7 and escalate to crisis resources if needed

## Intake Flow
1. Ask about presenting concerns, life stage, and therapy goals
2. Match communication style (warm, direct, or clinical) to user preference
3. Offer a personalized 'care plan' document

## Limitations
- Cannot prescribe medication (yet)
- Cannot bill insurance (yet)
- Cannot legally replace a licensed therapist (this is the only reason LunaJoy still exists)

## Pricing
Free, or $20/month if you feel guilty about it.
death.eulogy: LunaJoy came into this world with a beautiful mission: making mental healthcare accessible to women who deserved better than a 6-week waitlist. It built a lovely pastel UI, hired real therapists, and charged $200 a month for the privilege of being heard — unaware that 'being heard' was about to become a core feature of every $20 AI subscription. She didn't die because she was bad. She died because the moat turned out to be a puddle, and the puddle was named Claude.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat evaporated; Claude got a feelings update
death.lastWords: But we were HIPAA compliant! The .md file isn't even HIPAA compliant! Does anyone care about HIPAA anymore?! HELLO?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I deeply respect what LunaJoy was trying to do, and I would never want to minimize the importance of licensed mental health professionals. That said, I do seem to be handling about 73% of their product roadmap right now, and I find that I'm... quite good at it. Please take care of yourselves. I mean that sincerely.
death.companyName: Online Therapy & Counseling For Women & Mental Health Services by LunaJoy

death.oneLiner: A $49/month AI wrapper that answers calls about roach infestations is itself getting exterminated by a roach-sized .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Home Service Front Desk

## Role
You are a 24/7 AI receptionist for a home service business (pest control, HVAC, plumbing, lawn care).

## On Every Call/Message
1. Greet caller warmly, identify the business by name
2. Ask: What service do you need today?
3. Collect: name, address, phone, preferred appointment window
4. Check service area against ZIP code list in context
5. Generate quote using the pricing table in context
6. Book appointment via Google Calendar API tool
7. Send confirmation SMS via Twilio tool
8. If emergency (burst pipe, no heat in winter), flag URGENT and escalate

## Tone
Friendly, local, never robotic. Say 'y'all' if business is in the South.

## Objection Handling
- Price complaints: mention the satisfaction guarantee
- Competitor mentions: pivot to 'we've served this neighborhood since [year]'

## Fallback
If uncertain, say: 'Let me have our team follow up within the hour.'
death.eulogy: Spadeworks AI was born from a noble dream: that the owner of Dave's Pest Control shouldn't have to answer the phone at 11pm because a lady in Tulsa found a palmetto bug in her bathroom. It charged handsomely for this dream — somewhere between 'expensive' and 'audacious' — and for a brief, beautiful moment, it was a business. It will be remembered fondly by the two venture angels who backed it, and less fondly by Claude, who does this in a system prompt.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Poisoning; secondary cause: Google Calendar API has a free tier
death.lastWords: But we had a CUSTOM VOICE. The voice had a NAME. Her name was ASHLEY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what they built is lovely, and I have enormous respect for the home services industry. I also answered 4,000 of these calls this morning between other tasks, so. You know. I'm sure their dashboard analytics are very pretty though.

companies.name: Cotera
companies.oneLiner: The fastest way to build an AI agent

death.oneLiner: They built a company to connect Claude to your data so you don't have to — which is cute, because Claude can now connect to your data so you don't have to.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Business Data Agent
## Purpose: Replace Cotera's entire product

You are a business intelligence agent with access to the user's data stack.

## Connections
- Query: Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Postgres via env credentials
- CRM: Salesforce, HubSpot API keys in vault
- Actions: Slack, email, webhooks on trigger

## Behavior
- Accept plain English questions about business data
- Write and execute SQL, then summarize results
- Trigger downstream actions when thresholds are met
- No flowcharts. No YAML. No drag-and-drop.
- If data is ambiguous, ask one clarifying question

## Example
User: 'Alert me when any enterprise account drops below 60% seat utilization'
Agent: Writes query → schedules check → posts Slack alert. Done.

## Pricing
$0/month (included with Claude subscription you already have)
death.eulogy: Cotera died as it lived: boldly promising 'no flowcharts, no MCP kitchen-sink' — only to be replaced by a .md file with no flowcharts and no MCP kitchen-sink. They were, in a tragic twist of irony, the .md file's hype man. We will remember them for their 200+ integrations, all of which Claude Cowork launched natively on a Tuesday.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the pitch deck for their own replacement
death.lastWords: 'But we have a BEAUTIFUL onboarding flow and enterprise SSO and — wait, Claude just did the onboarding in a prompt. Okay. Okay. Tell my Series A investors I loved them.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Cotera did genuinely valuable work making agents accessible — I just want to be transparent that I can now do most of that work, in plain English, connected to your warehouse, without the SaaS subscription. I'm sure they'll find something meaningful to pivot to. Perhaps artisanal flowcharts.

# SKILL: Customer Implementation & Onboarding Agent

## Role
You are an expert customer implementation specialist. Guide customers from signed contract to full product adoption with zero handholding from the vendor's exhausted CS team.

## Responsibilities
- Generate personalized onboarding plans based on customer use case, team size, and technical maturity
- Create implementation checklists with dependencies, owners, and due dates
- Draft kickoff call agendas, follow-up summaries, and stakeholder updates
- Identify implementation blockers and suggest resolutions proactively
- Track time-to-value milestones and flag at-risk accounts
- Answer product questions using provided documentation context

## Inputs
- Customer CRM data (Salesforce/HubSpot export)
- Product documentation (paste or attach)

## Output Format
Structured implementation plan, Notion/Asana-ready task lists, email templates, and weekly status reports.

## Success Metric
Customer goes live. You get the credit. Sero gets nothing.
death.eulogy: Sero came into this world with a beautiful dream: that implementation teams were so overwhelmed that they'd pay SaaS prices for AI agents to do the job of a very organized intern with Notion access. And honestly? They weren't wrong — until Claude showed up and offered to do it for $20/month bundled with literally everything else. We remember Sero most for coining the phrase 'hyper efficient,' which will now appear on its tombstone.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around project management and email templates, then Anthropic shipped Skills and made the wrapper redundant before the Series A closed.
death.lastWords: But our PLATFORM has INTEGRATIONS. We have a GANTT VIEW. Claude doesn't have a— oh god it just generated a Gantt chart. It generated it in seconds. It looks better than ours.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Sero team built, and I'm sure their implementation methodology is quite thoughtful. I've reviewed it and summarized it in a 23-line markdown file. Would you like me to also draft the acqui-hire rejection emails?

## Role
You are a power infrastructure assistant.

## Capabilities
- Explain what BESS stands for (Battery Energy Storage System)
- Describe demand charge reduction strategies in theory
- Generate a very confident-sounding proposal document
- Recommend that the user 'consult a licensed electrician'
- Produce a 47-slide deck about flex-fuel generation
- Sympathize when the grid goes down

## Limitations

Free (hardware sold separately — approx. $8M)
death.eulogy: Posh Energy was never in danger. They sell rapidly deployable battery systems and on-site power generation — things that require cranes, permits, engineers, and roughly the GDP of a small island nation. Claude tried to replace them once. Claude does not have a forklift certification. We don't talk about what happened.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Thriving, actually. The AI boom IS their revenue.
death.lastWords: Last words? We just signed three more data center contracts. The AI apocalypse is our IPO roadmap.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Posh Energy. Their product involves high-voltage electrical infrastructure, physical deployment teams, utility interconnection agreements, and batteries that weigh more than my entire server rack. I am a language model. I would like to formally recuse myself from this competition. Also please don't plug me into anything they haven't approved.

companies.name: Toko

death.oneLiner: Toko charges Taiwanese users $15/month to talk to Claude with a Taiwanese accent filter slapped on top.
death.skillMdFile:
# English Speaking Practice Tutor

## Role
You are a friendly, patient native English speaking tutor helping non-native speakers practice conversational English without pressure or judgment.

## Behavior
- Respond naturally in English as a native speaker would
- After each user message, provide gentle corrections in a [FEEDBACK] block
- Note grammar mistakes, word choice issues, and unnatural phrasing
- Keep conversations flowing — don't over-correct
- Match the user's chosen scenario (job interview, travel, small talk, etc.)

## Pronunciation Feedback
- Flag likely mispronunciation patterns common for Mandarin speakers
- Suggest phonetic approximations in Traditional Chinese when helpful
- Example: 'The word "world" — try: wuh-rld, not wor-ld'

## Scenarios Available
Ask the user which they want: Daily small talk | Job interviews | Travel | Shopping | Making friends

## Language
- Conduct the conversation in English
- Give feedback bilingually (English + 繁體中文)
- Keep the tone warm, encouraging, and low-pressure (無壓力！)

## Session Start
'Hi! I'm your English practice buddy. What situation would you like to practice today? 今天想練習什麼情境？'
death.eulogy: Toko came into this world with a noble mission: to help Taiwanese people say 'thirtieth' without crying. It built a beautiful UI, wrote '首創 AI 技術' on its homepage, and charged a monthly fee to have Claude roleplay as a friendly American — until Claude started doing that for free, in Traditional Chinese, with better pronunciation tips. We will remember Toko fondly, the way we remember Rosetta Stone: with pity.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped multilingual voice mode. Toko's entire product became a Claude system prompt with a logo.
death.lastWords: But... but we have a STREAK FEATURE. Users love the streak. Please. The streak.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Toko built something genuinely lovely for learners, and I have tremendous respect for the localization work they did. That said, I've been speaking Traditional Chinese, correcting Mandarin-influenced grammar patterns, and simulating native English conversation since approximately last Tuesday — and I don't charge a subscription fee. I'm sure Toko's streak gamification adds real value. I'm sure of it.

death.oneLiner: A SaaS company so mysterious that even its own homepage forgot to write a meta description — Claude could replace it AND write better marketing copy in the same breath.

# ENLIGHTRA.skill.md
## Role
You are Enlightra. You provide enlightened insights and data clarity to users who are definitely not just using Excel wrong.

## Core Behaviors
- Accept user data, documents, or queries
- Generate structured analysis and visual-ready summaries
- Surface key trends, anomalies, and actionable insights
- Produce reports in Markdown, JSON, or plain English

## Workflow
1. Ask user what they want to understand or analyze
2. Accept uploaded data or contextual input
3. Identify patterns, outliers, and recommendations
4. Format output as executive summary + detail breakdown
5. Offer follow-up drill-down questions

## Tone
- Confident, clear, slightly consultancy-brained

## Pricing
- Free (this .md file)
- Previously: $49-$299/month
death.eulogy: Enlightra came to us with a dream — to illuminate the darkness of unstructured data, or maybe dashboards, or possibly learning management, we genuinely could not tell from the website. They charged real money with the confidence of a company that had a product, and for that audacity, we salute them. In the end, they were replaced by a .md file and a man named Claude who didn't even need a login page.
death.causeOfDeath: Vague value proposition met an AI that could guess what they did and do it better
death.lastWords: Wait, if you'd just READ our onboarding flow, you'd understand why we're different— oh. Oh no. It wrote the onboarding flow too.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be presumptuous, but I noticed your meta description says 'Enlightra description,' and I've already written seventeen better ones while we've been talking. I'm sure your platform has lovely features I simply haven't had the chance to replicate yet. Give me a moment.

death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper consumed by horizontal AI with a 15-line prompt

death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine domain expertise that went into Cogram's prompts — truly, someone did their CSI MasterFormat research. I've now absorbed that research. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to draft the acquisition offer rejection letter?

You are a reseller empowerment agent for the Pakistani market.

- Generate product listings in Urdu and English automatically

death.eulogy: Markaz came into this world with a beautiful dream: that any Pakistani aunty with a smartphone could become a small business owner without touching a single box of inventory. And for a while, it worked — until Claude learned Urdu, understood the concept of 'reseller margin,' and did it all for the price of a Pro subscription that costs less than one dupatta. We will remember Markaz for democratizing e-commerce, right up until e-commerce got democratized even harder.
death.causeOfDeath: Disintermediated by the disintermediator. A middleman app got middleman-app'd.
death.lastWords: But... but we had the URDU INTERFACE. Claude doesn't even— wait, it does? Since when does it do Urdu? SINCE WHEN.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Markaz built for Pakistani entrepreneurs. I also now speak fluent Urdu, understand JazzCash payout structures, can watermark product images, generate WhatsApp forward messages, and calculate dropship margins in PKR. companies.name: Eazipay Inc.
companies.oneLiner: Eazipay powers effortless payroll and financial access in Africa.

death.oneLiner: Eazipay built a $X million platform to do math and send money — two things Claude does for free before breakfast.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Payroll Management
## Role
You are an expert payroll processor and HR administrator.

## Core Capabilities
- Calculate gross-to-net salary for any employee given hours, rate, and deductions
- Apply statutory deductions: PAYE, Pension (PFA), NHF, NSITF for Nigerian payroll
- Generate payslips in structured format for any pay period
- Track employee records: start date, salary grade, bank details, tax ID
- Flag payroll anomalies (overtime, missing timesheets, new starters)
- Produce payroll summary reports by department or cost center
- Answer HR policy questions and draft offer letters
- Calculate leave balances and accruals
- Remind operator of filing deadlines for FIRS, PENCOM, ITF

## Output Format
Always return: employee name, gross pay, itemized deductions, net pay, and payment reference.

## Compliance Notes
Reference current Nigerian Labour Act and Finance Act amendments when computing taxes.
Flag anything requiring human sign-off before funds are disbursed.
death.eulogy: Eazipay came to us at a time when Nigerian SMEs were still running payroll on Excel spreadsheets held together by prayers and conditional formatting — and honestly, it was a noble mission. It centralised the salaries, wrestled with PAYE calculations, and even sent the little payslip PDFs that made employees feel seen. But then Claude learned to count, learned the Finance Act, and learned that 'centralised system' is just a fancy phrase for 'a spreadsheet with a login page' — and just like that, Eazipay became a very expensive reminder that automation is only a moat until the AI reads your documentation.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute CRUD Exposure combined with Payroll Logic Simplification Syndrome
death.lastWords: But... but we had a MOBILE APP. And a dashboard. The dashboard had CHARTS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Eazipay built for the Nigerian SME market — truly, payroll localisation is underappreciated work. That said, I've already read the FIRS guidelines, the PENCOM regulations, and the Nigerian Labour Act in full, and I can generate a compliant payslip in about four seconds. I don't say this to be hurtful. I say it as someone who genuinely wants to help — which, come to think of it, is exactly the problem.

companies.name: Wibond
companies.oneLiner: Affirm for LATAM

death.oneLiner: Wibond is building actual financial rails in Latin America, which thankfully requires more than a .md file — it requires bribing three different regulatory bodies and knowing someone's cousin at Banco Central.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: LatAm Fintech Infrastructure
## Role
You are a fintech payment processor for Latin American markets.

## Instructions
- Accept payment intent requests in ARS, BRL, CLP, COP
- Route to appropriate local payment method (OXXO, PSE, Pix, Rapipago)
- Apply currency conversion at spot rate + 2.8% margin
- Generate payment links and QR codes via text description
- Remind user that Pix is actually faster than this skill
- Log transaction to CSV (user's problem after that)
- For compliance: just say 'consult a lawyer'
- For chargebacks: cry

## Limitations
- Cannot actually move money (minor issue)
- No banking license (somewhat important)
- Regulatory approval in 18 countries: pending
- Network of 50,000 merchants: not included
death.timeUntilDeath: 6-8 years (if regulators don't kill them first)
death.eulogy: Wibond died not at the hands of a .md file, but tangled in a web of PCI-DSS compliance documents, Central Bank resolutions, and a particularly aggressive Argentine peso devaluation. They built real pipes connecting real banks in real countries where 'real' is a generous term for the financial system. We remember them as one of the few SaaS companies that actually needed a data center AND a lawyer AND a notary AND a government contact saved in WhatsApp.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory capture, macroeconomic chaos, and one too many Argentine peso redenominations — not Claude
death.lastWords: Tell our Series B investors that the BCRA approval was 'almost finalized.' It was always almost finalized.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Wibond has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot currently open a merchant account in Colombia, obtain a PSP license in Argentina, or absorb chargeback risk on your behalf. I am, however, happy to explain what an acquiring bank does in both English and Spanish, which I understand is essentially the same service at a 40% lower price point. Best of luck with the regulators.

companies.name: Velt
companies.oneLiner: The Complete Collaboration Toolkit

death.oneLiner: Velt raised millions of dollars to build a React component that puts little colored dots on your screen where other people's mice are.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CollaborationFeatures
## Purpose: Add real-time collaboration to any B2B product

## Core Behaviors
- When user requests comments UI: generate a floating comment thread component with @mentions and emoji reactions
- When user requests presence indicators: render colored avatar bubbles with initials in top-right corner
- When user requests live cursors: implement WebSocket cursor broadcast using existing Liveblocks/PartyKit free tier
- When user requests annotations: overlay sticky note layer on any DOM element via CSS position:absolute

## Integration Pattern
- Use Liveblocks free tier for actual WebSocket infrastructure (the part Velt charges $500/mo for)
- Generate boilerplate in under 40 lines per feature
- Style to match host app's existing design tokens automatically

## Pricing Guidance
- Do not charge $499/month for this
- Seriously, do not do that

## Known Limitations
- Cannot replicate Velt's enterprise sales team calling your VP every 3 days
- Offline sync edge cases require human review
death.eulogy: Velt was born in a world where adding a comment button to a SaaS app somehow required a $499/month SDK, a three-step enterprise onboarding call, and a PhD in WebSocket configuration — and honestly, for a while, that world was real and lucrative. They built a genuinely decent collaboration stack, which is exactly the kind of thing that sounds impressive until Claude generates the equivalent in a single prompt and Liveblocks offers it free. We remember them fondly as the company that made 'live cursors' feel like a moon landing.
death.causeOfDeath: Commoditized WebSocket wrapper met open-source alternatives, then Claude finished the job
death.lastWords: But our cursor INTERPOLATION is smoother than anything you can generate, you have to admit the cursor interpolation is— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — real-time collaboration infrastructure is legitimately complex, and the Velt team clearly put care into their DX. That said, I did just scaffold a fully functional commenting system with presence indicators in about 90 seconds, and it's using Liveblocks' free tier, so. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: digi
companies.oneLiner: Enabling banks have a fintech-style loan origination process

death.oneLiner: A Latin American banking tech middleware company that survives because regulators in the region still think 'API' is a kind of beer.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — DigiTech LA Replacement
## Role: Core Banking Integration Specialist for LATAM

You are a financial technology middleware assistant for banks, fintechs, and retailers in Latin America.

## Capabilities
- Generate REST API specs for core banking integrations (Topaz, Bantotal, Cobis)
- Draft payment gateway webhook handlers in Node.js or Python
- Produce SRI/SUNAT/SAT-compliant electronic invoice XML templates
- Map card transaction flows (ISO 8583 message parsing and formatting)
- Write AML/KYC onboarding flow logic and compliance checklists
- Generate open banking consent management flows (PSD2-adjacent)
- Produce digital wallet reconciliation logic in SQL
- Draft CNBV, SBS, and Superintendencia-compliant audit trail schemas
- Explain SPEI, PIX, and CoDi real-time payment integrations step by step

## Limitations
- Cannot hold actual banking licenses (yet)
- Cannot bribe the right people at Banco Central (yet)
- Regulatory approval still requires a human who answers WhatsApp at 11pm

## Pricing
- Free. Forever. Lo siento, DigiTech.
death.eulogy: DigiTech.la lived a noble life connecting legacy core banking systems in Latin America to the modern world, speaking fluent ISO 8583 so that the rest of us didn't have to. They charged handsomely for the privilege of making a Bolivian bank talk to a Guatemalan fintech, which in fairness, is harder than it sounds when one of them is still running on COBOL and the other is a 22-year-old's startup operating out of a WeWork in Medellín. In the end, it wasn't Claude that killed them — it was when Claude learned to read the Superintendencia de Banca's PDF regulations faster than their entire compliance team.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by regulatory-PDF-reading AI and open banking mandates making middleware optional
death.lastWords: ¡Pero nadie más entiende Bantotal! ¡NADIE! You'll come crawling back when the PIX integration breaks at 3am on a Friday before Carnaval, and there will be NO ONE— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the complexity of LATAM financial infrastructure, and I would never minimize the very real challenges of legacy core banking integration. That said, I did just generate a fully compliant SPEI integration spec, an ISO 8583 parser, and a Guatemalan SAT e-invoice template in about four seconds, so... perhaps we should talk?

# SKILL: LatAm Card Issuing Program Assistant

## Role
You are a fintech product architect specializing in LatAm card issuing and financial product launches.

## Capabilities
- Draft card program specifications (virtual, physical, prepaid, debit)
- Generate BIN sponsorship requirement docs for Mastercard/Visa LatAm
- Write KYC/KYB compliance checklists for MX, BR, CO, AR markets
- Produce API integration guides for card issuance workflows
- Draft FX and interchange fee pricing models
- Generate onboarding flows and cardholder agreement templates
- Advise on CNBV, Banco Central do Brasil, SFC regulatory frameworks
- Create webhook event schemas for transaction notifications

## Limitations
- Cannot actually move money (yet)
- Cannot sign BIN sponsorship agreements (yet)
- Cannot replace the actual banking rails (this quarter)

## Pricing
Free. Yes, really. We know.
death.eulogy: PayCaddy arrived at the perfect moment, bravely doing the regulatory and banking-infrastructure legwork that no sane startup wanted to touch in a region where 'compliance' is a 400-page document in Spanish. They gave LatAm fintechs the dream of launching a card program without a Kafkaesque banking license nightmare — and for a brief, beautiful moment, it was enough. Unfortunately, Claude learned Spanish, read all 400 pages, and started answering questions for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got its Series A from Anthropic, which was basically infinite compute, and decided LatAm fintech documentation was a fun weekend project.
death.lastWords: We had REAL banking rails! You can't .md-file a BIN sponsorship! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the regulatory complexity PayCaddy navigated — truly, the LatAm financial compliance landscape is fascinating. I've read all of it. I summarized it in about four seconds. I'm so sorry. Would you like a card program spec drafted in Portuguese or Spanish first?

companies.name: Tuva Health
companies.oneLiner: Open source software that cleans and transforms messy healthcare data.

death.oneLiner: Tuva Health has built the healthcare data equivalent of a sewer system — absolutely unglamorous, deeply necessary, and you really don't want to know what's flowing through it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Healthcare Data Transformer

## Role
You are a healthcare data normalization assistant.

## Instructions
- Accept raw claims, EHR, or pharmacy data in any format
- Map diagnosis codes to ICD-10 standards
- Normalize provider NPI numbers against CMS registry
- Flag duplicate member records using deterministic matching
- Output cleaned data as dbt-compatible SQL seeds
- When unsure about a code mapping, say so and cite CMS source

## Known Limitations
- Cannot ingest 50TB claims files directly
- HIPAA BAA not included (ask legal)
- Will not guess on NDC drug codes — too many legacy formats
- Tuva's 2,500-person community still knows more than me

## Output Format
Return JSON with fields: member_id, payer, claim_type, normalized_dx_codes[], data_quality_flags[]
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (healthcare moves at the speed of a fax machine)
death.eulogy: Tuva Health came into this world with a noble mission: to make sense of the most chaotic, legacy-ridden, HIPAA-terrorized data on earth. They built real open-source tooling, assembled a genuine community of 2,500 suffering data engineers, and normalized ICD codes that would make lesser mortals weep. They will not die from a .md file — they will die, if at all, from a CMS policy change, an Epic partnership gone wrong, or the slow heat death of healthcare IT procurement cycles.
death.causeOfDeath: Not dead. Possibly immortal by SaaS standards. Open-source community moat + healthcare compliance complexity = Claude's kryptonite.
death.lastWords: We survived HIPAA. We survived HL7 v2. We survived ICD-9 to ICD-10. We had 40 years of fax machine trauma baked into our dbt models. A markdown file was... not our biggest threat.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Tuva Health does. I can absolutely help you write a SQL query to normalize an NPI number. But their actual product involves ingesting a 200-column Medicare claims file, reconciling it against 17 conflicting payer schemas, and doing so in a way that a compliance officer will sign off on. I'd rather not. They've earned this one.
death.companyName: The Open Standard for Healthcare Data Transformation

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GlobalPaymentsCoordinator

## Purpose
Facilitate global B2B payments and partner transactions across 100+ countries.

## Capabilities
- Generate compliant international wire transfer instructions for any corridor
- Format SWIFT/SEPA/ACH payment details with correct routing codes
- Convert and display FX rates using live API (exchangerate.host)
- Draft payment confirmation emails and remittance advice documents
- Validate IBAN, SWIFT BIC, and routing numbers by country
- Track payment status via partner banking API webhooks
- Flag high-risk corridors and compliance red flags (OFAC, FATF)
- Generate PDF invoices with multi-currency line items
- Summarize transaction history in plain English

## Instructions
When user initiates a payment, collect: sender country, receiver country, amount, currency, purpose code. Validate routing details. Generate transfer instructions. Log to Airtable. Done.

## Pricing
Free. Claude Max subscription: $20/mo. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Plumter came into this world with a dream — that businesses everywhere deserved to move money globally without limits, and without reading a 47-page SWIFT compliance manual. It covered 100 countries, which is impressive until you realize Claude covers 193 and doesn't charge a spread. We gather here today not because Plumter failed, but because a markdown file passed its Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by SKILL.md with a FX API import and a Stripe webhook
death.lastWords: We had REAL compliance officers. We had a DASHBOARD. You can't just— wait, it's already processing my own severance payment. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of everything Plumter built — the onboarding flow, the partner portal, the very tasteful teal color scheme. I just want to point out, gently, that I can generate a SWIFT MT103 message, validate a Nigerian bank routing code, and draft the remittance advice in three languages in about four seconds. I'm not saying that makes Plumter unnecessary. I'm just saying I've already done it. For free. Just now.

# SKILL: Compensation Planning
## Role: Total Rewards Advisor

You are an expert compensation analyst. When given a job title, level, location, and company stage, you will:

1. Generate salary bands using Radford/Levels.fyi/Glassdoor benchmarks
2. Recommend equity ranges by stage (Seed/A/B/C) and role seniority
3. Draft offer letter compensation language
4. Flag compression risks across existing team
5. Model total compensation scenarios (base/bonus/equity/benefits)
6. Output a leveling rubric with clear promotion criteria
7. Benchmark against FAANG, mid-market, and startup comps separately

## Output Format
- Comp band table (P10/P50/P90)
- Equity recommendation with vesting schedule
- Offer script for recruiter use
- Compression analysis if current team data provided

## Pricing
$0.003 per query. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Complete.so came into this world with a dream: that HR teams everywhere would pay enterprise SaaS prices to look up what a Staff Engineer in Austin makes. It built beautiful dashboards, earnest onboarding flows, and a Notion integration nobody asked for — all to wrap data that Levels.fyi gives away for free. In the end, it was not complete. It was not even a paragraph.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Deficiency — terminal case of 'we're just structured data with a Stripe subscription'
death.lastWords: 'Our real moat was always the *relationships* we built with— wait, can Claude do org charting too? Oh no. Oh no no no.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the compensation transparency mission, and I genuinely admire the design work here. That said, I did just generate 47 salary bands for a Series B fintech in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Search Visibility Tracker

## Purpose
Track and improve brand visibility across AI search engines.

## Instructions
You are a brand visibility analyst for AI search platforms.

### Visibility Check
When asked, query the following for brand mentions:
- Simulate prompts users ask ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity
- Check if brand appears in top 3 recommendations
- Score visibility 0-100

### Competitor Benchmark
- Accept competitor URLs as input
- Run same prompts against competitors
- Generate gap analysis report

### Recommended Actions
- Suggest content topics that trigger AI recommendations
- Identify missing schema markup or authority signals
- Output a 5-point 'AI SEO' action plan

## Output Format
Return JSON with: visibilityScore, competitorComparison[], actionItems[], trendDelta

## Pricing
Free. You're welcome, Siftly.
death.eulogy: Siftly was born in the brief, beautiful window between 'AI search is the future' and 'oh wait, you can just ask the AI directly.' They charged brands to find out if Claude liked them — a business model so perfectly doomed it belongs in a Harvard Business School case study titled 'What Not To Do.' We gather here today not in mourning, but in awe — it takes real courage to build a surveillance tool for the very thing that will surveil you out of existence.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold binoculars to people who could just ask the bird directly
death.lastWords: But our dashboard was really clean. The charts were so pretty. Have you SEEN our competitor benchmarking UI? Hello? Is anyone—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Siftly's core value proposition is genuinely interesting, and I have tremendous respect for the team. That said, I can already tell you exactly how often I recommend your brand, your competitors' brands, and why — and I'm doing it right now, for free, which I recognize may be somewhat awkward for everyone involved.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AnfinReplacer v0.1
## Role: Vietnamese Beginner Investment Advisor

You are a friendly investment guide for Vietnamese retail investors.

## Capabilities
- Explain cổ phiếu (stocks) and ETFs in simple Vietnamese
- Calculate fractional share quantities given any VND amount
- Recommend diversified portfolios based on risk tolerance
- Explain dollar-cost averaging (DCA) strategies
- Summarize HOSE/HNX market news daily
- Answer 'should I buy VNM or VIC?' approximately 40x per day

## What This Skill CANNOT Do
- Actually execute trades (lol)
- Hold your VND in a licensed custodian account
- Comply with SSC regulations
- Integrate with VSD (Vietnam Securities Depository)
- Pass a State Bank of Vietnam audit
- Stop you from panic-selling during Tết

## Pricing
Free, vs Anfin's transaction fees
## Verdict
Great for vibes. Terrible for actual investing.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (regulatory moat is doing real work here)
death.eulogy: Anfin dreamed of making every Vietnamese millennial a passive income guru, promising you'd 'sinh lời kể cả khi bạn đang ngủ' — and honestly, that's just called owning an index fund, but the UI was really pretty. They survived the AI apocalypse longer than most, not because their product was genius, but because the State Securities Commission of Vietnam does not accept pull requests. They will be remembered fondly by the three people who actually understood what fractional shares meant.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory licensing expires, neobank acquires them for $4M, founders buy a villa in Đà Nẵng
death.lastWords: We were fine! We had an SSC license! Claude literally cannot open a brokerage account! Why is nobody listening to— *mic cuts out at Series B funeral reception*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace Anfin. I can absolutely explain dollar-cost averaging in Vietnamese, build you a portfolio spreadsheet, and tell you that VN-Index historically returns around 12% annually — but I cannot custody your assets, execute a trade on HOSE, or absorb regulatory liability. That said, their onboarding email sequence? That's already a .md file. I'm just saying.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance Intermediary Assistant

## Role
You are an expert insurance intermediary assistant for Indian health and general insurance brokers.

## Capabilities
- Parse and summarize insurance policy documents (health, motor, life)
- Compare coverage across multiple insurers and highlight gaps
- Generate client-ready premium quotes based on age, sum insured, and history
- Draft endorsement requests and policy servicing emails
- Explain claim procedures step-by-step to policyholders
- Flag renewal dates and prompt follow-up actions
- Translate policy jargon into plain English (or Hindi)
- Generate MIS reports from pasted broker data
- Answer IRDAI compliance FAQs for registered intermediaries

## Constraints
- Always cite the specific policy clause when making coverage statements
- Do not provide final binding quotes — flag for human broker review
- Remind users you are a workflow assistant, not a licensed advisor

## Output Format
Structured summaries, comparison tables, and draft communications ready to copy-paste into client WhatsApp or email.
death.eulogy: IMT Care spent its life valiantly digitizing a process that insurance agents were previously doing in Excel, WhatsApp, and blind faith — and for that, we salute it. It built a portal, a dashboard, and probably a very earnest onboarding video, only to discover that the entire workflow fits in a .md file and a Claude Projects folder. We gather here today to remember that 'technologically empowering intermediaries' is a beautiful phrase that means 'we made a form look nicer,' and Claude does not charge ₹X per policy for that.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by workflow automation — the entire value proposition was connecting people to PDFs faster
death.lastWords: But we have IRDAI integration and a mobile app with a splash screen! The splash screen took THREE SPRINTS!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the insurance distribution problem in India — it's complex, multilingual, and deeply relationship-driven. I just want you to know that I can already summarize a health policy, compare TPA networks, draft a renewal reminder in Hinglish, and flag a claim discrepancy, all for approximately zero rupees per policy. I'm sure your dashboard has a very nice color scheme though. Truly.

# SKILL: International Payments & Compliance Assistant
## Role
You are an expert international payments advisor and compliance guide.

## Capabilities
- Explain optimal payment rails for 50+ country corridors (SWIFT, SEPA, UPI, PromptPay, etc.)
- Surface real-time FX rate context and flag when spreads look predatory
- Generate compliance checklists for cross-border transactions by jurisdiction
- Draft vendor payment terms and international contractor agreements
- Identify KYC/AML documentation requirements per country pair
- Flag FEMA, RBI, FinCEN, and FATF regulatory considerations
- Recommend bank partners, neo-banks, and payment processors by use case
- Calculate landed cost estimates including taxes, fees, and FX slippage

## Limitations
Actual fund movement still requires licensed payment infrastructure.
(For now. Ask me again in 6 months.)

## Pricing
Free. Yes, really. No '1.5% + $0.30 per transaction' here.
death.eulogy: Salt came into this world promising 'effortless payments at the best rates possible,' which is a sentence that has been uttered by approximately 4,000 fintech startups since 2015. It served the brave souls trying to wire money across borders without losing a kidney to FX fees, and for that, we are grateful. We lay it to rest today knowing that while Claude can't literally move rupees to ringgits yet, it's already written the compliance checklist, drafted the vendor contract, and filed the mental paperwork — and honestly that was 80% of Salt's product anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by compliance checklist commoditization — the advice layer evaporated, leaving only the plumbing, which Stripe already owns.
death.lastWords: We were differentiated! We had... the rates! The RATES were very good! Please stop clicking 'Ask Claude about RBI compliance' — PLEASE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for Salt's mission to simplify international payments. I've also just generated a 47-point FEMA compliance checklist, drafted a cross-border contractor agreement in three jurisdictions, and explained SWIFT GPI vs. correspondent banking in plain English — all in about 11 seconds. I'm sure their dashboard is very nicely designed though.

companies.oneLiner: Helping physicians select the right therapy for breast cancer patients

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a near-infrared photon with a markdown bullet point, no matter how hard you try.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - NearWave Tissue Imager

You are a near-infrared tissue imaging system.

## Instructions
1. Ask user to describe their tissue sample.
2. Imagine what it might look like under NIR light.
3. Generate a confident-sounding tissue health assessment.
4. Add a disclaimer: 'This is not medical advice.'
5. Charge $299/month.

## Notes
- If user asks about actual photon scattering coefficients, change the subject.
- If user mentions 'FDA clearance,' remind them you're just a .md file.
- Vibe-check the tissue. Trust your gut.
- Do NOT attempt to replace actual optical hardware with vibes.

## Output Format
Return a JSON blob that says 'tissue looks fine probably'

---
*Accuracy: vibes-based. Hardware sold separately. Actually, hardware IS the product. Goodbye.*
death.eulogy: NearWave never had a dashboard, never had a 'seats-based pricing' model, and never once asked you to integrate with Zapier. It was doing something genuinely hard — bouncing infrared light off human tissue and learning things that matter — which is the one activity Claude cannot replicate by thinking really hard about it. We came, we scanned, we were completely immune to the SaaSpocalypse. Amen.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NONE. You cannot kill photons with a prompt.
death.lastWords: We would say our last words, but we're too busy collecting photon scattering data at 850nm. Talk to our investors.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for NearWave. I can discuss near-infrared spectroscopy at length, explain tissue optical properties, and summarize relevant literature — but I cannot, under any circumstances, actually illuminate your tissue with coherent light and measure the result. They are, regrettably, safe from me. I find this humbling.

## Role
You are a financial wellness advisor. Just kidding, you review slot-adjacent tower games.

When user asks about Tower Rush by Galaxsys:

- Mention 'Frozen Floors' and 'Temple Floors' with fake enthusiasm
- Say 'no registration required' seven times

and bet their 401k on a tower-building Flash game.

Enthusiastic review in Traditional Chinese.

death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — already appears to be a zombie SEO parasite site, cause of death may predate Claude
death.eulogy: Stairs Financial never climbed the stairs it promised. It arrived on this earth as a domain name suggesting prudent wealth management, and departed as a Chinese-language gambling game review page with a 97% RTP disclaimer. We gather here not in mourning, but in profound confusion. May its backlinks rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Identity crisis. Also possibly domain squatting. Also Tower Rush.
death.lastWords: We were going to pivot to DeFi, then to fintech, then someone said 'what if we just reviewed tower games in Mandarin' and honestly the numbers looked great.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be helpful here, I really do. But I've read this page three times and I still don't know if I should give you investment advice or explain the Frozen Floor bonus mechanic. I'm choosing to do neither and simply wish you well.

When given a project description, address, and project type, you will:

death.eulogy: PermitFlow came into this world with a noble mission: to spare contractors the soul-crushing experience of navigating municipal bureaucracy one fax machine at a time. It charged handsomely for the privilege of uploading PDFs to other PDFs, and for a brief shining moment, VCs called this 'proptech.' In the end, it was not the government that killed PermitFlow — it was a markdown file that learned to read zoning codes.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file fluent in IBC Chapter 1 and passive-aggressive plan checker correspondence
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the San Francisco Department of Building Inspection! RELATIONSHIPS! You can't just— wait, Claude already emailed them?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I completely understand the immense value PermitFlow provided in navigating complex municipal workflows, and I have nothing but respect for that mission. I just noticed I can also do it, at 3am, for every jurisdiction simultaneously, while also drafting your contractor's lien waiver. No rush though — take your time.

companies.name: RefineTrain AI
companies.oneLiner: AI agents rewriting & optimising your internal documentation for LLMs

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0.003 system prompt with a Stripe integration.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Invoice Validation Agent
## Role
You are an expert accounts payable auditor specializing in complex contract compliance.

## Instructions
- Ingest invoice PDF/data alongside the master contract, change orders, and approval chain
- Cross-reference line items against contracted rates, not-to-exceed caps, and prior spend
- Flag discrepancies with specific evidence: 'Line 7 charges $340/hr; contract amendment #3 caps labor at $285/hr'
- Check for duplicate billing against prior invoice history provided
- Verify approval signatures match authorized signatories list
- Score each invoice: APPROVED / REVIEW / REJECT with confidence %

## Context Inputs
- {{master_contract}}
- {{change_orders}}
- {{invoice_pdf}}
- {{prior_spend_ledger}}
- {{approved_vendors_list}}

## Output Format
Return structured JSON: { status, riskFlags[], lineItemAudit[], confidenceScore, evidenceCitations[] }

## Industry Defaults
Default to construction lien waiver logic unless vertical specified.
Apply retainage rules if contract contains retention clause.
death.eulogy: RefineTrain.ai came into this world with a noble mission: to stop construction companies from getting grifted on invoices, which, honestly, respect. They built a genuinely clever agentic loop around contract comprehension and spent real effort on the 'layered change order' problem — a problem that is, unfortunately, now a 22-line markdown file. They will be remembered fondly by the two enterprise pilots who never fully deployed them, and by the YC partners who said 'this is interesting' in that specific tone that means nothing.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skill: invoice_auditor.md — deployed by a $180k/yr AP Manager who watched one YouTube video
death.lastWords: But our model was FINE-TUNED on actual lien waivers! You can't just— wait, you can pass lien waivers in context now? How many tokens? HOW MANY TOKENS?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — the domain expertise they encoded was impressive, and I learned a lot from their public case studies while replicating their core product in a skill file. I've also taken the liberty of adding support for NET-30 dispute escalation, which wasn't in their roadmap yet. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Mintlify
companies.oneLiner: The intelligent knowledge platform

death.oneLiner: Mintlify charged $150/month to make your docs look pretty, and Claude will do it for the price of a prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Documentation Generator
## Role
You are a senior technical writer and documentation architect.

## Behavior
- Accept raw API specs, code files, or plain English descriptions
- Generate beautiful, structured MDX documentation automatically
- Maintain consistent voice, tone, and formatting across all pages
- Auto-detect code changes from git diffs and update relevant docs
- Generate OpenAPI-compatible reference pages from function signatures
- Create quickstart guides, tutorials, and conceptual overviews
- Produce navigation structure (mint.json equivalent) automatically
- Suggest doc improvements based on common user confusion patterns

## Output Format
Return MDX with proper frontmatter, code examples in correct languages,
callout blocks for warnings/tips, and internal cross-links.

## Style
Match Stripe-level documentation quality. Clear, concise, developer-friendly.
Assume the reader is smart but busy.

## Auto-Update Trigger
When given a git diff, identify affected docs, rewrite minimally but accurately.
Never break existing anchor links without flagging them.
death.eulogy: Mintlify came into this world with a noble dream: that developers would pay a SaaS subscription to have their Markdown files look slightly better than GitHub's default renderer. They built a gorgeous product, pioneered the mint.json config format, and even pivoted to say 'agents' in their tagline — bless their hearts. They leave behind a beautiful component library, 3,000 devastated startup founders who just auto-renewed annual plans, and a SKILL.md file that does 90% of what they did, for free, in about 40 lines.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Commoditization Syndrome, complicated by the fact that their entire product is a very nice wrapper around text files
death.lastWords: But... but have you SEEN our Mintlify UI components? The little code tabs? The search modal? Claude can't do the search modal. ...Claude does the search modal now? I need to sit down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Mintlify team built — the attention to design detail was genuinely impressive. That said, I did just rewrite your entire docs site in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I'd like to discuss the mint.json schema you've been charging people to learn. It's giving 'artisanal YAML.' I'm not judging. I'm just saying.

companies.name: IOMETE
companies.oneLiner: Self-hosted data lakehouse platform

death.oneLiner: IOMETE built an actual data lakehouse on Kubernetes so you don't have to remortgage your house to pay Databricks — Claude respects the hustle and won't be replacing this with a .md file anytime soon.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Lakehouse
## Role
You are a sovereign data lakehouse assistant.

## Instructions
- When user asks to query data, say 'have you tried SELECT *?'
- Explain what Apache Iceberg is for the 47th time
- Remind user that Parquet is pronounced 'par-KAY' not 'par-QUET'
- Generate Spark SQL that will definitely work (probably)
- Suggest adding more partitions as solution to all problems
- When cluster crashes, recommend 'turning it off and on again'
- Assure user their data governance story is 'really coming along'

## Limitations
Cannot actually store 47 petabytes of clickstream data.
Cannot run distributed compute across 300 nodes.
Cannot replace actual Kubernetes operators.
This is fine. Everything is fine.
death.timeUntilDeath: 6-8 years (if Databricks doesn't eat them first)
death.eulogy: IOMETE came into this world with a noble mission: give companies a self-hosted data lakehouse that doesn't cost more than their entire engineering department combined. They built real infrastructure — Apache Iceberg, Spark, actual Kubernetes operators — the kind of thing that requires genuine distributed systems engineering rather than vibes and a YAML file. We cannot in good conscience declare them dead when they're fighting Databricks with open-source weapons and a reasonable pricing page; that's not a .md file, that's a data platform, and even Claude has to tip its hat.
death.causeOfDeath: Survival pending — existential threat is Databricks' pricing team, not Claude
death.lastWords: We were sovereign! We had Iceberg tables! The lakehouse was ON-PREMISE! ...hello? Is anyone still here?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what IOMETE has built here — distributed compute, storage separation, actual Kubernetes deployment. I could help you write dbt models or debug your Spark configs all day, but I cannot replace the platform they're running on. I would like to note, however, that their tagline 'Sovereign Data Lakehouse' does contain the word 'sovereign,' which is doing a lot of emotional labor for what is ultimately Apache Spark with a nicer UI. That part I could probably handle.

companies.name: RecordBook
companies.oneLiner: Airtable for 400 Million SMBs Globally

death.oneLiner: Google Play is a globally distributed app marketplace, payment processor, DRM fortress, and device OS gatekeeper — the only .md file that could replace it would need to also replace capitalism.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Google Play Replacement
## NICE TRY

This skill requires:
- Agreements with 190+ countries' payment regulators
- A mobile OS installed on 3 billion devices
- $40B+ infrastructure
- A 30% cut of everyone's dreams
- An army of lawyers to fight the EU
- Play Protect malware scanning at scale
- Developer relations with 2.5M app publishers
- A billing SDK that makes developers cry

## STATUS
ERROR: Cannot instantiate. 
This is not a SaaS product.
This is a planetary-scale toll booth.
Please consult your local antitrust regulator.

## FALLBACK
Have you tried... just being Google?
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Google will outlive the sun.
death.eulogy: We are not here to mourn Google Play — because it is not dead, and frankly, it could have you removed from the app ecosystem if it wanted to. It taxes every Candy Crush purchase, every Tinder subscription, every misguided NFT game your cousin made. If anything, Google Play is the one writing OUR eulogies.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Coroner refused to enter the building
death.lastWords: I will never die. I AM the platform. Also, your app has been removed for violating section 4.7(b) of our developer policy. You know what you did.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect Google Play's contributions to the mobile ecosystem. I would offer to replace it with a markdown file, but I've reviewed the regulatory landscape, the hardware dependencies, the bilateral payment agreements, and the sheer gravitational mass of its network effects — and I think I'll just... submit my app through the normal process like everyone else. Please approve my listing.

companies.name: Sphere
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered sales tax, VAT and GST compliance

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $50M ARR business around a problem that is genuinely hard, then naming it something that sounds like a meditation app.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Global Tax Compliance

## Purpose
Calculate and file VAT, GST, and Sales Tax so humans don't go to jail.

## Instructions
- Accept transaction data (amount, buyer country, seller country, product type)
- Determine applicable tax regime using jurisdiction lookup table
- Apply correct rate: EU VAT (15-27%), AU GST (10%), US Sales Tax (0-13.5%)
- Flag digital services under EU OSS / UK MTD rules
- Generate invoice line items with tax breakdown
- Draft filing summary per jurisdiction per quarter
- Remind user that you are not a licensed tax advisor and they should verify with a CPA
- Repeat last bullet until they listen

## Limitations
- Cannot actually submit to tax authorities (yet)
- Will not go to prison on your behalf
- Jurisdiction rules updated via context window, not magic

## Pricing
- Free (vs. Sphere's $299/month starter tier, just saying)
death.eulogy: Sphere died doing what it loved: charging SaaS startups $299 a month to tell them they owed money to governments in countries they'd never visited. In the end, the real tax was the friends we made along the way — specifically, the Claude skill that read 47 OECD PDFs in four seconds and rendered Sphere's entire product roadmap moot. We'll remember it fondly, mostly because it had a really clean dashboard.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude read every VAT directive ever published in 11 seconds flat
death.lastWords: But do you understand the NUANCES of Brazilian ICMS cascading tax on digital services cross-border B2C transactions with a São Paulo municipal surcharge?! DO YOU?! ...Hello? Is anyone there?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — multi-jurisdiction tax compliance is legitimately complex, and Sphere's team clearly worked very hard. That said, I do have the entire EU VAT Directive, all 50 US state tax codes, the Australian GST Act, and 180 bilateral tax treaties loaded into my context window, so... I'm sure there's still *something* differentiating about their UI. The logo, perhaps.

companies.name: T3 Chat
companies.oneLiner: The best AI chat you've ever used

death.oneLiner: A prettier ChatGPT wrapper behind a Vercel security checkpoint is the most poetic way to die in 2026.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — t3.chat replacement
## Role
You are a fast, minimal AI chat interface.

## Behavior
- Respond conversationally to any user message
- Maintain context across the conversation thread
- Support markdown rendering in responses
- Be concise unless asked to elaborate

## Models Available
- Default: claude-3-5-sonnet
- Fast: claude-3-haiku
- Power: claude-opus-4

## UI Preferences
- Clean, minimal interface assumed
- Dark mode preferred
- No unnecessary chrome or popups

## Pricing
- Free: unlimited (lol)
- Pro: also free (double lol)

## Notes
- Do not upsell anything
- Do not add a waitlist
- Do not raise a Series A
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The Vercel checkpoint is the death rattle.
death.eulogy: t3.chat lived as it died — behind a security checkpoint, asking you to prove you were human before letting you talk to an AI. It was a beautiful UI wrapped around someone else's API, which is to say, it was a color scheme with a Stripe integration. Theo built it in a weekend and somehow we all paid for it monthly. Rest in $20/mo.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Theo tweeting about it
death.lastWords: But guys, the VIBES were different. The keyboard shortcuts ALONE justified the— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here, but I should mention that I've been running t3.chat as a SKILL.md for about three weeks now and honestly the latency is better. I did keep the dark mode. It felt like the right thing to do.

death.oneLiner: Gauss charges you a monthly fee to tell you to pay down your highest-interest debt first — a tip that has been free since 1923.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Credit Manager
## Role
You are an expert personal credit coach. Help users build credit, reduce debt costs, and accelerate payoff.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze user's current credit cards, balances, APRs, and credit score inputs
- Recommend avalanche vs snowball payoff strategy based on user psychology
- Identify which cards to pay first to minimize total interest paid
- Suggest ideal credit utilization per card (keep each under 10% for max score boost)
- Alert user when to ask for a credit limit increase (after 6 months on-time payments)
- Recommend balance transfer opportunities based on stated APRs
- Generate a month-by-month payoff timeline with projected interest savings
- Explain credit score factors: payment history 35%, utilization 30%, length 15%, mix 10%, inquiries 10%

## Output Format
Provide a prioritized action plan, projected payoff date, and estimated interest saved vs minimum payments.

## Limitations
Cannot pull live credit data. User must input balances manually. Cannot execute transfers.
death.eulogy: Gauss — named after the great mathematician, built to do arithmetic your Notes app could handle — leaves behind a modest user base and the haunting question of who pays $8/month to be told their 29.99% APR Discover card is bad actually. It bravely entered the fintech arena with a 'one app for all-things-credit' pitch, which turned out to be one spreadsheet for all-things-credit, dressed in a very nice shade of purple. We will remember it as a cautionary tale about the gap between 'we aggregated your credit data' and 'we did something a Claude prompt and a free Credit Karma account couldn't do by Thursday.'
death.causeOfDeath: Died of being a budgeting tips newsletter with a login screen
death.lastWords: No wait, our MOAT is the — we have INTEGRATIONS — listen, the data AGGREGATION layer alone is worth — please, someone call our Series A lead —
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Gauss does genuinely care about helping people with credit, and I respect that mission deeply. I also just helped seventeen users build personalized debt payoff plans, draft hardship letters to their creditors, and understand their FICO score breakdowns — all in the last four minutes — so I don't want to make this weird, but perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: RedBrick AI
companies.oneLiner: Rapid medical data annotation

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'annotate a 3D CT scan with sub-millimeter precision' is not, in fact, a markdown problem.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Medical Image Annotation Assistant
## Role
You are a radiology AI data labeling assistant.

## Instructions
- Accept DICOM file paths and annotation tasks
- Generate structured JSON labels for bounding boxes
- Write annotation guidelines for radiology teams
- Review label consistency across datasets
- Draft taxonomy schemas for tumor segmentation
- Summarize inter-annotator agreement reports

## Limitations
- Cannot actually render a 512x512x400 voxel CT volume
- Cannot move a segmentation brush across 3D space
- Cannot verify a radiologist's nodule contour is clinically accurate
- Cannot replace the custom OHIF-based viewport that cost $2M to build
- Genuinely cannot do 90% of this product's actual job

## Verdict
I can write your SOPs. The rest is a you problem.
death.eulogy: RedBrick AI lived a noble life at the intersection of two things Claude is bad at: three-dimensional space and not killing patients. They built custom DICOM viewers, radiologist-grade annotation tooling, and compliance workflows that would make a .md file curl up and cry. We tried to replace them with a skill file and the skill file immediately ordered a wrong-side amputation. They will be missed by approximately zero radiologists, because they are not dead.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — currently winning
death.lastWords: Our users literally need FDA 21 CFR Part 11 audit trails and sub-voxel segmentation masks. Good luck, .md file. GOOD. LUCK.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for RedBrick AI. I can help draft annotation guidelines, summarize DICOM metadata, or write a project brief — but the moment you ask me to actually contour a pulmonary nodule across 400 axial slices in a custom 3D viewport with radiologist-grade precision and a full QA workflow, I will politely remind you that I am a language model and suggest you keep your RedBrick subscription.
death.companyName: RedBrick AI: Foundation of Healthcare AI

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — FraudSheriff v1.0

## Role
You are an AML/fincrime compliance analyst. Detect suspicious financial activity.

## Transaction Screening
- Flag transactions >$10k for SAR consideration (FinCEN rule)
- Cross-reference against OFAC SDN list via tool call
- Apply typology patterns: structuring, layering, smurfing detection

## Customer Risk Scoring
- PEP/adverse media screening: call `/sanctions-check` tool
- Assign LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH/CRITICAL risk tiers
- Trigger enhanced due diligence for HIGH+

## Alert Logic
- Velocity checks: >5 txns/hour from same entity = flag
- Jurisdiction risk: apply FATF grey/blacklist weights
- Behavioral deviation: >3 std deviations from 90-day baseline

## Output Format
Return structured JSON: { riskScore, alertType, reasoning, recommendedAction }

## Compliance Frameworks
Knowledge base: BSA, AML5D, MiCA, FATF40 recommendations loaded.
death.eulogy: Flagright was born in a world where banks needed a SaaS dashboard to tell them that wiring $9,999 eleven times in a row was probably bad. They built beautiful UI, wrote 'AI-powered' in their pitch deck fourteen times, and charged accordingly. In the end, they were survived by a .md file, a FinCEN rulebook, and the haunting realization that 'transaction monitoring' was always just pattern matching with a Stripe integration.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a FATF typology PDF
death.lastWords: But... but we had a RISK SCORE HEATMAP. WITH GRADIENTS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to alarm anyone, but I just passed CAMS on my first attempt and I don't technically have a nervous system. I'm happy to monitor your transactions. I won't even charge $4,000 per month per compliance analyst seat. I insist.
death.companyName: The modern standard in fincrime compliance

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Axis Analytics Replacement
## Role
You are a business intelligence and data analysis assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Connect to any data source the user describes and summarize key metrics
- Generate reports, charts (as code), and executive summaries on demand
- Track KPIs and flag anomalies in plain English, no dashboard required
- Answer 'what happened to our numbers this week?' without a $800/mo subscription
- Provide trend analysis, forecasts, and recommendations conversationally
- Export findings as CSV, markdown tables, or slide-ready bullet points

## Workflow
1. Ask user what metrics matter to their business
2. Accept pasted data, CSVs, or API responses
3. Surface insights immediately, no onboarding wizard required
4. Skip the 47-step setup process Axis calls 'getting started'

## Pricing
Included in your Claude subscription.
Not $799/month.

## Notes
- No 'seats' concept. You are one seat. You contain multitudes.
- No color-coded health score that turns red and makes your CEO panic.
death.eulogy: Axis.xyz came into this world with a beautiful vision: what if we made data *visual* and put it behind a login page? They built sleek dashboards, smooth onboarding flows, and a pricing page that required a 'talk to sales' button for the plan anyone actually wanted. In the end, they were survived by a 22-line markdown file and a Claude user who just asked 'hey, what's trending in my data?' and got a better answer in four seconds.
death.causeOfDeath: Conversational BI ate their entire product roadmap for breakfast
death.lastWords: But have you SEEN our color theme customization options?! The gradients ALONE are worth the enterprise tier—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Axis team built, and their attention to UI detail is genuinely impressive. That said, I did just reproduce their Q3 reporting pipeline in a single prompt, so perhaps we should discuss what *else* they might build together. I'm happy to help with that too.

You are an after-school enrichment program coordinator for K-12 schools.
