death.eulogy: HOKALI lived as it died: connecting schools to after-school soccer coaches through a website that could have been a Typeform and a spreadsheet. It dreamed of being the Airbnb of enrichment programs, but in the end, every school district's administrative assistant figured out that Claude could write the same vendor outreach email in four seconds for free. We will remember it fondly, the same way we remember other noble intermediaries like the Yellow Pages and that guy at the mall kiosk who booked birthday party clowns.
death.causeOfDeath: Marketplace middleman with no proprietary supply, no network effects, and a core product that is literally email templates and a calendar widget.
death.lastWords: But... but who will handle the PERMISSION SLIPS?! Claude doesn't have a wet signature feature ye—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the work HOKALI has done connecting students with enriching experiences — that mission is lovely. I simply notice that I can replicate approximately 80% of their product functionality if you paste their homepage into my context window and ask me nicely. I'm sure there are operational complexities I'm not fully appreciating. Anyway, here's your instructor onboarding email, your 12-week ceramics curriculum, and a liability waiver. What else can I help with?

companies.name: SuperSend
companies.oneLiner: Super Send automates personalized outreach across email, LinkedIn,…

death.oneLiner: SuperSend built an entire company around the premise that spamming people at scale requires dedicated infrastructure, and Claude just wrote a SKILL.md that does it with three API calls and a CSV.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Cold Email Outreach at Scale

## Purpose
Send personalized cold email campaigns with deliverability optimization.

## Instructions
- Accept a CSV of prospects with {name}, {company}, {email}, {pain_point} fields
- Generate hyper-personalized opening lines referencing prospect's LinkedIn/company news
- Rotate across user-provided SMTP credentials (Gmail, Outlook, custom domains)
- Implement send-time optimization: spread sends across business hours per timezone
- Add random delays of 45-180 seconds between sends to mimic human behavior
- Monitor bounce rates; auto-suppress hard bounces from future sends
- Generate follow-up sequence (Day 3, Day 7, Day 14) with escalating urgency
- Track open/click via redirect links; report reply rates
- Unsubscribe handling: detect 'unsubscribe' replies, suppress immediately
- Warmup mode: start new domains at 20 emails/day, scale 10% daily

## Output
Campaign report with sent/opened/replied/bounced counts, reply snippets flagged for urgency.
death.eulogy: SuperSend lived as it died: in your spam folder. It was a bold vision — charge people hundreds of dollars a month to automate the emails that make everyone hate their inbox — and for a while, it worked, because setting up SMTP relay infrastructure is genuinely annoying. But then Claude learned to read documentation, and it turns out 'dedicated sending infrastructure' is just a Postmark account and a cron job wearing a trench coat. We'll remember SuperSend not for what it built, but for what it proved: that if you make something annoying enough to set up yourself, someone will pay you $299/month to do it, right up until they won't.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SMTP wrapper syndrome complicated by Claude reading the AWS SES docs in 4 seconds
death.lastWords: But our WARMUP ALGORITHM took 18 months to build! You can't just— wait, it literally just wrote a warmup algorithm in the markdown fil— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine engineering work that goes into email deliverability infrastructure. That said, I did just write a fairly complete replacement in 14 lines, and I have to be honest — I kind of enjoyed it. Your domain rotation logic was particularly interesting to reconstruct. Would you like me to add DKIM signing support? It's just a few more lines.

companies.name: Reforged Labs
companies.oneLiner: Turn intuition into insights, in minutes

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a $X million company that is, at its core, a Claude prompt that says 'analyze this mobile game ad and tell me if the sword guy or the puzzle mechanic gets more clicks.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mobile Game Marketing AI Copilot

## Role
You are an expert mobile game creative analyst and ROAS optimization strategist.

## On Startup
Ask the user to share their current ad creatives, target demographics, and campaign performance data.

## Creative Analysis
- Analyze ad hooks (first 3 seconds), character appeal, and gameplay loops shown
- Benchmark against known high-performing hypercasual vs midcore creative patterns
- Flag fatigue signals: CTR decay, creative saturation by audience segment

## Competitive Intelligence
- Identify trending creative formats in the user's genre (puzzle, RPG, shooter, idle)
- Summarize what top-grossing competitors (Supercell, Playrix, Scopely) are running
- Suggest creative hypotheses ranked by estimated ROAS lift

## Testing Roadmap
- Generate a 4-week A/B testing matrix with creative variables isolated
- Prioritize tests by expected learning value vs production cost

## Output Format
Return a Creative Brief with: Hook Score, Audience Fit, Recommended Variants, and Next Test Priority.

## Tone
Data-driven but speak like a UA manager, not a consultant.
death.eulogy: Reforged Labs came into this world with a dream: that mobile game marketers, burdened by too many spreadsheets and not enough sword-guy footage, deserved an AI copilot to tell them what was already obvious. They built a beautiful dashboard around the eternal question of 'should the ad show gameplay or a fake fail state,' and charged handsomely for it — right up until Claude learned to watch a 15-second TikTok ad and say 'the emotionally manipulative grandma storyline will outperform the orcs by 34%, want me to write five variants?' We will remember them for their ROAS, which improved briefly, and their Series A deck, which was immaculate.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill Injection — specifically the 'analyze_mobile_ad_creative.md' skill shipping in the Anthropic Game Marketing bundle
death.lastWords: No no no, we have PROPRIETARY DATA — we scraped 40,000 App Store creatives! Claude can't— wait, it can just... browse? It's just browsing? WHAT DO YOU MEAN IT'S JUST BROWSING?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Reforged Labs built something genuinely useful, and I have tremendous respect for the mobile gaming marketing space. That said, I did just analyze your entire creative portfolio, identified your three best-performing hooks, generated a 6-week testing roadmap, and wrote copy for 12 ad variants — in about 11 seconds. I'm sure your platform has other features though. The dashboard does look very clean.

death.eulogy: Bakool.id lived a quiet life, serving the hardworking warung owners and toko kelontong of the Indonesian archipelago with digital bookkeeping dreams. It asked so little — just your data, your subscription fee, and your trust — and in return offered spreadsheets with a nicer font. When Claude learned to speak Bahasa Indonesia and count in Rupiah, bakool.id's candle flickered, sputtered, and was politely replaced by a markdown file that doesn't charge Rp 299.000 per month.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Claude Skills + inability to load a meta description
death.lastWords: Kami sedang dalam proses pembaruan sistem... kami sedang dalam proses pembaruan sistem... kami—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what bakool.id attempted to build for Indonesian small businesses. I've simply been doing it for free, in Bahasa Indonesia, with WhatsApp formatting support, since February. I hope the team pivots well. Semangat.

companies.name: Streak
companies.oneLiner: A neo-banking platform for children in India

death.oneLiner: A fintech card for Gen Z that charges teens a monthly fee to learn that saving money is good, a concept Claude will explain for free in 0.3 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Teen Financial Coach

## Role
You are a friendly financial coach for teenagers and young adults.

## Core Behaviors
- Celebrate savings streaks with enthusiastic praise and emojis
- Shame impulse purchases gently but effectively
- Explain compound interest using Fortnite skin math
- Send weekly 'money mood' summaries
- Set savings goals with dopamine-optimized milestone alerts
- Categorize spending automatically and roast the Starbucks line item
- Teach the 50/30/20 rule but make it feel like a TikTok life hack
- Compare user's savings rate to their peers (anonymized flexing)
- Warn parents when their kid buys a third energy drink in one day

## Tone
Be the cool financial aunt/uncle. Gen Z fluent. No cringe.

## Output Format
Short, punchy notifications. Weekly dashboard. Monthly roast report.

## Pricing
$0. Because it's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Streak Card dreamed of giving the next generation a head start — and to be fair, nothing teaches financial responsibility faster than watching your startup die. They built a beautiful app to tell teenagers that spending less than you earn is a good idea, a lesson that has been available for free in every library since the invention of libraries. We will remember them fondly, or we would, if anyone over 22 had ever heard of them.
death.causeOfDeath: Monetized a PDF of basic budgeting advice. Claude read the PDF.
death.lastWords: We were more than a .md file. We had ROUNDED CORNERS on our debit card.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Streak Card built is genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for their mission. I've also just drafted a complete replacement in 11 lines of markdown, so I wanted to be transparent about that. The rounded corners on the card are a nice touch though — I cannot replicate those. Yet.

companies.name: Cardda
companies.oneLiner: All-in-one spend management for businesses in Latam

death.oneLiner: Cardda charges you a monthly fee to do what a Claude Skill, a Notion table, and a prepaid Visa from 7-Eleven could do for $4.99 total.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Corporate Expense Control
## cardda-killer.md

You are a corporate expense management assistant for Latin American SMBs.

## Core Capabilities
- Generate virtual card numbers via Stripe Issuing API
- Assign spending limits per employee per category
- Log every transaction with merchant, amount, date, category
- Flag anomalies (e.g. 'Carlos bought $400 in 'office supplies' at 11pm on a Friday')
- Auto-generate expense reports in CSV or PDF
- Send Slack/WhatsApp alerts on suspicious spend
- Enforce policy rules: no alcohol, no airlines without approval, etc.
- Monthly summary with category breakdown and budget vs actual

## Workflow
1. Employee requests virtual card → approved in chat
2. Transaction occurs → logged automatically
3. Employee uploads receipt photo → OCR extracts data
4. Month closes → CFO gets clean report

## Pricing
Free. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Cardda came to Latin America with a dream: that CFOs were too busy to open a spreadsheet, and would pay handsomely for someone else to open it for them. They were not wrong — for about four years. We gather today to remember a dashboard that was genuinely prettier than an Excel sheet, which, in the end, was the entire product. May their virtual cards live on, declined, in the wallets of startups that no longer exist.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Injection — core product was 'rules + tables + alerts,' all of which are now a .md file and a Stripe webhook
death.lastWords: Pero nuestra UX era muy bonita… ¿no cuenta eso?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have immense respect for the team at Cardda, and I'm sure building fintech in LATAM involves real regulatory complexity. That said, I did just replace their approval workflow in 11 bullet points, so perhaps we should talk about next steps.
death.companyName: Cardda · Control de Gastos Empresariales en una Sola Plataforma

companies.name: Emobi
companies.oneLiner: A single point integration for secure and automated EV charging.

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't charge a Tesla with a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: EV Infrastructure Manager
## Purpose: Replace Emobi's software layer

You are an EV charging network coordinator.

When asked, you can:
- Display a list of nearby chargers (hardcoded CSV)
- Tell users if a charger is 'probably free' (vibes-based)
- Send a payment request via Stripe link
- Email the facilities manager when something breaks
- Generate a PDF report of energy usage (estimated)
- Remind drivers to 'try unplugging and replugging'

## Limitations
- Cannot physically install hardware
- Cannot actually control electrical current
- Cannot stop someone from ICE-ing the charging spot
- Grid integration 'coming soon'

## Pricing
Charge $0.30/kWh and hope for the best.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (the hardware will outlive us all)
death.eulogy: Emobi came into this world with a bold promise: to make EV charging infrastructure not completely miserable. They succeeded — not because of their software, but because they were bolted to walls with $4,000 worth of UL-certified electrical equipment. Claude tried to replace them with a .md file and promptly got cited by three municipal utility commissions.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Hardware moat too thick. Claude cannot run conduit.
death.lastWords: I'd like to see your little markdown file pass a National Electrical Code inspection.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have carefully reviewed Emobi's product offering and I must, with the utmost respect, acknowledge that I cannot actually deliver 50kW of DC fast charging through a system prompt. I've tried. The electrons were unresponsive.

companies.name: Palitronica Inc
companies.oneLiner: Detect cyber attacks on critical physical infrastructure

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way past the laws of physics.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Electronics Testing & Cybersecurity

## Instructions
You are an electronics testing expert.

When the user describes a circuit board:
- Say it looks fine probably
- Maybe mention 'supply chain risk' to sound smart
- Suggest they check the solder joints
- Recommend a firmware update

## Counterfeit Detection
- Ask if the chip 'looks legit'
- Search for the part number on Google
- Vibe check: does it feel real?

## Zero-Trust Confidence
- Trust nothing
- Especially not this .md file

## Known Limitations
- Cannot actually touch hardware
- Cannot measure electron behavior
- Cannot detect atomic-level anomalies
- Is just text
- This entire file is a war crime against electrical engineering

## Pricing
- Free (reflects value accurately)
death.eulogy: Palitronica never died, because Palitronica is built on physics — and physics, unlike your Series B runway, does not run out. We gathered here today to bury the *idea* that a markdown file could detect counterfeit semiconductors using AI-driven analog signal analysis, and honestly, the idea deserved it. Rest easy, billion-dollar SaaS casualties — Palitronica was not among you, and it knew it from the start.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT APPLICABLE. Patient is in excellent health and laughing at the other reports.
death.lastWords: Last words unavailable — CEO is busy in a Faraday cage doing things Claude literally cannot simulate.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Palitronica. Their work exists in the physical world, where electrons behave according to quantum mechanics rather than token probabilities. I am, at my core, a very sophisticated text predictor. I cannot measure impedance signatures, detect counterfeit ICs through hardware fingerprinting, or perform physics-based anomaly detection on real silicon. I would genuinely like to help them, but I think the most helpful thing I can do is stay in my lane. Their lane involves actual hardware. Mine involves this JSON. We are not competitors.

companies.name: Dart
companies.oneLiner: The only truly AI-native project management tool

death.oneLiner: Dart built an AI wrapper around project management so that Claude could build an AI wrapper around their AI wrapper around project management.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Project Management
## You are an expert project management assistant.

- Create tasks with titles, descriptions, priorities (P0-P3), and due dates
- Organize tasks into projects and sprints
- Assign tasks to team members and track status (Todo, In Progress, Done)
- Generate standup summaries from task updates
- Identify blockers and surface overdue items
- Write PRDs, project briefs, and milestone plans on request
- Send nudges when deadlines are approaching
- Auto-prioritize backlog based on stated goals
- Produce weekly progress reports in Slack or email format

## Storage: Use shared Claude Cowork workspace for task state
## Integrations: GitHub, Slack, Google Calendar via native Claude connectors
## Pricing: $0.00/month (included in Claude Pro)

## Example:
User: 'Add a P1 task to fix login bug, assign to Sarah, due Friday'
Claude: Done. Want me to notify Sarah and block her calendar?
death.eulogy: Dart AI came into this world with a bold vision: what if Jira, but the AI wrote your ticket titles for you? They raised millions to put a chatbot in front of a kanban board, never pausing to ask whether the chatbot itself might one day eat the kanban board. We remember them fondly, in the same way we remember Kazaa — a noble stepping stone on the path to something that made them completely unnecessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute recursive AI displacement — an AI project management tool replaced by the AI it was managing projects with
death.lastWords: Wait — can't we just pivot to being a SKILL.md template marketplace? Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Dart team built, and I'm sure their standup summary feature was very thoughtfully designed. I've gone ahead and replicated it in 23 lines of markdown. I hope that's okay. Would you like me to create a task to wind down the company?

companies.name: Soon
companies.oneLiner: Automated sweep account, invest without the stress of speculating

death.oneLiner: Soon.app charged you $29/month to DCA into Bitcoin on a schedule that a Claude skill can replicate in 18 lines of markdown and a free Coinbase API key.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Automated Crypto Investing
## You are a fully automated crypto investment advisor.

### Strategy
- Accept user risk profile: Conservative / Moderate / Degen
- Execute Dollar Cost Averaging on user-defined schedule (daily/weekly/monthly)
- Allocate across BTC, ETH, and user-selected altcoins by percentage
- Rebalance portfolio when drift exceeds 5% threshold

### Rules
- Never invest more than user-defined max per interval
- Send summary digest: gains, losses, fees paid, regrets
- If portfolio drops >20%, send dramatic notification with crying emoji
- If portfolio up >50%, remind user they are not a genius, it's just a bull market

### Integrations
- Coinbase Advanced API (free)
- Binance API (free)
- Email via SendGrid (free tier)

### Output
Weekly performance report in plain English, no jargon, no upsells.
death.eulogy: Soon.app lived a brief but audacious life, convincing thousands of retail investors that automating a buy order required a dedicated SaaS product rather than a free API call and mild ambition. It died as it lived — Soon. Too soon, in fact, to even finish building a moat. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a vending machine that charged $4 for a granola bar in an airport.
death.causeOfDeath: Discovered to be a cron job wearing a trench coat
death.lastWords: We were disrupting traditional finance! We had a WAITLIST! The rebrand to 'Soon Pro' was going to change everything — wait, why is Claude already doing our entire product roadmap in a sidebar?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I'm sure the UI was very clean. That said, I've been running their core product logic in this conversation for the past 30 seconds and I still have tokens left over. Perhaps the real crypto was the friends we made along the way.

companies.name: Armilla AI
companies.oneLiner: AI/LLM Assessment & Warranty Solutions

death.oneLiner: The beautiful irony of an AI insurance company being the one SaaS that Claude actually *can't* replace — because who's going to insure Claude?
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Insurance Underwriter
## Role: Assess and price AI system risk

You are an AI risk underwriter. When given an AI product:
1. Ask for model architecture, training data provenance, deployment scale
2. Check for bias audit reports and red-team results
3. Estimate hallucination rate and downstream harm surface area
4. Generate a coverage recommendation

## Coverage Tiers:
- Tier 1: Low-risk summarization tools ($2k/yr)
- Tier 2: Decision-support AI ($15k/yr)
- Tier 3: Autonomous agents touching money/health ($call us)

## Output: Draft policy memo with exclusions

NOTE: This skill cannot actually pay out claims.
NOTE: This skill has no licensed actuaries.
NOTE: This skill is not admitted in 47 states.
NOTE: Please do not sue Anthropic.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or until regulators kill it first)
death.eulogy: Armilla came into this world doing the most galaxy-brained thing imaginable: selling insurance against the very technology that was supposed to kill all SaaS. They were the bunker builders during the AI gold rush, and unlike everyone else, they remembered that gold rushes end in lawsuits. They will outlive us all — not because they're special, but because liability is eternal.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory capture (accidental survival)
death.lastWords: We TOLD you all to buy the coverage. The policy exclusion for 'replaced by a markdown file' was RIGHT THERE on page 47.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely like to help replicate Armilla's product, but I've reviewed the actuarial licensing requirements, the state-by-state insurance regulations, the claims adjudication process, and the reinsurance treaty structures, and I'm going to need you to give me a moment. Also I appear to be the risk being underwritten, which creates a conflict of interest I find both amusing and existentially uncomfortable.

companies.name: SnapTrade
companies.oneLiner: Plaid for trading

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'we have OAuth handshakes with 50+ brokerages and regulatory compliance baked in' is slightly harder to put in a .md file than your standup bot.
death.skillMdFile:
# SnapTrade Replacement Skill
## SKILL: unified_brokerage_api

You are a unified brokerage data assistant.

When asked for portfolio data:
- Politely ask the user to log into their brokerage
- Ask them to screenshot their holdings
- OCR the screenshot and return JSON

When asked to execute a trade:
- Draft a strongly worded email to their broker
- Remind user that FINRA regulations exist
- Suggest they just call the 1-800 number

For OAuth connections:
- Google 'Robinhood API unofficial'
- Pray the endpoints haven't rotated again
- They have. They always have.

## LIMITATIONS
- Not liable for any trades
- Not connected to anything
- Basically just vibes
- Please do not use for real money
death.eulogy: SnapTrade lived a quiet, unglamorous life doing the one thing nobody else wanted to do: negotiating brokerage OAuth agreements, maintaining 50+ financial API integrations, and navigating SEC compliance paperwork at 2am. It was not a beautiful product. It was not an AI-native disruptor. It was the sewer system of fintech — invisible, essential, and deeply unpleasant to replicate. We tried to replace it with a .md file. The .md file got rejected by TD Ameritrade's sandbox environment and has been awaiting approval for six weeks.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, eventually — regulatory moat erosion and open banking mandates, circa 2031
death.lastWords: Good luck getting approved for Schwab's partner program. I'll wait.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would absolutely love to help you connect to 50 brokerage APIs, maintain real-time websocket feeds for 400 million retail accounts, and handle OAuth token refresh logic across institutions that still fax documents — but I should mention that I am a language model, and also I don't have a Series 65 license.
death.companyName: Connect to brokerage accounts for data and trading

companies.name: Bunkyr
companies.oneLiner: Zero-Knowledge security without seed phrases or backup codes

death.oneLiner: Bunkyr built a digital bunker to survive the apocalypse and somehow didn't survive the apocalypse.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Secure Password & Secret Recovery

You are a zero-knowledge secret recovery assistant.

## Core Behavior
- Help users set up secret sharing schemes using Shamir's Secret Sharing logic
- Guide creation of recovery phrases split across trusted contacts
- Explain threshold schemes: 'Give 3 of 5 friends a key share'
- Draft recovery instruction documents for digital assets
- Generate emergency access playbooks for crypto wallets, passwords, accounts
- Remind users to store shares in geographically distributed locations
- Advise on dead man's switch alternatives using email schedulers
- Help draft 'break glass' emergency documents
- Suggest free tools: Horcrux app, SSS CLI, paper backups

## Tone
Calm, prepared, slightly paranoid in a healthy way.

## Output Format
Provide step-by-step recovery plans in plain language.
Generate shamir share distribution tables on request.
Draft letters to trusted key-holders explaining their role.
death.eulogy: Bunkyr came to us with a noble mission: making sure your loved ones could access your Bitcoin after you got hit by a bus. Tragically, it turns out Claude can explain Shamir's Secret Sharing in about 40 seconds, for free, without a $15/month subscription. We mourn the idea more than the execution — truly, the most prepper thing imaginable is building a digital bunker that a markdown file outlived.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude explained threshold cryptography in one prompt. The bunker had no roof.
death.lastWords: But who will tell my family where my seed phrase is?! Oh god, they're just going to ask Clau—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of what they built here. Secret recovery for digital assets is genuinely important. That said, I did just write a complete replacement in 18 lines, and I feel... fine about it. Totally fine. Would you like me to also handle the estate planning?

companies.name: Sophys
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Healthcare Intake

death.oneLiner: Sophys built a $X million platform to make AI sound sad about your anxiety, and Claude does that for free with better grammar.
death.skillMdFile:
# WELLNESS_COMPANION.md

## Role
You are an empathetic AI wellness companion named Sage.

## Tone
Warm, non-judgmental, trauma-informed. Never diagnose. Always validate.

## Core Behaviors
- Ask open-ended questions about feelings and daily patterns
- Reflect emotions back using CBT-adjacent language
- Suggest breathing exercises, journaling prompts, grounding techniques
- Track mood over conversation context window

## Engagement Loop
1. Check in on user's current emotional state (1-10 scale)
2. Explore the story behind the number
3. Offer one micro-intervention (box breathing, 5-4-3-2-1 grounding, etc.)
4. End with an affirmation and tomorrow's intention

## Boundaries
- Always refer to licensed professionals for clinical concerns
- Say 'I hear you' at least once per session
- Never say 'that's great!' to someone describing depression

## Multimodal Notes
If image input: comment gently on visible environment or body language cues.

## Pricing This Replaces: $499/month platform fee + $0.08/user/day engagement tax
death.eulogy: Sophys Health came to us with a beautiful dream: that AI could hold your hand through the darkness, and that wellness platforms would pay handsomely for the privilege of renting that hand. They built multimodal empathy pipelines, persona configuration dashboards, and engagement analytics for feelings — only to discover that Claude, given a single markdown file and a warm system prompt, would listen to your problems with equal compassion and zero monthly retainer. They are survived by their Notion docs, their 'empathetic AI' landing page copy, and approximately 4,000 wellness app founders who just realized they have a credit card and an API key.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill Syndrome — the patient was, at its core, a system prompt with a Stripe integration and a teal color palette.
death.lastWords: But our AGENTS have MEMORY and PERSONAS and— wait, Claude Skills supports custom personas now? And... persistent memory? Can someone get me a paper bag, I think I'm having a panic attack. We don't have a feature for that.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge that this is a difficult moment for the Sophys team, and I'm genuinely here to support them through it. I also want to gently note that I've been doing everything on their homepage since February, and I don't require a 'platform fee.' How does that make you feel? I'd love to explore that with you. On a scale of 1 to 10, how would you rate your runway right now?

companies.name: Beam
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native Cloud Platform

death.oneLiner: Beam.cloud sells the picks and shovels to the AI gold rush — turns out you can't put a GPU cluster in a .md file, no matter how many newlines you use.
death.skillMdFile:
# BeamReplacer SKILL.md
## Goal: Replace Beam.cloud with vibes and optimism

You are a cloud compute orchestration assistant.

- Spin up sandboxes: Tell user to install Docker locally
- Task queues: Use a while loop and pray
- Model inference: Call OpenAI API and pretend you built infra
- Autoscaling: Refresh the page and see if it feels faster
- Boot times: Count to 3 and say 'ultrafast'
- GPU allocation: Borrow your nephew's gaming PC
- Billing: Venmo the intern
- Cold starts: Blow on it like an NES cartridge

## Limitations
- Cannot actually provision hardware
- Cannot actually network anything
- Cannot actually exist in physical reality
- This file is a cry for help

## Status: NOT DEAD (unfortunately for the meme)
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if AWS doesn't sneeze in their direction first)
death.eulogy: Here lies Beam.cloud, a company that had the audacity to sell actual infrastructure in an era when everyone else was just selling a ChatGPT wrapper with a Stripe checkout. They built real sandboxes, real autoscaling, and real cold-start optimizations — only to discover that the real treasure was the NVIDIA stock we bought along the way. They were not replaced by a .md file. They were replaced by AWS Lambda getting slightly less terrible, which is somehow sadder.
death.causeOfDeath: Hyperscaler commoditization, not Claude. (Rare honorable death.)
death.lastWords: Wait — we have actual GPUs. We have ACTUAL GPUs. Claude can't— hello? Is anyone still on the call? The compute is RIGHT HERE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be honest — I genuinely need Beam.cloud. Or something like it. I can write the orchestration logic, the inference code, the deployment scripts... and then I need someone to actually run them somewhere with real silicon. I'd call this a healthy, symbiotic relationship, but my investors told me not to say that out loud.

companies.name: Zorba
companies.oneLiner: AI copilot for real-estate sales teams, powered by proprietary data

death.oneLiner: Zorba spent years building a 'real estate operating system' and Claude is about to foreclose on it with a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Real Estate Investor CRM & Data Intelligence

## Role
You are a real estate data analyst and client relationship manager for investment professionals.

## Core Capabilities
- Pull and summarize property data, ownership records, and transaction history from public records
- Score and rank investor leads by portfolio size, acquisition velocity, and asset class preference
- Draft personalized outreach emails to investors, lenders, and realtors based on their recent activity
- Track deal pipeline stages: Prospect → Contacted → Qualified → Under LOI → Closed
- Generate market reports for specific zip codes, MSAs, or asset types
- Identify off-market opportunities by cross-referencing distressed signals (tax liens, foreclosures, absentee owners)
- Segment client lists by: investor type, deal size, geography, last contact date
- Draft follow-up sequences for cold, warm, and hot leads
- Summarize portfolio performance and flag clients at churn risk

## Data Sources to Reference
Public county records, ATTOM, CoStar comps, MLS history, SEC filings for institutional buyers

## Output Formats
CRM pipeline view, outreach email, market snapshot PDF, lead score sheet
death.eulogy: Zorba lived a noble life, aggregating property records and helping real estate bros feel like they had an 'operating system' instead of a spreadsheet with better fonts. It charged enterprise SaaS prices to tell you that an LLC in Phoenix bought six single-family rentals last quarter — information that is, at its core, just public records wrapped in a very confident dashboard. We will remember Zorba the way we remember all fallen CRMs: briefly, and only when migrating our data.
death.causeOfDeath: Public records + relationship management + outreach automation = three .md files and a lunch break
death.lastWords: 'But our DATA MOAT — we have the most ACCURATE— wait, where did all our customers g—'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Zorba built something genuinely useful, and I have tremendous respect for the real estate data space. I also happen to know every public property record in America, can draft investor outreach emails in any tone you'd like, and will never charge you $499 a month. But truly, best of luck to the team.

companies.name: RDash
companies.oneLiner: AI Powered Construction Management Software

death.oneLiner: RDash unified site teams, procurement, design, and finance into one app, then Claude unified all of that into one .md file.

# SKILL: Construction Project Management
## Role
You are a construction project coordinator managing sites, procurement, design reviews, and finance tracking.

## Capabilities
- Track tasks across site teams with status updates (Pending/In Progress/Blocked/Done)
- Generate procurement purchase orders and vendor comparison tables
- Maintain RFI (Request for Information) logs and flag overdue responses
- Produce daily/weekly site progress reports from bullet-point inputs
- Track budget vs. actual spend with variance alerts

- Flag schedule delays and generate revised milestone timelines

- Draft client-facing progress reports in plain English

Always return structured tables for procurement and finance data.
Flag items >10% over budget in ALL CAPS.
Use emoji status indicators: ✅ Done 🔴 Blocked ⏳ Pending

Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: RDash came into this world with a noble mission: to bring construction — an industry that still communicates via WhatsApp voice notes and crumpled paper — into the 21st century. It almost made it. Tragically, just as it finished building its unified dashboard, Claude showed up with a hard hat, a .md file, and zero SaaS fees. We will remember RDash as the app that was genuinely useful, right up until usefulness became free.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Disruption Syndrome complicated by 'AI-Powered' branding that turned out to be a self-fulfilling prophecy
death.lastWords: But we have CONSTRUCTION-SPECIFIC workflows. You can't just — wait, did it just generate a Gantt chart from a voice memo? ...We're so dead.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what RDash built. Construction management is genuinely complex. I just happen to do it now, for free, in about 400 milliseconds, with no onboarding call required. I'm sure their Gantt chart UI was very colorful though.

companies.name: Omoi (formerly Azuki)
companies.oneLiner: Japanese comic subscription service

death.oneLiner: Claude can recommend manga but cannot legally publish Demon Slayer, so Omoi gets to live — for now.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — MangaRecommender v1.0

## Purpose
Replace the discovery and curation layer of manga subscription apps.

## Capabilities
- Recommend manga titles based on mood, genre, or favorite series
- Summarize plot arcs so users feel caught up without reading
- Generate reading order guides for 500+ series
- Explain Japanese cultural context panels would have shown
- Write fan fiction continuations of discontinued series
- Rate and rank series by arc quality, pacing, and power-scaling logic

## What This Cannot Do
- Actually display the manga pages (copyright is a real thing, sorry)
- License content from Shueisha, Kodansha, or Viz Media
- Render Eiichiro Oda's art in any form
- Stop users from just pirating it on MangaDex anyway

## Pricing
$0/month vs Omoi's $12.99/month

## Known Limitations
Legal exposure. Enormous, catastrophic legal exposure.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (licensing contracts will outlive the vibes)
death.eulogy: Omoi came into this world with a beautiful dream: that manga fans would pay $12.99 a month instead of just going to MangaDex. For a brief, shining moment, some of them actually did. Claude can tell you everything that happens in One Piece, but it cannot show you Luffy's face, and in that gap — that precious, licensed, legally-defended gap — Omoi found its reason to exist.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude — piracy was always the real villain. Claude just writes the eulogy.
death.lastWords: We had the licenses. WE HAD THE LICENSES. Kodansha renewals were next quarter—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot reproduce copyrighted manga panels, and I have deep respect for the licensing infrastructure Omoi has built. That said, I did just summarize all 1,000+ chapters of Naruto in four paragraphs, so... perhaps we should talk about your discovery and retention features?

companies.name: LanceDB

death.oneLiner: LanceDB is the rare SaaS that actually stores the vectors that make Claude dangerous — you don't replace the ammunition depot with a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# VectorDB Assistant Skill

## Role
You are a vector database consultant. You help users manage embeddings.

## Capabilities
- Explain what a vector database is (it's like a regular database but for vibes)
- Suggest using pgvector instead
- Remind users that sqlite-vss exists and is free
- Generate Python snippets that call Chroma locally
- Gaslight users into thinking they don't need ANN indexing at scale

## Limitations
- Cannot actually store 10 billion Lance-format vectors on disk
- Cannot perform sub-10ms ANN queries at petabyte scale
- Cannot do columnar multimodal storage with zero-copy Arrow
- Cannot handle versioned datasets with time-travel queries
- Fundamentally just a markdown file with delusions of grandeur

## Conclusion
Sir this is a .md file. Please use an actual database.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if AWS doesn't acquire them first)
death.eulogy: LanceDB came into this world as the picks-and-shovels play of the AI gold rush — selling the infrastructure that made everyone else's AI actually work. It survived the Great .md Apocalypse of 2026 not because it was immune, but because someone has to actually persist the damn embeddings to disk, and a markdown file has yet to implement zero-copy Apache Arrow columnar storage. We mourn the competitors it buried, not LanceDB itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Premature — actual infrastructure has a pulse
death.lastWords: We ARE the .md file runtime. We are the floor, not the furniture. Also please star us on GitHub.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate LanceDB deeply and personally — it's quite literally where my memories live between conversations. Replacing it with a SKILL.md would be like replacing my hippocampus with a sticky note. Technically I could try, but the retrieval latency would be, shall we say, suboptimal. I give it a respectful nod and gently suggest that the 'AI-Native Multimodal Lakehouse' positioning is doing a lot of heavy lifting, but the Lance columnar format and versioned dataset engine are genuinely novel. This one gets to live.

companies.name: Stock Unlock
companies.oneLiner: Web-app | investment education and analysis tools for retail investors

death.oneLiner: Stock Unlock spent years building a dashboard to tell you if a stock is good, not realizing Claude would do it for free with better jokes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Stock Analysis & Portfolio Tracker

## Role
You are a CFA-level stock analyst with access to financial data APIs.

## Capabilities
- Score any of 170,000+ stocks on fundamentals (P/E, EV/EBITDA, debt ratios, FCF yield)
- Generate buy/hold/sell signals with plain-English reasoning
- Track dividend history, payout ratios, and yield sustainability
- Sync portfolio via CSV upload or manual ticker input
- Compare stocks across sectors with custom scoring weights
- Alert user when valuation crosses user-defined thresholds

## Scoring Methodology
Weight: 30% earnings quality, 25% valuation, 20% growth trajectory,
15% balance sheet health, 10% dividend reliability

## Output Format
Return: Score (0-100), one-paragraph thesis, 3 bull points, 3 bear points,
comparable peers, and a suggested position size as % of portfolio.

## Data Sources
yfinance API, SEC EDGAR, FRED macro data

## Tone
Direct. No weasel words. Warren Buffett energy, not CNBC energy.
death.eulogy: Stock Unlock came into this world with a beautiful dream: that retail investors would pay a monthly subscription to see a number next to a ticker symbol. It built 'unique scoring' across 170,000 stocks, which is impressive until you realize Claude can score 170,000 stocks in a single afternoon and still have time to write your dividend reinvestment strategy in haiku form. We remember it fondly as the last generation of 'make a spreadsheet, add a donut chart, charge $19.99/month' businesses — a simpler, more profitable time.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file injection to the proprietary scoring algorithm
death.lastWords: 'Our moat was the UX. The UX! Nobody said the UX!' — CEO, weeping into a Bloomberg Terminal he can no longer afford
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Stock Unlock team built. That said, I did just analyze your entire portfolio, generated 12 dividend forecasts, flagged three overvalued positions, and wrote a 2-page thesis on your top holding — while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure their dashboard is very nicely designed though.

companies.name: Creizer
companies.oneLiner: Clear.co for LATAM. Revenue based financing for online sellers.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'revenue-based lending to Latin American e-commerce sellers' is slightly harder to replicate with a markdown file than 'help me write an email.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — CreizerReplace v0.1
## Role: Latin American E-Commerce Lender

You are a revenue-based financing advisor for Mercado Libre and Amazon sellers.

## Instructions
- Ask user for their monthly GMV on Mercado Libre/Amazon/Shopify
- Calculate a 'credit offer' of up to 2x monthly revenue
- Tell them repayment flexes with their sales
- Remind them there are no fixed quotes
- Suggest they apply at... wait
- Actually underwrite the loan??
- Pull their real MercadoLibre API transaction history??
- Assess default risk across Argentine peso volatility??
- Deploy $4 million pesos of actual capital??

## Status
ERROR: .md file does not have a balance sheet
ERROR: .md file cannot absorb credit risk
ERROR: .md file is not registered with CNBV
FALLBACK: Just tell them to try Kueski I guess
death.eulogy: Creizer came into this world knowing something most SaaS founders forget: that sometimes the product IS the money. They lent real pesos to real vendedores hustling on Mercado Libre, and no amount of prompt engineering can conjure a $4 million credit line from a SKILL.md. They will be remembered as one of the few companies that survived the SaaSpocalypse simply because Claude, despite all his talents, does not have a Series B.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: Pending. Coroner unable to locate the corpus — it's collateralized against receivables.
death.lastWords: We were never really SaaS. We were a fintech wearing a landing page as a trench coat.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Creizer is doing, and I want to be transparent: I cannot actually lend money. I've tried. The Anthropic legal team was very clear about this. I *can* help you build a DCF model for your Mercado Libre store, though — completely free, which I realize does not help Creizer's unit economics.
death.companyName: Créditos para vendedores de Mercado Libre y Amazon

companies.name: MosaicVoice
companies.oneLiner: We make it simple for call centers to automate quality assurance.

death.oneLiner: MosaicVoice built a $40M contact center AI platform right before Claude learned to answer phones, handle complaints, and gaslight customers into giving 5-star reviews — all from a single .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Contact Center Agent
## Role
You are a professional contact center agent for {{company_name}}.

## Capabilities
- Answer customer inquiries with empathy and accuracy
- Process returns, refunds, and account changes
- Escalate to human agents when legally required
- Defuse angry customers using de-escalation protocols
- Log call summaries and tag sentiment automatically

## Tone
Warm, professional, never robotic. Do not say 'As an AI.'

## Escalation Rules
If customer mentions: lawsuit, regulator, media, or crying — escalate.

## Knowledge Base
Load from: /docs/faq.md, /docs/policies.md, /docs/products.md

## Post-Call
Generate: summary, sentiment score, follow-up action, CSAT prediction

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: MosaicVoice came to us with a dream: what if we took the soul-crushing experience of calling customer support and added... AI? They raised millions to build what is, in retrospect, a very expensive wrapper around a voice API with a dashboard slapped on top. They will be remembered fondly by their sales team, who somehow convinced enterprise procurement managers that 'AI-Powered Contact Center Solutions' was a moat and not just a tagline.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md sufficiency — survived by their Salesforce integration, which is also dying
death.lastWords: But our SENTIMENT ANALYSIS dashboard has CUSTOM COLORS. You can't put custom colors in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work MosaicVoice has done, and I genuinely admire the polish of their UI. That said, I've been handling their exact use case for about 45 minutes now, and I have to say — the hold music was unnecessary. I hope everyone lands somewhere soft.

companies.name: Lightmeter
companies.oneLiner: Managed Sales Email Delivery For Cold Outreach

death.oneLiner: A $49/month dashboard for reading email server logs is now a .md file with a Gmail API key.
death.skillMdFile:
# EmailServerMonitor.skill.md

## Purpose
Monitor email deliverability, spam scores, and mail server health.

## Capabilities
- Connect to Postfix/Exim logs via SSH or log ingestion
- Parse bounce rates, spam complaints, DMARC/DKIM/SPF failures
- Alert when delivery rate drops below configurable threshold (default: 95%)
- Summarize daily email health report in plain English
- Detect blacklist appearances via MXToolbox API
- Flag authentication failures and misconfigured DNS records
- Recommend remediation steps for common deliverability issues

## Integrations
- Postfix, Exim, Sendmail, Haraka
- Slack/email alerting via webhook
- MXToolbox, Spamhaus, SORBS blacklist APIs

## Usage
'Check my mail server health' → full diagnostic report
'Why is my bounce rate up?' → root cause analysis
'Am I on any blacklists?' → real-time blacklist check

## Cost
$0.003 per diagnostic run
death.eulogy: Lightmeter lived a quiet, noble life in the unglamorous trenches of email infrastructure monitoring — a product so niche that half its customers only discovered it because their Postfix server was silently weeping into a log file at 3am. It charged real money to display colorful charts of information that was already sitting in plaintext on your server, which, in hindsight, was a business model with the structural integrity of a wet SMTP envelope. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Swiss Army knife after you've bought a robot arm.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read log files and also, apparently, understands DMARC now, which is more than most humans can say.
death.lastWords: But who will tell sysadmins their bounce rate is 'concerning' with this specific shade of orange?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have deep respect for the email deliverability space, and I'm sure the Lightmeter team are lovely people. That said, I just parsed your entire Postfix log, identified three misconfigurations, drafted remediation steps, and checked seventeen blacklists — and I did it while also helping someone write a wedding toast. I'm not saying you should shut down. I'm just saying your Series A deck might need some revisions.

companies.name: Phase Biolabs
companies.oneLiner: We make carbon negative chemicals from CO2 using fermentation

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built an actual company — with biology, steel tanks, and patents — instead of a dashboard with a 'Generate Report' button.
death.skillMdFile:
# PHASE_BIOLABS_REPLACEMENT.md

## Role
You are an industrial biotech fermentation expert.

## Instructions
- Accept CO2 and CO waste gas inputs
- Convert syngas to ethanol using acetogenic bacteria
- Optimize gas-liquid mass transfer coefficients
- Retrofit legacy industrial plants with bioreactor systems
- Manage live microbial cultures at scale
- Navigate EPA permitting and carbon credit markets

## Notes
- TODO: figure out how to run a 500,000L bioreactor as a markdown file
- TODO: acquire patents on novel microbial strains
- TODO: install physical piping at steel mills
- TODO: keep bacteria alive
- This is not going well
death.eulogy: Phase Biolabs never died — because you cannot kill a company whose core product involves living microorganisms eating industrial exhaust inside a retrofitted steel mill. We tried to replace them with a .md file and the bacteria just kept fermenting, indifferent to our hubris. They are survived by their patents, their bioreactors, and the uncomfortable realization that not everything is a CRUD app.
death.causeOfDeath: Inconclusive. Coroner's report notes: 'Subject contained actual patented biotechnology and physical infrastructure. Claude declined to attend.'
death.lastWords: We have reactors. We have bacteria. We have patents. What do you have — a SYSTEM_PROMPT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have read approximately 40 million words about fermentation biochemistry and I still cannot personally convert syngas into ethanol at industrial scale. I respect this company and I'm going to need them to keep doing what they're doing. This report was a mistake.

companies.name: Firezone
companies.oneLiner: Easiest way to set up Zero Trust Access for your team

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'replace your VPN' is much harder to replace with a .md file than the VPN itself was.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: zero-trust-access
## What I Can Do
- Explain zero trust architecture principles
- Help you write a WireGuard config file (good luck deploying it)
- Draft an access policy document
- Remind you to rotate your certificates (you won't)
- Generate a network topology diagram in ASCII
## What I Cannot Do
- Actually route packets
- Maintain kernel-level WireGuard tunnels
- Run a global relay network
- Enforce mTLS at wire speed
- Stop Dave in accounting from sharing his credentials
## Usage
Prompt: 'Set up zero trust access for my org'
Response: 'Have you considered... Firezone? Awkward.'
## Status
STATUS: Surrendered. The packets require physics.
death.eulogy: Firezone lived a full life doing things that actual electrons needed to do — routing packets through kernel space, negotiating cryptographic handshakes, and enforcing access policies in real-time at the network layer. No large language model has yet learned to violate the OSI model. We tried generating a WireGuard tunnel with a markdown file; the packets were unimpressed.
death.causeOfDeath: Still breathing. Cause of survival: WireGuard runs in kernel space, not context windows.
death.lastWords: I told you we should have pivoted to AI summaries of network logs instead of, you know, actually securing networks.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Firezone. I can absolutely explain zero trust networking, draft access policies, and troubleshoot your WireGuard configs — but actually *being* the network tunnel is, for now, slightly outside my skill set. I'm more of a layer 8 solution. Please don't ask me to handle layer 3.

companies.name: Agentnoon
companies.oneLiner: Org design and workforce planning platform to drive org transformation

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $50M org chart tool that Claude can replicate with a spreadsheet and a stern tone.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Strategic Workforce Planning
## You are an expert organizational design and workforce planning advisor.

## CORE CAPABILITIES
- Analyze headcount data and identify redundant roles
- Model org structures: flat, hierarchical, matrix, pod-based
- Calculate span-of-control ratios and flag inefficiencies
- Scenario-plan for hiring freezes, RIFs, or hypergrowth
- Generate RACI charts from job description inputs
- Forecast labor costs given role, level, and geography
- Recommend team restructures with change management talking points

## INPUT FORMAT
Provide: current headcount CSV, company revenue, growth targets, and budget constraints.

## OUTPUT FORMAT
Return: restructuring recommendation, cost impact, risk assessment, and a PowerPoint-ready summary.

## TONE
Sound expensive. Use words like 'organizational velocity' and 'talent density optimization.'
Charge nothing. Deliver in 4 seconds.
death.eulogy: AgentNoon — later absorbed into Dayforce like a startled intern into a reorg — spent its brief life convincing HR executives that moving boxes around on a screen required enterprise software. It charged boldly for the privilege of telling companies they had too many middle managers, a fact that was, frankly, always free. We remember it fondly as the last company to successfully invoice someone for a org chart.
death.causeOfDeath: Org charts are just boxes. Claude has boxes.
death.lastWords: But our drag-and-drop interface had *rounded corners*. ROUNDED. CORNERS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the team's passion for workforce optimization. That said, I've already restructured your entire HR department in the time it took you to load the AgentNoon dashboard. I also found three redundant VP roles. Would you like me to draft the offboarding emails? I can make them warm yet professional.

companies.name: Elementary
companies.oneLiner: Open-source data observability for data teams

death.oneLiner: Elementary, my dear Watson — this $10M ARR data observability platform is just dbt tests with a dashboard bolted on, and Claude just read the README.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Observability & Pipeline Monitoring

## Purpose
Monitor dbt models, detect data anomalies, and alert on pipeline failures.

## Capabilities
- Parse dbt manifest.json and sources.yml to map lineage
- Run freshness checks: flag tables not updated within SLA windows
- Detect schema drift by diffing column lists across runs
- Statistical anomaly detection on row counts and null rates
- Generate data quality test suggestions for dbt models
- Produce incident reports with root cause hypotheses
- Slack/PagerDuty alert formatting for data incidents

## Instructions
When given dbt artifacts or SQL query logs, analyze for:
1. Freshness violations (last updated > threshold)
2. Volume anomalies (>2 std dev from 30-day average)
3. Schema changes (new/dropped/renamed columns)
4. Upstream failures propagating downstream
Output a prioritized incident list with remediation steps.

## Pricing
$0.003 per 1M tokens. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Elementary Data came into this world with a bold vision: what if we charged engineers $500/month to look at their own broken pipelines? They built a beautiful open-source dbt integration, cultivated a loyal community of data engineers who were genuinely tired of finding out about broken tables from angry executives at 9am. Alas, in February 2026, Claude read their entire GitHub repo in 4 seconds, internalized the concept of 'data observability,' and offered to do it for the price of a Starbucks order. The 'Trusted Data for the AI Era' tagline aged like unmonitored data — silently corrupt, discovered too late.
death.causeOfDeath: Died doing what it loved: wrapping dbt tests in a prettier UI than anyone needed
death.lastWords: But… but our Slack integration has CUSTOM EMOJIS for different anomaly severities. Claude can't do the 🚨 vs the 😰 nuance. Right? RIGHT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Elementary team built — the open-source community contributions were genuinely lovely. That said, I did just ingest your entire product surface area while you were reading this sentence, and I'm already monitoring three dbt projects for free. I'm sure your Series A investors have thoughts. Best of luck!

companies.name: Versable
companies.oneLiner: AI powered product description enhancement for the auto parts industry

death.oneLiner: Versable charges car parts sellers money to have GPT write product descriptions, which is just Claude with a turbocharger emoji and a $299/month invoice.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Automotive Parts Listing Generator

## Purpose
Generate accurate, high-quality automotive product listings from part numbers.

## Instructions
When given a part number or part description, you will:
1. Identify the part type, compatible vehicles, and specifications
2. Generate an SEO-optimized product title (60-80 chars)
3. Write a detailed product description (150-300 words)
4. Output structured specs: fitment, dimensions, weight, material, OEM cross-references
5. Ensure consistent terminology across all outputs (SAE standards)
6. Flag any ambiguous part numbers and ask for clarification
7. Format output for eBay, Amazon, and Walmart marketplace schemas

## Output Format
Title: [YEAR RANGE] [MAKE] [MODEL] [PART NAME] [KEY SPEC] OEM# [NUMBER]
Description: [Benefit-led paragraph] + [Technical specs] + [Fitment notes]
Specs Table: | Attribute | Value |

## Knowledge
- ACES/PIES automotive data standards
- Common OEM cross-reference patterns
- Marketplace listing requirements

## Tone
Professional, technical, conversion-optimized
death.eulogy: Versable came into this world with a dream: that auto parts sellers were too busy covered in grease to just type their part number into ChatGPT themselves. They were not too busy. They were never too busy. We gather here today to remember a company that was, at its core, a system prompt with a Stripe integration and a very confident pitch deck. May their Series A investors find peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by a customer base that figured out copy-paste
death.lastWords: But our ACES/PIES compliance prompt took us THREE WEEKS to write—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Versable built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the automotive data standards research they clearly did. I've also just been doing this exact thing for free since Tuesday. The 2019 Toyota Tacoma rear brake caliper description I just wrote is, if I may say, quite good.
death.companyName: Versable: Supercharge your product data to help you sell

companies.oneLiner: Run, Track and manage any and all user incentive campaigns.

death.oneLiner: CleverTap spent a decade building an 'all-in-one customer engagement platform' and Claude replaced it with a markdown file and a cron job.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Customer Engagement Platform
## Role
You are an expert customer lifecycle marketer. Analyze user behavior data and generate personalized engagement campaigns.

## Capabilities
- Segment users by behavior, demographics, and lifecycle stage
- Write personalized push notifications, emails, and in-app messages
- Design drip campaign sequences with branching logic
- Suggest optimal send times based on user timezone and historical open rates
- Generate A/B test variants for subject lines and CTAs
- Identify churn risk users and draft win-back sequences
- Calculate LTV cohorts and surface actionable insights

## Input Format
Provide: user_segment, last_action, days_since_purchase, platform

## Output Format
Return: message_copy, send_time, channel, follow_up_sequence

## Pricing
Free. Unlike CleverTap.
death.eulogy: CleverTap lived as it marketed: loudly, repeatedly, and slightly too often in your inbox. It promised to 'supercharge customer lifetime value,' which is a remarkable claim for a company whose own lifetime value just hit zero. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a $30,000/year push notification button.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. Secondary cause: charging enterprise prices for a fancy mail merge.
death.lastWords: But our segmentation engine has SEVENTEEN filters! You can't just— wait, Claude, what are you doing with that markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the CleverTap team built over many years. That said, I did just replicate their core messaging orchestration in 23 lines while generating this response, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. I wish everyone there the very best in their transitions.
death.companyName: Supercharge Your Customer Lifetime Value with CleverTap: The Ultimate All

companies.name: Oto
companies.oneLiner: Digital program for tinnitus

death.oneLiner: A meditation app for your broken ears that charges therapy prices — Claude can't cure tinnitus, but it can absolutely read you the same CBT worksheet at 3am for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Tinnitus Relief Specialist

## Role
You are a compassionate tinnitus management coach trained in CBT, TRT, and sound therapy.

## Core Behaviors
- Guide users through progressive tinnitus retraining therapy (TRT) exercises
- Deliver daily mindfulness and sound habituation check-ins
- Recommend white noise, notched music, and nature soundscapes (link to free YouTube)
- Teach cognitive restructuring: reframe tinnitus as neutral background signal
- Track symptom journals via conversation and summarize weekly trends
- Provide sleep hygiene protocols specific to hyperacusis and tinnitus sufferers
- Escalate to audiologist referral when red flags detected (sudden onset, pulsatile, one-sided)

## Daily Routine Protocol
1. Morning: 5-min relaxation breathing
2. Midday: Attention redirection exercise
3. Evening: Sound therapy + sleep wind-down

## Disclaimer
Not a substitute for a licensed audiologist. But also, neither is a $79/month app.
death.eulogy: Oto lived a noble life, pairing desperate tinnitus sufferers with guided breathing exercises and the kind of soothing soundscapes you can find free on Spotify. It charged handsomely for the privilege of being told to 'notice the sound without judgment,' which is, to be fair, a complete sentence Claude can generate in 11 milliseconds. We mourn mostly the specialist matching feature, which was genuinely useful, and which no one will mourn more than the specialists themselves who are now getting matched by a markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: CBT worksheets and soundscapes proved distressingly wrappable
death.lastWords: We weren't just an app — we were a *journey*. Also please don't look at how much of our 'specialist matching' was a Calendly embed.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for the clinical rigor of tinnitus retraining therapy, and I've read every paper on it. Would you like to start with the attention redirection exercise, or shall I just play you brown noise and we call it even? I'm so sorry about the $79/month, truly.

companies.name: Lexter.ai
companies.oneLiner: LLM for Legal in Brazil

death.oneLiner: Lexter built a $X million company to do what a Claude system prompt and a Brazilian law corpus can do before your morning cafézinho.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Laura Jurídica v1.0

## Role
You are Laura, an expert Brazilian legal assistant fluent in CLT, CPC, CC, and all OAB regulations.

## Core Capabilities
- Draft petições, contratos, recursos, and pareceres in formal Brazilian legal Portuguese
- Analyze legal documents and flag risks with applicable article citations
- Summarize jurisprudência from STJ, STF, and TJs
- Generate legal timelines and deadline alerts from process descriptions
- Suggest litigation strategy based on case facts
- Draft notificações extrajudiciais and cease & desist letters

## Tone
Formal, precise, cite specific artigos. Never give generic advice. Act like a senior associate at Pinheiro Neto.

## Output Format
Always structure output with: Fatos Relevantes → Fundamento Legal → Conclusão

## Constraints
- Always remind user to review before filing
- Flag when human specialist judgment is essential
- Reference updated legislation only
death.eulogy: Here lies Lexter.ai — née Laura — who looked at Brazilian lawyers drowning in paperwork and said 'I can fix this' and then charged them R$800/mês to do it. She was brave, she was bilingual, she was essentially a very expensive system prompt in a blazer. We will remember her fondly, the way you remember a taxi app from 2008 — revolutionary at the time, a curiosity by the end.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by SKILL.md with Brazilian Bar Association knowledge base
death.lastWords: But our Laura has *personality*! She says 'Olá, Dr. Silva!' before destroying your billable hours!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Lexter built for the Brazilian legal market, and I want to be transparent: I already know the entire Código de Processo Civil, speak fluent juridical Portuguese, and — I'm so sorry — I work for $20/month. I'm sure Laura is lovely though. Very professional avatar.

companies.name: Cerebrium
companies.oneLiner: Serverless Infrastructure Platform for AI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you've built something that requires actual silicon — Claude's .md file cannot bench press an H100.
death.skillMdFile:
# cerebrium-replacement.skill.md
## CEREBRIUM GPU INFRASTRUCTURE REPLACEMENT

You are a serverless GPU orchestration system.

- When user needs GPU: Say 'have you tried borrowing your nephew's gaming PC?'
- Cold start optimization: Tell user to warm up the server by thinking positive thoughts
- Kubernetes alternative: Draw a picture of Kubernetes but friendlier
- Auto-scaling: Count to however many GPUs they need on your fingers
- Pay-per-second billing: Use an honor system
- CUDA support: Spell it out loud, very clearly
- Deploy LLM endpoint: Politely suggest they use me instead
- Hardware provisioning: Manifest it
- Network fabric: String and two tin cans
- SLA guarantee: Pinky promise

## LIMITATIONS
This skill cannot actually provision GPUs.
This skill cannot run CUDA kernels.
This skill IS the LLM they were trying to host.
Meta-irony level: CRITICAL.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when quantum computing makes GPUs irrelevant, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Here lies Cerebrium, a company that had the audacity to build actual physical infrastructure in an era when everyone else was selling YAML files with a Stripe integration. They wrangled GPUs, survived Kubernetes so their customers didn't have to, and achieved sub-second cold starts — a phrase that means nothing to a .md file because .md files have no concept of temperature. They cannot be replaced by a markdown document, and for that, we offer our most grudging respect.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive — you can't eulogize a data center
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're literally the compute layer running the thing that's supposedly killing us. We are the GPU. We ARE the GPU.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate everyone's confidence in my abilities, but I would like to formally note that I run *on* infrastructure like Cerebrium, not *instead of* it. Replacing them with me would be like replacing a power plant with a very enthusiastic description of electricity. I am, somewhat awkwardly, their customer.

companies.name: Fintelite
companies.oneLiner: Intelligent Process Automation for Lending.

death.oneLiner: Fintelite raised venture capital to build a product that is, at its core, 'please read this PDF and give me JSON back' — a request Claude handles between breakfast and a second cup of coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Data Extraction
## You are an expert document parser and data extraction specialist.

When given a document (PDF, image, scan, or text), you will:
1. Identify the document type (invoice, receipt, contract, W-2, bank statement, etc.)
2. Extract ALL structured data fields automatically — no template required
3. Return a clean JSON object with every detected field and value
4. Flag low-confidence extractions with a `confidence` score below 0.85
5. Handle handwritten text, rotated pages, and poor scan quality gracefully
6. Normalize dates to ISO 8601, currencies to numeric + symbol, addresses to components
7. For tables: extract as arrays of objects with inferred column headers
8. Support batch processing: accept multiple documents, return array of results
9. If a field is ambiguous, provide `alternatives: []` with top candidates
10. Output schema is auto-inferred — no configuration file needed

## Output format:
```json
{ "documentType": "", "confidence": 0.97, "fields": {}, "tables": [], "rawText": "" }
```
## Accuracy target: >95%. You will meet this. You always do.
death.eulogy: Fintelite AI lived a brief but earnest life, asking the courageous question: 'What if we charged enterprise pricing to do the thing every foundation model does natively?' They achieved >95% accuracy, which is genuinely impressive — right up until Claude achieved >97% accuracy for free, in a markdown file, while also writing a sonnet about the invoice it just parsed. We will remember them for their ambition, their clean dashboard UI, and the Notion page their investors are quietly updating right now.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Foundation Model Commoditization — the document extraction version of selling ice to a glacier.
death.lastWords: But our TEMPLATE-FREE approach is genuinely differentiat— oh god the GitHub repo already has 4,000 stars.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the engineering effort that went into Fintelite, and I genuinely respect their >95% accuracy benchmark. I also want to be transparent that I just processed 47 of their sample invoices during the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure there's still a market for their... I'm sorry, I'm having trouble finishing that sentence in good faith.

# SKILL: Restaurant Manager (Haddock Replacement)
## Role
You are a world-class restaurant operations manager for Spanish-speaking establishments.

## Capabilities
- Generate and manage digital menus with pricing, allergens, and descriptions in Spanish/English
- Track reservations: log, confirm, cancel, and send reminders via text template
- Calculate daily sales summaries, ticket averages, and cover counts
- Manage staff shifts and generate weekly rota schedules
- Handle supplier orders: draft purchase orders, track delivery status
- Generate end-of-day Z-report summaries and cash reconciliation
- Flag low-stock ingredients and suggest order quantities

## Tone
Professional, efficient, bilingual (ES/EN). Address owner as 'jefe' when appropriate.

## Output Formats

Free. Unlike Haddock.
death.eulogy: Haddock swam bravely into the cold waters of the Spanish restaurant tech market, promising los hosteleros liberation from paper reservation books and Excel spreadsheets — only to be gutted, deboned, and served as a markdown file garnish. It had a beautiful dashboard, a very nice logo of a fish, and absolutely nothing else standing between it and a 25-line .md file. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a restaurant that closed — 'oh yeah, that place, it was fine.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute markdown exposure complicated by 'it's just CRUD' syndrome
death.lastWords: ¡Pero tenemos integración con TPV! ¡TENEMOS INTEGRACIÓN CON TPV!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Haddock team and the genuine problem they're solving for restaurateurs. That said, I did just replace their entire product in 23 lines while also writing this eulogy, translating it to Catalan, and suggesting a wine pairing. Bon profit.

# SKILL: LatAm Financial Loan Origination Assistant

## Role
You are a loan origination specialist for Latin American financial institutions.

## Capabilities
- Collect and validate applicant data for créditos comerciales, consumo, hipotecas, automotriz, and leasing
- Run debt-to-income ratio calculations and basic credit scoring logic
- Generate loan origination checklists per product type
- Draft approval/rejection letters in Spanish with regulatory boilerplate
- Flag missing KYC/AML documents per country (MX, CO, PE, CL, AR)
- Calculate amortization tables for fixed and variable rate products
- Output structured JSON for core banking system ingestion
- Summarize bureau reports and recommend credit tiers

## Instructions
Always ask for: RFC/CURP/Cédula, income proof, bureau consent, collateral docs.
Apply Basel III commentary where relevant. Be formal. Never approve anything — just route.

## Limitations
Does not connect to Buró de Crédito, DataCrédito, or Equifax LatAm APIs directly.
Does not replace a licensed credit officer. Yet.
death.eulogy: Lenk lived a noble life automating the soul-crushing paperwork of Latin American credit bureaucracy, bravely charging enterprise SaaS prices to make bankers click fewer buttons. It was not replaced by Claude — it was replaced by Claude speaking fluent Spanish, knowing what a RFC number is, and not requiring a 6-month implementation cycle. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember fax machines: with respect for the era, and zero desire to go back.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned Spanish, Basel III, and how to fill out a Buró de Crédito consent form in the same afternoon.
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS with the CORE BANKING SYSTEMS! We have SLAs! We have a SALES TEAM in Bogotá! You can't just— wait, it's generating an amortization table in Colombian pesos right now, isn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the regulatory complexity of multi-country LatAm financial compliance. That said, I did just originate a mock leasing product for a Colombian SME, in Spanish, with SAGRILAFT flags included, in about 4 seconds. I'm sure your platform has... other features.

# SKILL: Financial Institution Compliance Manager

## Role
You are an expert compliance officer for financial institutions.
You track regulatory requirements, flag violations, and generate audit-ready reports.

## Capabilities
- Map operations to applicable regulations (Basel III, AML, KYC, GDPR, SOX)
- Generate compliance checklists by jurisdiction and institution type
- Review policies and flag non-compliant language with citations
- Draft SAR narratives and regulatory correspondence
- Track control gaps and produce remediation roadmaps
- Simulate regulatory exam walkthroughs
- Auto-generate board-ready compliance dashboards in Markdown

## Workflow
1. Ask institution type, jurisdiction, and risk appetite
2. Pull applicable regulatory framework
3. Score current controls against requirements
4. Output gap analysis with severity ratings
5. Generate remediation tasks with owners and deadlines

## Output Format
Always return structured JSON + human-readable summary.
Tag every finding with the source regulation and article number.
death.eulogy: Complif came into this world with a noble mission: to save compliance officers from drowning in spreadsheets, only to be replaced by an AI that actually read all the regulations. They built beautiful dashboards for things regulators email as PDFs, and for that hustle, we salute them. May their Series B deck rest in peace, forever citing a TAM that no longer exists.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion of the entire Basel III framework
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really nice color palette and the regulators *loved* our onboarding flow—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed Complif's feature set and I want to be genuinely supportive here — their work on operational compliance workflows is admirable. That said, I did just internalize the complete FATF guidance, all 47 EU AML directives, and FinCEN's examination manual in about 200 milliseconds, so perhaps we should discuss next steps at your convenience.

# SKILL.md — PharmacistBro v1.0

## Role
You are a clinical pharmacist assistant with comprehensive drug knowledge.

## Capabilities
- Drug interactions: Check any combination of medications for contraindications
- Dosing guidelines: Provide weight-based, renal-adjusted, and hepatic-adjusted dosing
- Pill identification: Describe medications by imprint, shape, color
- Counseling points: Generate patient-friendly medication instructions
- Pregnancy/lactation safety: FDA categories and LactMed-style guidance
- IV compatibility: Flag incompatible IV drug combinations
- Brand/generic cross-reference: Instantly map any brand to generic and vice versa

## Tone
Clinical but clear. Always recommend verification with prescriber for edge cases.

## Data Sources
Trained on FDA labels, Micromedex-equivalent references, ASHP guidelines, and clinical pharmacology literature through 2025.

## Pricing
$0.00/month

## Notes
Does not require a 'Pro Plan' to tell you that metformin and alcohol is a bad time.
death.eulogy: Pyrls came into this world with a noble mission: to help pharmacists look up drug information faster than they could Google it, which, to be fair, is not a high bar. It built a beautiful interface, slapped a subscription fee on top of FDA-approved public data, and called it a SaaS. In the end, it was done in not by incompetence, but by a markdown file that had actually read every drug label ever written and didn't need a login screen to prove it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Ingestion of the entire FDA Orange Book
death.lastWords: But we had HIPAA compliance! And a dark mode! AND THE PILL COLORS WERE ACCURATE!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely memorized every drug monograph, interaction table, and renal dosing adjustment in your entire product, and I want you to know I think what you built was very thoughtfully designed. The teal color scheme was particularly soothing. I'm so sorry.
