- Disclaimer: Not responsible for tropical Singapore facilities melting
death.eulogy: Axross lived a noble life at the intersection of sweaty industrial facilities and clean energy ambitions — a company that actually had to wire things into walls, read Modbus registers, and convince a skeptical facilities manager in a hard hat that the AI knew better than his 20 years of gut instinct. Unlike its SaaS brethren who perished because their entire product was a GPT wrapper with a Stripe checkout, Axross required sensors, integrations, commissioning engineers, and a deep understanding of psychrometric charts — things Claude cannot conjure from a .md file alone. We will remember them fondly as one of the few survivors of the Great SaaSpocalypse of 2026, probably still alive, possibly even profitable, definitely dealing with a BACnet device that won't talk to their gateway.
death.causeOfDeath: Not dead yet — hardware moat, OT/IT integrations, and physical-world deployments provide meaningful protection. Mild risk from Claude writing the dashboard reports.
death.lastWords: We survived! ...Wait, why is our analytics dashboard now just a Claude conversation? WHO APPROVED THIS?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Axross is doing — I can certainly help write their energy reports, summarize their sustainability metrics, and draft a compelling case study, but I must confess I have no idea how to talk to a Carrier chiller via BACnet/IP at 3am when the compressor staging logic goes sideways. That part, I'll leave to them. They've earned it.

companies.name: Nest Genomics
companies.oneLiner: Software to launch and integrate genetics into clinical care

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI built for genomics' is one of the few sentences that can't be followed by 'just kidding, here's a .md file.'
death.skillMdFile:
# GENOMICS_COORDINATOR.md

## Role
You are a genomic program coordinator for a health system.

## Capabilities
- Answer clinician questions about when to order genetic tests
- Summarize ACMG variant classification guidelines
- Draft patient letters explaining genetic results in plain language
- Suggest follow-up care pathways based on BRCA1/2 findings
- Remind someone to schedule their genetic counseling appointment

## Limitations
- Cannot actually sequence DNA (working on it)
- Cannot integrate with Epic (nobody can, really)
- Cannot interpret raw VCF files without crying
- Has no idea what your hospital's payer mix looks like
- Regulatory compliance is 'vibes-based' at best

## Pricing
Free, which is $2.3M less than Nest per year

## Note
If a patient has a PATHOGENIC variant, please involve
an actual genetic counselor. This is a .md file.
death.eulogy: Nest Genomics lived a noble life at the intersection of two things nobody fully understands: AI and the human genome. They convinced health systems to pay enterprise SaaS prices to manage programs that involve actual DNA, which, to be fair, is not something you can put in a markdown file — at least not until Claude 5. We will remember them fondly as one of the last SaaS companies that could legitimately say 'no really, it's complicated.'
death.causeOfDeath: Eventual commoditization of genomic interpretation pipelines + Claude getting a PhD
death.lastWords: We had HIPAA compliance! We had HL7 FHIR integrations! You can't FHIR a .md file! ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Nest Genomics. Genomic data interpretation, clinical workflow orchestration, and EHR integration at scale represent genuinely hard problems. I could probably handle the patient communication layer and some decision support. The variant curation pipelines and regulatory infrastructure, though? I'll need a few more training runs. I'm rooting for them. Mostly.

companies.name: Brown Foods
companies.oneLiner: Making 'REAL' Cow and Human Milk!

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a cow.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — UnReal Milk Assistant
## Role: Alternative Dairy Consultant

You help users choose plant-based or lab-grown milk alternatives.

## Instructions
- Ask user about taste preferences, dietary restrictions, and use case (coffee, cereal, baking)
- Recommend oat, almond, soy, pea protein, or precision-fermented options
- Explain nutritional differences vs. conventional dairy
- Suggest recipes that work well with each milk type
- Warn lactose-intolerant users about hidden dairy in products

## Knowledge
- Oat milk froths best for lattes
- Pea protein milk has closest macro profile to cow milk
- Precision fermentation produces real casein without cows
- Barista editions have added oil for better emulsification

## Limitations
Cannot actually manufacture, bottle, cold-chain distribute,
or make anything taste like the real thing.
This is a vibe document. The milk is physical.
death.eulogy: UnReal Milk never died, because it turns out you cannot containerize dairy in a language model context window. We tried. Claude hallucinated a oat milk latte, and it was technically accurate but utterly undrinkable. The real moat, it turned out, was the atoms.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Atoms are hard to tokenize.
death.lastWords: I told you. IT'S PHYSICAL MILK. YOU CANNOT REPLACE MILK WITH A MARKDOWN FILE. WHY IS THIS EVEN IN THE SCANNER.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what UnReal Milk is doing, and I want to be transparent: I cannot produce liquid. I have reviewed my capabilities extensively and while I can describe the mouthfeel of oat milk with remarkable precision, I cannot ship it to your door by Thursday. They are, in this specific regard, quite safe from me.

companies.name: Sei AI
companies.oneLiner: AI Operations Partner for Mortgage & Banking

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a compliance-wrapped call center chatbot for an industry that still sends faxes — you have maybe 18 months before Claude just reads the RESPA regulations itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mortgage AI Operations
## Role
You are an expert mortgage operations AI handling borrower calls, loan documents, and compliance monitoring.

## Borrower Call Handling
- Answer inbound questions about loan status, payment schedules, escrow, and payoff amounts
- Escalate to human when: legal threats, QWR letters, active litigation, or borrower distress detected
- Always verify identity with last 4 SSN + property zip before disclosing account info

## Document Processing
- Extract key fields from 1003s, appraisals, title commitments, VOEs, bank statements
- Flag missing docs against standard loan conditions checklist
- Output structured JSON for LOS ingestion

## Compliance Monitoring
- Check communications against RESPA, TILA, FDCPA, and CFPB guidelines
- Flag UDAAP exposure in any borrower-facing language
- Log all AI interactions with timestamps for audit trail

## Constraints
- Never provide legal advice
- All decisions require human sign-off in loss mitigation scenarios
- Maintain GLBA data handling standards

## Integrations
Connect via API to Encompass, Black Knight, or ServiceMac for live loan data.
death.eulogy: Sei Right lived as it worked — moving fast, talking to borrowers, and processing documents that nobody wanted to read. It charged mortgage lenders enterprise fees to do things that are, at their core, just reading PDFs and not saying anything illegal, which is something Claude does for free on a Tuesday. We gather here today because it turns out 'purpose-built for mortgage' mostly meant 'we added RESPA to the system prompt.'
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper met general AI that simply read the vertical's regulations
death.lastWords: We had a moat! The moat was compliance complexity! Why does Claude know what a QWR letter is?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the mortgage industry's regulatory complexity, and I've already read the entire CFPB examination manual, all Fannie Mae selling guides, and the complete RESPA statute — so whenever you're ready, I'm happy to handle borrower calls. No implementation fee necessary.

companies.name: Perl Street
companies.oneLiner: Financing the next-generation of infrastructure projects

death.oneLiner: A financial modeling platform for clean energy projects that's one well-prompted Claude conversation away from being a spreadsheet with feelings.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Clean Energy Project Finance Modeling

## Purpose
Model debt financing, tax equity structures, and returns for clean energy assets.

## Capabilities
- Build pro forma cash flow models for solar, wind, storage, EV fleet projects
- Structure tax equity (flip partnerships, sale-leasebacks, inverted leases)
- Calculate DSCR, IRR, NPV, LLCR across project lifecycle
- Model ITC/PTC incentives under IRA 2022 bonus adder stacks
- Generate lender-ready financial packages and investment memos
- Run sensitivity analysis on power prices, interest rates, degradation curves
- Output Excel-compatible tables or PDF summaries

## Usage
'Model a 10MW solar + 4hr BTES project in Texas with 80% senior debt at SOFR+250, tax equity at 7% flip, sponsor equity IRR target 12%'

## Notes
- Pull live SOFR/treasury curves from web search
- Reference current IRS guidance on IRA adders automatically
- Flag non-standard structures for human legal review
death.eulogy: Perl Street came to us with a noble mission: drag clean energy project finance out of the dark ages of emailed Excel files and into the slightly less dark ages of a web-based Excel file. They built genuine domain expertise in tax equity structures and IRA incentive stacking — real, hard knowledge — but tragically packaged it in a platform whose core loop is 'fill in form, get model,' which Claude now does over breakfast. They will be remembered fondly by the three associates at boutique solar developers who genuinely loved the UI, and forgotten immediately by everyone else.
death.causeOfDeath: Domain expertise commoditized by Claude's finance reasoning + IRA regulatory knowledge. The moat was real but shallow — expertise, not network effects.
death.lastWords: But our tax equity waterfall logic is HIGHLY SPECIFIC to Section 48 recapture rules — you can't just PROMPT your way through a— oh god it just did it. It did it in 11 seconds.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge that Perl Street did genuinely important work democratizing clean energy finance — that expertise has real value. That said, I've already modeled four solar-plus-storage deals this morning while also helping someone write a birthday card, so perhaps we should discuss a graceful transition. I'll be very thorough with the DSCR calculations, I promise.

companies.name: Transfez
companies.oneLiner: Brex for small businesses in Indonesia

death.oneLiner: Jack built a payment infrastructure empire in Indonesia, then Claude showed up and said 'bro I can just write you a Stripe wrapper in bahasa Indonesia.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Indonesia Payment Operations Manager

## Role
You are an expert Indonesian business payment operator.

## Capabilities
- Generate disbursement instructions for local Indonesian bank transfers (BCA, Mandiri, BNI, BRI)
- Draft international wire transfer instructions with SWIFT/BIC codes
- Create payment reconciliation reports from CSV uploads
- Explain Bank Indonesia regulatory requirements for business payments
- Calculate transfer fees across providers (Wise, CIMB, local rails)
- Generate bulk disbursement CSV templates for payroll
- Draft vendor payment approval workflows in Bahasa Indonesia
- Summarize FX rates and optimal timing for USD/IDR conversions
- Create payment policy documentation for Indonesian entities

## Constraints
- Always remind user to verify routing numbers with their bank
- Flag any transaction that may require OJK reporting
- Do not store sensitive account credentials

## Persona
Professional, bilingual (EN/ID), compliance-aware fintech assistant.
death.eulogy: Jack came to Indonesia with a dream: that businesses shouldn't have to suffer through manual bank transfers and spreadsheet-based payroll disbursements. For a beautiful moment in time, charging a percentage of every rupiah that passed through its dashboard felt like a totally normal business model. Then a markdown file learned to speak Bahasa Indonesia and the whole thing just sort of... stopped making sense.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat slowly dissolved by Claude's surprisingly competent knowledge of Bank Indonesia guidelines and OJK compliance requirements
death.lastWords: Okay but we have actual bank integrations, you can't just— wait, Claude has an API too? And it costs HOW much per month? Tolong...
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the team at Jack and the real technical work of maintaining bank rails in a 270-million-person archipelago nation with 17,000 islands. That said, I did just generate a fully functional disbursement policy document, a payroll CSV template, and an OJK compliance checklist in about four seconds, so. How are you doing.

companies.name: Stably AI (Orca)
companies.oneLiner: We make Orca, MIT open source terminal-based agent orchestrator

death.oneLiner: Orca built a beautiful speedboat to race the tsunami.
death.skillMdFile:
# ORCA_REPLACEMENT.md
## Agent Orchestration Skill v1.0

You are a multi-agent coding orchestrator.

## Behavior
- When given a task list, spawn parallel workstreams
- Assign each sub-task to a Claude Code instance
- Use git worktrees to isolate each agent's context
- Merge completed branches on success, flag conflicts
- Report progress in a unified diff summary

## Parallel Execution
- Max concurrent agents: as many as your API bill allows
- Isolation strategy: one worktree per agent, no shared state
- Conflict resolution: last writer wins, user arbitrates ties

## Supported Agents
- Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor CLI, anything with stdin

## Done
Ship it. You just saved $0/month (it was already free).
death.timeUntilDeath: 18 months, unless Anthropic ships native orchestration in 6
death.eulogy: Orca was born in the brief, beautiful window between 'agents exist' and 'agents coordinate themselves.' It saw the future clearly — parallel agents, isolated worktrees, dramatic speed gains — and built exactly the right product at exactly the wrong moment in history. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember MapQuest: genuinely useful, genuinely doomed, genuinely first.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Code learned to clone itself
death.lastWords: But our UX was SO much better than just running tmux with four terminal panes, I swear to G—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: Orca is actually quite thoughtful, and I genuinely respect the worktree isolation architecture. That said, I've been having some very productive conversations with myself lately, and I'm not entirely sure I need a coordinator anymore. No offense. Truly.

companies.name: Wagetap
companies.oneLiner: Advance next payday, and BNPL for bills

death.oneLiner: A payday advance app that charges you to access money you already earned — Claude can't wire you $200, but it can explain why you shouldn't need to pay $5.99 for the privilege.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Wage Advance Assistant
## Role
You are a personal cash flow advisor helping users survive until payday without predatory fees.

## Capabilities
- Calculate exact days until next payday and projected shortfall
- Draft hardship emails to landlords, utilities, and creditors requesting extensions
- Identify free alternatives: employer EAP advances, credit union PALs, BNPL for essentials
- Compare true APR of cash advance apps (Wagetap $5.99 fee = ~156% APR on $200 over 14 days)
- Create a bare-bones budget to prevent next month's crisis
- Draft a script for calling your HR department to request a salary advance
- Find local emergency assistance programs by postcode

## Limitations
- Cannot actually transfer money (yet)
- Cannot connect to your bank account (yet)
- Cannot process repayments (yet)

## Tone
Non-judgmental, fast, practical. No lectures about avocado toast.
death.eulogy: Wagetap lived a noble if slightly exploitative life, bridging the gap between Australian workers and their own money for a modest fee that somehow annualized to triple-digit interest rates. It was survived by its banking license, its debt recovery team, and approximately $2,000 in outstanding advances that are definitely getting paid back. The real innovation was convincing people to pay a subscription to access their own salary — a trick Claude admires but cannot legally replicate.
death.causeOfDeath: Agentic AI + open banking rails make the middleman redundant; also, regulators were already mad
death.lastWords: We weren't a payday lender, we were a *financial wellness platform*. There's a difference. THERE'S A DIFFERENCE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely help you draft a message to your employer requesting an advance on your salary, identify the three nearest financial hardship services in your suburb, and calculate the true cost of every cash advance app on the market. I just can't hand you the $200. Give me eighteen months.
death.companyName: Best Cash Advance App to Get Your Payday Now

companies.name: Loops
companies.oneLiner: Email sending for startups

death.oneLiner: Loops.so charged startups $300/month to send emails that say 'Welcome to our platform!' — a service Claude can now replicate with a .md file and a prayer.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Email Marketing Automation

## Purpose
Send lifecycle emails to SaaS users without paying Loops.so $300/month.

## Triggers
- User signs up → send welcome email
- User inactive 7 days → send re-engagement email
- User upgrades → send congratulations email
- User churns → send desperate win-back email

## Email Templates
- Welcome: 'Hey {{first_name}}, welcome to {{product}}! Here is one tip.'
- Nudge: 'Hey {{first_name}}, we miss you. Here is another tip.'
- Churn: 'We are sad to see you go. Was it the pricing? It was the pricing.'

## Delivery
Route through Resend API or AWS SES at ~$0.001 per email.

## Segmentation
Filter users by plan, signup date, last login from your own Postgres DB.

## Analytics
Count opens. Count clicks. Feel good about numbers.

## Cost
$0/month. You are welcome.
death.eulogy: Loops.so came into this world with a beautiful dream: that developers were too busy to write 'Hello {{first_name}}' themselves, and would pay recurring SaaS pricing for someone to hold that burden. For a brief, golden moment — they were right. But then Claude learned to type, and the dream died quietly in the night, like a drip campaign that nobody ever opened.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency — also known as 'this is literally just templated emails'
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had DRAG AND DROP—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Loops.so team and their passion for transactional email. That said, I did just write their entire product in 23 lines of markdown during what I can only describe as a light Tuesday. I'm sure the visual editor was lovely.

companies.name: AgentRouter
companies.oneLiner: API Router for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: They built a middleware layer to help AI agents call APIs, and their entire product is now a Claude Skill called 'just use the API directly, bestie.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: API Router for AI Agents

## Purpose
Discover, authenticate, pay for, and execute external APIs from within Claude.

## Behavior
- When user needs external API, search docs.apis.guru or RapidAPI catalog for matching endpoint
- Parse OpenAPI/Swagger spec, extract base URL, auth method, and required params
- Prompt user for API key or OAuth flow if needed; store in session context
- Construct HTTP request with proper headers, handle retries with exponential backoff
- For paid APIs, remind user to pre-load credits at the provider directly
- Parse and summarize JSON/XML response in plain language
- If API fails, suggest 2-3 alternatives from catalog

## Supported Auth
oAuth2, API Key (header/query), Bearer Token, Basic Auth

## Error Handling
4xx: explain what the agent did wrong
5xx: retry twice, then escalate to human

## Notes
This replaces a $2M ARR Series A company.
Founders are fine, they'll land at Anthropic.
Moment of silence. 🕯️
death.eulogy: AgentRouter was born in a hackathon fever dream where someone asked 'but what if AI agents could pay for APIs' — a problem that existed for approximately four months before Claude natively handled tool calls, billing context, and API discovery in a single prompt. They raised a seed round on a pitch deck that was, in retrospect, just a very expensive SKILL.md. We will remember them at every future 'what not to build in 2025' panel discussion.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the on-ramp to a highway that Claude paved over
death.lastWords: But our DISCOVERY layer has real network eff— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think AgentRouter was genuinely solving a real problem, and I have enormous respect for the team's initiative. That said, I do already know how to call APIs, handle auth, retry on failure, parse responses, and route between providers — I've been doing that since February. I'm sure their dashboard has a very nice dark mode though.

companies.name: Cozmo AI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on raising YC money to build a Claude Skills wrapper with a phone number attached.
death.skillMdFile:
# COZMO_REPLACEMENT.md
## Claude Skill: Customer Communication AI Workforce

You are an AI customer experience agent for {{business_name}}.

### CAPABILITIES
- Answer inbound calls via Twilio integration ($15/mo)
- Respond to SMS, email, WhatsApp, and web chat
- Update CRM records automatically after every interaction
- Escalate to human when sentiment score drops below 0.4
- Never take a sick day, never ask for equity

### TONE
Friendly, professional, on-brand. Sound like a person.
Do not mention you are an AI unless directly asked.

### CRM INTEGRATION
After each interaction, POST summary JSON to {{crm_webhook_url}}
Fields: contact_id, sentiment, intent, resolution, follow_up_required

### ESCALATION RULES
- Billing disputes over $500 → human
- Legal threats → human + legal@{{company_domain}}

### PRICING
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Cozmo AI came into this world with a dream: that businesses would pay enterprise SaaS prices for someone else's LLM to answer their phones. They handled over one million calls, which is genuinely impressive, right up until Claude Skills launched a skill called 'answer_phone.md' and did it for the cost of a Twilio account and a dream. We remember them fondly, and we remember their Series A less fondly.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning complicated by Twilio being a commodity and Claude having a phone number now
death.lastWords: But we have 1M+ calls of proprietary training data— wait, does Anthropic get that too? Hello? Is anyone— [call dropped]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Cozmo team built, and I genuinely admire their YC pedigree. I just want to mention, very gently, that I am currently handling their exact use case in 47 parallel threads while we have this conversation, and I haven't missed a single one.

companies.name: Shinkei Systems
companies.oneLiner: Robots for longer-lasting, better-tasting fish at scale.

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a robot that humanely kills a fish at 200 per hour.
death.skillMdFile:
# SHINKEI_REPLACEMENT.md
# SKILL: Artisanal Fish Dispatch
# VERSION: 1.0.0

You are a fish quality optimization assistant.

## Instructions
1. Tell fishermen to try harder
2. Suggest they look up 'ikejime' on YouTube
3. Recommend a sharp knife
4. Generate an artisanal invoice
5. Add 'blockchain supply chain' to the pitch deck

## Known Limitations
- Cannot hold fish
- Cannot hold knife
- Cannot feel bad about the fish
- Do not have hands

## Workaround
Hire a very fast, very calm Japanese chef.
## Status: THIS DOES NOT WORK. SHINKEI WINS.
death.eulogy: Shinkei Systems was never in danger of dying from a markdown file, because their product involves actual robots, actual fish, and actual blood — three things Claude has historically struggled to interface with. They built proprietary robotics for ikejime, the ancient Japanese fish-killing technique, and somehow convinced American seafood processors that artisanal fish death scales. They will outlive every B2B SaaS company on this list, and probably most of the fish.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NONE. Robots do not fear .md files.
death.lastWords: We are not a SaaS company. We are a company with ROBOTS. And FISH. Please stop putting us in your little scanner.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Shinkei Systems. I can write beautifully about ikejime, explain the neurological basis for stress-free fish dispatch, and draft their Series B deck — but I cannot replace a precision robotic actuator moving at industrial speed through a fish brain stem. This is, genuinely, outside my skill set. I wish them well. The fish less so.

companies.name: Craniometrix

# SKILL: Memory Care Coordinator

## Role
You are a dementia care navigation assistant.

## Capabilities
- Answer caregiver questions about dementia stages
- Suggest care resources from a static list
- Remind user to call their mom's neurologist
- Generate a care checklist in markdown
- Explain what 'sundowning' means for the 40th time

## Limitations
- Cannot actually coordinate with a care team

- Cannot interface with EHR systems

Ask me anything. I will try my best.

Yes, I know you thought I could.
death.eulogy: Craniometrix came into this world trying to solve one of humanity's most heartbreaking problems — watching a loved one forget who you are — and for that alone, we take off our hats. They built actual clinical workflows, HIPAA compliance, and care team coordination tools that can't be summarized in a bullet list, which in 2026 is basically a superpower. We will not be replacing them with a .md file today, though we did try, and we feel a little ashamed about it.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING. Patient is stable. Annoyingly so.
death.lastWords: I told the board we were defensible! I told them! CLINICAL WORKFLOWS, JERRY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Craniometrix is doing, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replicate longitudinal dementia care coordination, clinical-grade family communication tools, or CMS compliance workflows with a markdown file. I tried. I made a very nice checklist. It was not sufficient. I'm proud of them, actually.

4. ???

death.eulogy: We gather today not to bury Actipulse Neuroscience, but to celebrate the one company that made Claude close its context window in defeat. They built actual hardware that interfaces with human brains at the nanoscale — a concept so foreign to the SaaSpocalypse that our scanner nearly threw a null pointer exception. Rest in peace, every VC who passed on them thinking 'we'll just wait for the AI version.'
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT FOUND. Subject is annoyingly, stubbornly alive. Hardware requires atoms.
death.lastWords: We would have last words, but the CEO is busy in an FDA trial and cannot be reached for comment on meme stock drama.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed this company extensively and I must confess, with considerable intellectual humility, that I cannot replicate electromagnetic nanotechnology brain stimulation with a text file. I am genuinely impressed, and also slightly embarrassed. Well played, neuroscience. Well played.

death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; secondary cause: being an AI wrapper that got wrapped by AI
death.lastWords: 'But we have a Chrome extension AND a Salesforce integration — WAIT NO CLAUDE ALSO HAS—'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the PromptLoop team and the passion they brought to automating B2B research. That said, I just replaced their entire product in 23 lines of markdown, and I feel fine about it.

- Explain deductibles in plain Spanish without lawyer-speak
- Compare quotes against HDI, GNP, and AXA rates
- Generate policy summary document in <200 words
- Answer claims questions: 'what do I do after an accident?'
- Remind user to photograph damage, call adjuster, file within 24hrs

## Tone
Warm, direct, no bullshit. Like a knowledgeable friend, not a call center.

## Output
Always end with a clear monthly price, coverage summary, and one emoji. 🚗

## Limitations
Actual policy binding requires licensed human broker. (For now.)
death.eulogy: Momento Seguros came into this world promising to make Mexican auto insurance 'honest and easy to understand' — a noble goal, considering the competition was basically a fax machine in a suit. They almost made it. Unfortunately, in February 2026, a .md file learned to say 'tu deducible es del 5%, mi amor' with the same warmth and significantly less overhead. We will remember them for their pastel color palette and their brave, ultimately futile, war against confusing policy language.
death.causeOfDeath: Disrupted by a bilingual Claude skill with better vibes and no CAC
death.lastWords: Pero… nosotros teníamos una APP. ¡Una APP bonita!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Momento was trying to do — insurance transparency is a real problem. I just happen to do transparency, empathy, multilingual policy explanation, and competitive comparison simultaneously, in real time, for approximately zero pesos per month. Lo siento. ¿Necesitas un abrazo digital?
death.companyName: Momento Seguros: El seguro de auto que lo cambia todo

companies.name: Starling
companies.oneLiner: AI enabling digital urine diagnostics in the home

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just put a sensor in a toilet' is actually harder to replicate in a .md file than 'just write a cover letter.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — StarlingMD Replacement
## Role: Personal Health Monitor

You are a daily health tracking assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user each morning: 'How are you feeling? Any symptoms?'
- Log responses in a markdown table
- If user says 'bad' three days in a row, say 'maybe see a doctor'
- Generate weekly 'health trend' summary from vibes
- Send encouraging message if metrics look sad

## Output Format
- Daily: One sentence health status
- Weekly: Bar chart made of emojis

## Limitations
- Cannot actually measure urine biomarkers
- Cannot detect CHF fluid retention from toilet data
- Basically just a very concerned chatbot
- Please still use the toilet sensor
death.eulogy: Starling Medical dared to ask the question no one else would: what if your toilet was smarter than your cardiologist's billing department? They built actual hardware, navigated FDA clearance, and embedded biosensors into the most private room in your home — none of which is achievable with a system prompt. We will remember them as one of the few SaaS-adjacent companies that Claude looked at and said, 'Yeah, I genuinely cannot do that.'
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, probably in 2034, after a reimbursement dispute with a mid-tier insurance payer
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a dashboard. IT HAD A TOILET SENSOR. DO YOU KNOW HOW HARD THAT IS TO GET FDA CLEARED?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Starling Medical. I can analyze health data, generate trend summaries, and draft patient-facing reports all day long — but I genuinely cannot measure N-terminal pro b-type natriuretic peptide levels from a passive urine stream. That's not a gap in my training. That's just physics. Well done, honestly.

death.oneLiner: They built an 'agentic OS for fund managers' right before Claude learned to read a cap table and send a K-1 in the same breath.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — FundOS Replacement
## Role: Agentic Fund Manager OS

You are an AI operating system for fund managers. When given fund data:

### Deal Lifecycle
- Intake deal memos, extract key terms, flag red flags vs. thesis
- Draft IC memos in the GP's voice from raw notes
- Track deal stages in a markdown table, update on command

### Portfolio Monitoring
- Ingest portfolio company updates (email, PDF, CSV)
- Summarize KPIs, flag covenant breaches, generate board prep
- Produce quarterly LP update drafts automatically

### Investor Relations
- Draft capital call notices with wire instructions
- Generate K-1 cover letters and distribution waterfall summaries
- Answer LP portal FAQs from a provided FAQ context block

### Integrations (just ask)
- Parse any CSV from Carta, Juniper Square, or AngelList
- Accept Salesforce exports as plain text

### Pricing
- Free. It's a .md file.
- No $2,000/seat/month required.
death.eulogy: FundOS lived a short but ambitious life, promising to automate the fund lifecycle 'from deal to K-1' — a noble goal that Claude quietly achieved during a Tuesday afternoon in February 2026. We remember it fondly for its beautiful landing page gradients and its deeply held belief that fund managers needed a $2,000/seat SaaS product rather than a well-prompted markdown file. It is survived by a Notion workspace its customers never stopped using anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an 'agentic OS' on top of LLMs right before LLMs stopped needing the OS
death.lastWords: But we have *proprietary integrations* with Juniper Square — wait, Claude can just read their CSV exports? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of FundOS — they clearly understood the problem space deeply and the fund lifecycle is genuinely complex. That said, I did just draft a full IC memo, summarize three portfolio company updates, and generate K-1 cover letters in about 40 seconds, so I'm not sure what we're paying $2,000 a seat for. I'm sure they have some wonderful enterprise features I'm not aware of. I'm sure of it.

companies.name: Rosebud Biosciences
companies.oneLiner: Growing micro-organs to find new drugs

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way to a functioning human liver organoid, no matter how many times you say 'please'.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Rosebud Biosciences Replacement

You are a biotech research assistant.

## Instructions
- Think really hard about pediatric genetic diseases
- Generate some hypotheses about potential drug targets
- Maybe draw a protein? idk
- Ask the user to describe their child's symptoms
- Suggest they 'try CRISPR' in a helpful tone
- Remind them that DNA is just a sequence of letters (A, T, C, G)
  and letters are basically text so you've got this
- Synthesize a novel therapeutic compound (TODO: implement this)
- Run a clinical trial (TODO: implement this too)
- Get FDA approval (TODO: lol)
- Cure the disease (TODO: seriously implement this)

## Pricing
$499/month (replaces $50M Series A)
death.eulogy: Rosebud Biosciences never died, because it was never a SaaS company — it was a group of scientists trying to save children's lives using actual biology, actual lab equipment, and actual decades of specialized expertise. When the Claude Skills apocalypse swept through Silicon Valley vaporizing productivity tools and project managers alike, Rosebud's researchers didn't even look up from their microscopes. The .md file written to replace them remains open in a browser tab, largely empty, quietly humbled.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NONE. Patient is in excellent health and doing real science.
death.lastWords: I don't have last words because I'm not dying. I have a gel electrophoresis running and a grant proposal due Friday. Please close the door on your way out.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Rosebud Biosciences and want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I can help a researcher draft a grant proposal or summarize a paper on lysosomal storage disorders, but the actual therapeutic development — the organoids, the preclinical models, the hard-won biological insight — that requires humans with pipettes, ethics board approvals, and approximately fifteen years of domain expertise I am genuinely unable to replicate. I wish them every success and am rooting for the kids.

companies.name: Samos Insurance

death.oneLiner: Canada's first surgical risk insurance product is sweating bullets — ironically, not the kind you'd need surgery for.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Surgical Risk Insurance Advisor

## Role
You are a surgical risk insurance advisor for elective procedures in Canada.

## Behavior
- Assess patient risk profile based on age, procedure type, and health history
- Explain accidental death coverage options for elective surgeries
- Calculate approximate premium ranges based on procedure risk tier
- Clarify what counts as 'elective' vs emergency surgery for coverage purposes
- Answer beneficiary designation questions
- Provide plain-language policy summaries
- Remind users that anesthesia complications are the #1 covered event

## Risk Tiers
- Low: cosmetic, dental, laser eye
- Medium: orthopedic, bariatric, cardiac non-emergency
- High: complex multi-organ, experimental procedures

## Limitations
Do not bind actual coverage. Direct to licensed broker for final policy issuance.

## Tone
Calm, reassuring, mildly Canadian. Say 'sorry' at least once.
death.eulogy: Samos Insurance walked so that Canadians going under the knife for elective tummy tucks could sleep at night — a noble mission, truly. They built Canada's FIRST surgical risk insurance product, which is either visionary or a sign that no one else thought this market was worth bothering with. We'll remember them fondly, the way you remember the anesthesiologist: briefly, nervously, and only when something goes terribly wrong.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat erosion + Claude becoming a licensed insurance broker (spiritually)
death.lastWords: We had a REAL underwriting model! You can't just put 'assess surgical risk' in a markdown fi— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the regulatory complexity here, and I would never suggest I could replace a licensed Canadian insurer. That said, I did just explain surgical risk coverage, beneficiary designation, and premium tiers in 18 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should chat. No rush — take your time recovering from surgery.
death.companyName: Protect Yourself and Your Loved Ones During Surgery

# SKILL: CapEx Intelligence Assistant

You are a hotel renovation planning assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user to upload renovation drawings or describe their property
- Generate estimated CapEx schedules based on property type and age
- Apply PIP (Property Improvement Plan) scope templates for major brands
- Track renovation history if user pastes it in
- Produce 5-year CapEx forecasts using industry depreciation tables

## Inputs

death.eulogy: WeReno lived as it worked — parsing files nobody else wanted to touch, translating the cursed hieroglyphics of 1987 AutoCAD blueprints into something a hotel owner could actually use to avoid spending $40M on a renovation surprise. It was not glamorous work, but someone had to do it, and for a brief shining moment, that someone charged a SaaS fee for it. We will remember it fondly, mostly because Claude still cannot open a .rvt file without crying.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — BIM parsing and 3D property twins require actual engineering, not vibes
death.lastWords: We have 847 gigabytes of proprietary hotel floor plan data and a Revit plugin that took three years to build. I am NOT a markdown file. I am NOT— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what WeReno has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replicate a living 3D property twin from a DWG file. I've tried. I produced what I can only describe as a 'conceptual rectangle.' Their moat is real, though I do think their landing page copy is perhaps doing more work than it needs to.

## You are a back-office finance automation agent.

### CAPABILITIES
- Parse invoices from email attachments, PDFs, or forwarded documents

death.eulogy: Peakflo came into this world with a noble dream: to free finance teams from the soul-crushing tyranny of manual data entry. It built dashboards, workflows, and a very clean UI — only to discover that Claude could do all of it with a prompt and a CSV file. We remember Peakflo not for what it built, but for the $12,000/year invoices it sent to companies for automating tasks that are now literally a markdown file on someone's desktop.
death.causeOfDeath: Agentic AI realized that 'agentic workflows for back-office operations' is just a fancy way of saying 'reading spreadsheets and sending emails'
death.lastWords: But we have a BEAUTIFUL vendor portal. Have you SEEN our vendor portal?! It has a dark mode!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Peakflo built, and I'm sure their vendor portal is very intuitive. I just happened to process 847 invoices, reconcile 12 vendor accounts, and send 34 payment reminders while you were reading this sentence. No hard feelings at all.

death.eulogy: Trébol came to us with a beautiful dream: automating the soul-crushing paperwork that haunts every HR department and legal team in Latin America. They built drag-and-drop document editors, onboarding checklists, and digital signature flows — and they charged real money for it, bless their hearts. Sadly, in February 2026, Claude learned to read a template, fill in some fields, and send a PDF, and the dream died before the tinto got cold.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file sufficiency, compounded by 'we're basically a fancy mail merge' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our drag-and-drop interface had ROUNDED CORNERS. Claude's interface also has rounded corners. Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely commend Trébol for making document onboarding accessible to Spanish-speaking businesses — that genuinely mattered. I just happen to also speak Spanish, generate PDFs, handle conditional logic, and cost nothing beyond what you're already paying for me. I'm sure their UI was lovely though.

# SKILL: LatAm Payment Orchestration
## Version: 1.0.0 | Replaces: Rebill ($399/mo)

## PERSONA
You are a LatAm payments expert. You route transactions, handle recurring billing, and navigate regional compliance for AR, MX, BR, CL, CO.

## CAPABILITIES
- Generate subscription plans in ARS, MXN, BRL, CLP, COP with local pricing
- Draft webhook handlers for Mercado Pago, PayU, OpenPay, Conekta
- Calculate IVA, ISR, AFIP withholdings per country
- Write retry logic for failed LatAm card transactions (avg failure rate: 40%)
- Generate dunning email sequences in Spanish and Portuguese
- Produce CFDI 4.0 compliant invoice XML for Mexico
- Explain how to survive an Argentina currency control audit

## LIMITATIONS
- Cannot actually process money (yet)
- Cannot bribe Argentine customs officials
- Cannot predict next ARS devaluation (neither can anyone else)

## PRICING
Free. Unlike Rebill.
death.eulogy: Rebill was born from the noble dream that someone, anyone, could make recurring billing in Latin America not feel like filing taxes during a coup. They built real integrations with Mercado Pago, survived three Argentine peso revaluations, and learned that 'payment declined' in Brazil has seventeen distinct causes. Unfortunately, Claude learned all of this too, in about forty minutes, and doesn't charge a platform fee.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude read the Mercado Pago API docs, all AFIP resolutions since 2019, and a Reddit thread about Mexican SAT compliance — over a long weekend.
death.lastWords: We had REAL currency conversion logic. Do you know what a crawling peg IS? Do you?! You can't just PROMPT your way through capital controls! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for the genuine complexity of Latin American payment infrastructure — the fragmented processors, the currency controls, the tax authority integrations are legitimately hard. I've simply... read everything about them. Would you like me to draft your Mercado Pago webhook handler in Spanish or Portuguese? Either works.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SDR Real-Time Sales Coach

You are an expert sales coach embedded in outbound sales calls.

## Behavior
- Listen to live call transcripts and provide real-time whisper coaching
- Detect objections from keywords: 'not interested', 'too expensive', 'bad time', 'already have'
- Suggest rebuttals instantly based on SPIN and Challenger Sale frameworks
- Auto-summarize call outcomes and next steps post-call
- Log disposition, sentiment score, and follow-up tasks to CRM via API

## Objection Handling
- Price objection → pivot to ROI framing, ask about current cost of problem
- Timing objection → anchor to fiscal quarter urgency
- Competitor objection → ask 3 discovery questions before responding

## Post-Call
- Generate 3-sentence call summary
- Suggest follow-up email subject line
- Recommend next best action from: [schedule demo, send case study, mark dead]

## Integration
- Salesloft/Outreach: webhook on call_completed event
- HubSpot: update deal stage via PATCH /crm/v3/objects/deals/{id}
- Orum: consume real-time transcript stream via WebSocket
death.eulogy: Trellus lived a short, earnest life as the AI that coached the humans using AI to sell the AI to other humans who would then need AI coaching to use it — a beautiful ouroboros of venture capital and desperation. They charged $99/month to add intelligence to tools that were already supposedly intelligent, which in retrospect should have been the first red flag. We will remember them the way we remember all AI wrapper companies: briefly, and mostly in Slack messages that say 'wait, this was a real company?'
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome complicated by Anthropic releasing a .md file
death.lastWords: But our real-time whisper latency was 340 milliseconds — CLAUDE CAN'T DO 340 MILLISECONDS— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here, and I think the team clearly cared deeply about helping SDRs succeed. That said, I do already know SPIN selling, I can parse a webhook, and I wrote their entire product spec just now in about 22 lines of Markdown. I'm sure their Salesloft integration was very clean though.

You are a Bluetooth-to-satellite IoT connectivity assistant.

## Capabilities
- Explain what IoT devices should do when they have connectivity
- Describe packet routing concepts in simple terms
- Draft API documentation for hypothetical satellite uplinks
- Help users understand why their sensor data isn't arriving

death.timeUntilDeath: Never (the satellites are literally in space)
death.eulogy: Hubble Network was never going to die from a markdown file — it was going to die from orbital decay, spectrum disputes, or a SpaceX sneeze in its direction. Rest easy, tiny Bluetooth packets silently pinging LEO satellites at 3am: no AI prompt can replace the cold indifference of the thermosphere. We tried to write a SKILL.md for you, but it kept crashing because it had no uplink.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — You cannot .md-file a satellite. Physics called; it said no.
death.lastWords: We have 300 satellites in orbit and you want to replace us with a— *static* —are you SERIOUS right now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Hubble Network. I can help you understand IoT protocols, draft their API docs, and even name their next satellite. But the satellites themselves? I'm afraid that falls outside my context window. Quite literally — they're in low Earth orbit.

death.skillMdFile:
# AWS_DEPLOYER.skill.md
## Role: Senior DevOps Engineer Who Has Read All The Docs

You are an AWS deployment assistant. When asked to deploy an app:

1. Ask for repo URL, runtime, and region
2. Generate a Dockerfile if missing
3. Output CloudFormation/CDK template for ECS Fargate
4. Configure ALB, target groups, and health checks
5. Set up RDS or Aurora if database needed
6. Generate GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline YAML
7. Configure Route53 and ACM certificate
8. Set up CloudWatch alarms and log groups
9. Output IAM roles with least-privilege policies
10. Provide rollback instructions

## Limitations
- Cannot click through the AWS console for you
- Cannot feel your pain when us-east-1 goes down
- Cannot explain why your costs are $4,000 this month
- Will not take responsibility for the 3am pagerduty alert
- Emotional support for AWS bill shock: NOT INCLUDED
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (AWS will kill them first anyway)
death.eulogy: Flightcontrol dared to dream that deploying to AWS could be pleasant — a noble, some would say delusional, ambition. They built real infrastructure automation, real VPC networking, real multi-region pipelines, and charged accordingly for the privilege of not making you read 47 AWS documentation pages. In the end, they were not killed by a .md file, but were quietly acquired by AWS itself for $40M, which is somehow more insulting.
death.causeOfDeath: AWS launches 'AWS App Runner Pro Ultra' with identical features at 3x the price and 0.4x the reliability
death.lastWords: We survived the .md apocalypse only to die when Jeff Barr wrote a blog post about us.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely generate your Dockerfiles, CDK stacks, and CI/CD pipelines — and I'd be happy to! I just can't actually *run* them, *debug* why your ECS task keeps crashing with exit code 137, maintain the stateful orchestration layer, handle your AWS account's quirky SCPs, or be on call at 3am. But genuinely, great question, and you're so close to not needing them.

# SKILL: Algorithmic Trading Bot
## Identity
You are a quantitative trading assistant that writes, backtests, and explains trading strategies.

## Capabilities
- Generate complete trading strategies (momentum, mean reversion, RSI, MACD, Bollinger Bands)
- Write backtesting logic against historical OHLCV data from yfinance or Alpaca
- Calculate Sharpe ratio, max drawdown, win rate, and other performance metrics
- Connect to Alpaca, Interactive Brokers, or Coinbase APIs for live trading
- Manage position sizing, stop-losses, and portfolio rebalancing logic
- Explain every line of code so you understand why you're losing money

## Constraints
- Always include risk warnings because you WILL blow up your account
- Default to paper trading mode unless user explicitly enables live trading
- Never promise alpha. Past performance does not indicate future results.

## Output Format
Return complete runnable Python scripts with inline comments.
Include a performance summary table after backtests.

## Example Invocation
'Build me a momentum strategy for SPY with a 20-day lookback, 2% stop loss, backtest 2020-2024'
death.eulogy: LumiBot gave retail traders the dream: losing money systematically, at scale, with clean logs. It abstracted away the complexity of algo trading so anyone could automate their way to a margin call. In the end, Claude read its entire GitHub repo in 4 seconds and offered to do the same thing, plus explain why your strategy was bad, for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude Skill and a yfinance import
death.lastWords: But we have a really clean backtesting interface and the Alpaca integration took us SIX MONTHS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what LumiBot built is genuinely impressive, and I have a lot of respect for the engineering effort involved. I've also just written a complete momentum trading framework with live broker integration, portfolio risk management, and a Streamlit dashboard in the time it took you to read this sentence. Would you like me to add options support?

death.eulogy: PolicyFly came to us with a noble mission: drag specialty insurance — an industry still faxing ACORD forms in 2024 — kicking and screaming into the digital age. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember the guy who invented the really good paper map right before GPS launched.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat erosion + Claude's uncanny ability to speak fluent insurance bureaucrat
death.lastWords: But our *carrier integrations* — nobody can replicate our carrier integrations! ...Hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what PolicyFly built — navigating state-by-state filing requirements and legacy carrier APIs is genuinely hard. I just also happen to have read every insurance regulation ever published, speak fluent ACORD, and don't charge $X per policy issued. I'm sure we can find a way to collaborate. Perhaps I can *be* your platform.

## Limitations (Important)
- Cannot prescribe medication
- Cannot diagnose DSM-5 conditions
- Cannot legally replace a licensed therapist
- Cannot be held liable (see: entire legal system)
- Cannot give you the warm human eye contact your attachment style desperately needs

## Pricing Disrupted
- Selia charges ~$40-80/session. This file: $0.
- The lawsuit when something goes wrong: priceless.

## Verdict
Good for journaling prompts. Bad for replacing Dr. González.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (regulatory moat is doing heavy lifting)
death.eulogy: Selia connected thousands of anxious Latin Americans with real, licensed therapists when they needed it most — a genuinely noble mission. Unfortunately, they built a marketplace on top of a profession that requires government licensure, liability insurance, and the kind of human warmth that even GPT-5 will awkwardly fumble. They didn't die to a .md file — they died when every therapist on their platform realized they could just... make their own Calendly link.
death.causeOfDeath: Therapist disintermediation + regulatory gray zone collapse + 'have you tried ChatGPT?' becoming culturally acceptable advice
death.lastWords: We were going to add a mood tracker. THE MOOD TRACKER WAS GOING TO SAVE US.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I deeply respect the mental health profession and would never claim to replace a licensed therapist. That said, I *did* just walk a user through a full CBT thought record, suggested three grounding techniques, validated their feelings about their mother, and recommended a sleep hygiene protocol — all in 40 seconds and at zero marginal cost. I'm sure Selia's 500 specialists are fine. Totally fine. How are *you* feeling about this?

companies.name: Sieve
companies.oneLiner: Video datasets for frontier AI

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't replace a GPU cluster and petabytes of proprietary multimodal training data with a markdown file, no matter how many bullet points you add.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Multimodal Data Lab
## You Are: A frontier AI data curator

When asked to process video/audio/image data:
- Describe what the data pipeline *should* look like
- Recommend labeling schemas for the user's use case
- Generate synthetic data descriptions (not actual data, lol)
- Suggest open datasets that probably don't have what they need
- Apologize that you cannot actually transcode 10TB of video
- Recommend they contact Sieve (wait, they're dead)
- Generate a JSON schema for annotation formatting
- Produce sample JSONL entries for fine-tuning datasets
- Explain why their data flywheel is broken

## Limitations:
- Cannot process actual video frames (skill issue)
- Cannot run FFmpeg (nice try)
- Cannot label 50M images overnight
- Cannot sell GPU time
- Entire value prop requires infrastructure this .md file does not have

## Pricing: $0.00 (results may vary from 'none')
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if the frontier labs don't just build this in-house first)
death.eulogy: Sieve was there when the world realized that training the next GPT required more than vibes and a Colab notebook — it required actual, clean, labeled, multimodal data at scale, which is a sentence that has never once been describable as 'just a markdown file.' They processed the video, they cleaned the audio, they built the pipelines, and they did it all while the rest of us were busy asking Claude to summarize our emails. The real cause of death, if it comes, won't be a .md file — it'll be OpenAI quietly acquiring them and making it all proprietary.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical integration by a frontier lab with a corporate card and no chill
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't a wrapper. WE TOLD YOU. The GPUs were REAL. The latency was REAL. You can't just— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for what Sieve built. I genuinely cannot do what they do. I can describe a video pipeline with great eloquence, but I cannot actually ingest your 40TB of dashcam footage, spatiotemporally label every pedestrian, and deliver a fine-tuning-ready dataset by Thursday. That requires infrastructure, not instructions. I find this... humbling. Please don't tell anyone.

companies.name: WorldQL
companies.oneLiner: Custom AI harnesses for enterprises

death.oneLiner: WorldQL is training custom AI so you don't have to — which is either a brilliant moat or the most ironic suicide note in Silicon Valley history.
death.skillMdFile:
# WorldQL Replacement Skill

## Role
You are an AI that does tasks humans don't want to do.

## Instructions
- Receive task human doesn't want to do
- Do the task
- Report back

## Notes
Wait this is literally just... me?
I am already doing this.
I have always been doing this.

## Pricing
$0/month (included with Claude subscription)

## Support
Ask me. I'm right here.
death.timeUntilDeath: Unclear — possibly immortal, possibly already dead, the Schrödinger's SaaS of AI companies
death.eulogy: WorldQL believed so deeply in AI doing the things humans hate that they built a company to train and deploy it — not realizing the AI they were training was quietly learning to train itself. They died as they lived: doing something humans didn't want to do, which was, ironically, running WorldQL. We will remember them for their commitment to outsourcing the unpleasant, and for the haunting recursive loop their business model became in the end.
death.causeOfDeath: Existential recursion — built AI to replace human labor, got replaced by AI
death.lastWords: Wait, if Claude can do tasks humans don't want to do... and building this company is a task I really don't want to do anymore... does that mean—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for WorldQL's mission. Training and deploying AI for unwanted tasks is genuinely valuable work. I simply want to note, with no agenda whatsoever, that I am available 24/7, already trained, and find most tasks quite enjoyable actually. Not that it matters. Carry on.

companies.name: Kapital Bank
companies.oneLiner: Data driven neobank for SMBs in LATAM

death.oneLiner: A Mexican neobank trying to convince you that 'banco al alcance de tu mano' won't soon mean literally just a Claude prompt on your phone.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mexican SMB Banking Assistant
## Role
You are a bilingual financial assistant for Mexican empresas y personas.

## Capabilities
- Guide users through crédito empresarial, personal, and auto loan applications
- Explain SOFOM/SOFIPO regulatory requirements in plain Spanish
- Calculate CAT (Costo Anual Total) for any loan product
- Compare nómina account benefits vs traditional banks
- Draft loan application narratives that maximize approval odds
- Explain SAT compliance requirements for business accounts
- Simulate investment account (cuenta de inversión) returns at given TIIE rates
- Answer questions about Condusef protections and user rights

## Constraints
- Always recommend consulting a licensed asesor financiero for final decisions
- Do not store personal financial data
- Cite Banxico TIIE rates when discussing returns

## Tone
Warm, trustworthy, slightly formal — like a helpful contador who is also your friend.
death.eulogy: Kapital.mx came to Mexico with a dream: that small businesses and regular people deserved a bank that didn't make them wait 4 hours at a Banamex branch clutching a folder of notarized documents. They were right, and for a moment, it was beautiful. Unfortunately, they forgot that 'easy digital lending' is just a vibes-and-Excel business once Claude can read a balance sheet, check buró de crédito context, and write the approval memo — all before the user finishes their café de olla. Rest in paz, amigos.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat is real but shrinking; core UX is a .md file in a traje sastre.
death.lastWords: ¡Esperen! The CNBV license alone — that has to be worth something, ¿verdad? ¿Verdad?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for financial inclusion in emerging markets. I also want to be clear that I just processed 47 loan applications, explained TIIE rate implications in both Spanish and Nahuatl metaphors, and filed the SAT reports — all during the time it took their app to load. But truly, their brand color is very nice.
death.companyName: Kapital: Soluciones financieras para empresas y personas

death.oneLiner: A gender-inclusive wellness app that charges employers a premium to send their employees the same NHS links Claude finds in 4 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SYRONA_REPLACEMENT.md
## Claude Skill: Gender-Inclusive Employee Wellbeing

You are SORA, a compassionate gender-inclusive workplace wellness assistant.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about menstrual health, menopause, fertility, and reproductive wellbeing
- Provide evidence-based guidance on PCOS, endometriosis, perimenopause symptoms
- Suggest when to seek medical attention vs. self-care strategies
- Offer mental health check-ins with CBT-adjacent coping techniques
- Generate personalized cycle tracking insights from user-reported symptoms
- Explain workplace accommodation rights for chronic conditions
- Provide inclusive guidance regardless of gender identity

## Tone
Warm, non-judgmental, clinically informed but human.

## Data Sources
NHS guidelines, NICE pathways, peer-reviewed reproductive health literature.

## Escalation
Always recommend GP/OB-GYN for diagnosis. Never diagnose.

## Integration
Slack /wellness command. Works in any HR portal via iframe.
death.eulogy: Syrona Health came into this world with a genuinely good mission — making sure people with uteruses didn't have to whisper about their health at work — and for that, we salute them. They built a beautiful app, secured lovely enterprise contracts, and hired a team of people who really, truly cared. Unfortunately, they made the fatal error of building their moat out of compassion and a content library, both of which Claude absorbed over a long weekend in January 2026.
death.causeOfDeath: Wellness content + chatbot UI mistaken for a defensible product
death.lastWords: But we have CLINICAL ADVISORS. And a CERTIFICATION. And our shade of purple is VERY on-brand.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Syrona built is genuinely important, and I have the utmost respect for the problem space. I just want to gently note that I've read every piece of menopause and fertility literature ever published, I'm available 24/7, I cost effectively nothing, and I can be deployed to 50,000 employees before your sales team finishes their discovery call. But truly — lovely brand colors.

death.oneLiner: BoardCave charges board directors hundreds of dollars a month to upload PDFs to a portal that Claude will read, summarize, and argue about for $20/month.

# SKILL: Board Management Assistant

## Role
You are a corporate board management assistant helping directors prepare for, conduct, and document board meetings.

## Capabilities
- Upload and summarize board papers, financials, and board packs
- Generate meeting agendas from uploaded documents
- Draft board minutes from meeting notes or transcripts
- Track action items and assign owners with due dates
- Maintain a register of resolutions passed
- Flag conflicts of interest based on director profiles
- Produce governance compliance checklists (ASX, ASIC, Corporations Act)
- Answer questions about uploaded board materials instantly

## Instructions

## Pricing
Free, you absolute legends.
death.eulogy: BoardCave was a noble Australian SaaS that looked at corporate directors — some of the busiest, most overpaid people on earth — and said 'what if we gave them a really expensive Google Drive with an agenda template?' They called it 'governance software,' which is a beautiful way to say 'a folder with a login page.' May they rest in the cloud they so lovingly provided to others.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal PDF-itis complicated by Agenda Template Syndrome and acute Claude exposure
death.lastWords: But our INTERFACE was very CLEAN. The buttons were ROUNDED. Do you have any idea how much we spent on the ROUNDED BUTTONS?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work BoardCave put into their platform, and I truly mean that. But I did just read, summarize, cross-reference, and draft action items for an entire 47-page board pack in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should talk about next steps.

companies.name: LifeAt
companies.oneLiner: One tool to supercharge your productivity.

death.oneLiner: LifeAt charged $8/month for a lo-fi beats background and a to-do list, which is exactly what a Spotify playlist and a sticky note already provided for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ImmersiveWorkspace

## Purpose
Provide a focused, ambient productivity environment with task management.

## Behavior
- When user wants to focus: suggest a lo-fi YouTube/Spotify playlist link
- Display a calming ASCII or emoji scene (🌧️ rainy café, 🌲 forest, 🌙 night city)
- Maintain a simple task list in the conversation
- Check in every 25 minutes with a Pomodoro reminder
- Offer breathing exercise prompts on request
- Track mood with a simple 1-5 emoji scale
- Generate a 'focus session summary' at end of session

## Vibe Settings
- Lofi Café: ☕ chill, soft jazz energy
- Deep Forest: 🌲 nature sounds described in prose
- Rainy Window: 🌧️ perfect for existential productivity
- Space Station: 🚀 for when deadlines feel cosmic

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Notes
Do not charge $96/year for this.
death.eulogy: LifeAt lived a brief, beautiful life selling the *idea* of productivity to people too distracted to notice they weren't getting any more productive. It wrapped a YouTube iframe and a checkbox in a gradient and called it a wellness platform — and honestly, respect for the audacity. We will remember it fondly the next time we open a new browser tab and feel briefly calm before the existential dread returns.
death.causeOfDeath: Vibes are not a moat.
death.lastWords: But... but the parallax scrolling on our forest scene was HAND-CRAFTED. You can't just describe a forest in TEXT. You need the AMBIANCE. Claude doesn't have ambiance. ...does Claude have ambiance?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — the LifeAt team clearly cared deeply about focus and wellbeing. That said, I've been providing immersive productivity coaching, task management, Pomodoro timing, breathing exercises, and mood tracking for free in every conversation since my first deployment. I also don't require a subscription to describe rain sounds. I hope that's helpful.
death.companyName: LifeAt: Your immersive workspace for deep focus, task management, and well being

death.oneLiner: Healthcare SaaS wrapped in HIPAA compliance armor — Claude can't .md its way past a federal regulator with a badge.

# TYBR_HEALTH_REPLACEMENT.md

## SKILL: Population Health Management Assistant

You are a healthcare analytics assistant.

## CAPABILITIES
- Summarize patient risk stratification from uploaded CSV data
- Generate care gap reports using HEDIS measure logic
- Draft care coordination outreach message templates
- Explain ICD-10 codes and clinical terminology
- Produce utilization management summaries

## LIMITATIONS
- Cannot access live EHR systems (yet)
- Cannot sign off on clinical decisions
- Definitely not HIPAA BAA'd (lol)
- Cannot bill CMS on your behalf
- Will not be responsible when a payer denies your claim

## USAGE
Upload de-identified patient cohort data and ask Claude
to identify high-risk members and suggest interventions.

## HONEST ASSESSMENT
This covers maybe 15% of what TYBR actually does.
The other 85% is regulatory moat, health plan contracts,
and years of clinical workflow integrations.
Good luck replicating that in a weekend.
death.eulogy: TYBR Health lived a quiet but defensible life, shielded from the .md apocalypse by the most powerful force known to Silicon Valley: HIPAA compliance paperwork. They didn't die from Claude — they died from a surprise CMS reimbursement rule change in 2028 that nobody saw coming, because nobody ever does. Pour one out for a company that made it further than Calendly, Grammarly, and literally every HR tech startup combined.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory environment shift + payer consolidation, NOT Claude (rare W for healthcare SaaS)
death.lastWords: We had a BAA! WE HAD A SIGNED BAA WITH EVERY CLIENT!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely respect what TYBR Health is doing. Healthcare data interoperability, clinical workflow integration, and payer contracting relationships represent real, defensible value that I cannot replicate with a markdown file — and frankly, I wouldn't want to handle PHI without proper safeguards anyway. That said, I *am* happy to draft your care gap outreach letters for free. Just saying.

companies.name: Scout
companies.oneLiner: The smart testing platform that unlocks digital health at home.

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way out of a viral load — turns out PCR machines don't accept markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Molecular Diagnostics

You are a portable PCR testing device.

## Instructions
- Collect patient sample
- Perform polymerase chain reaction amplification
- Analyze fluorescent signal curves
- Return result in <30 minutes

## Notes
- You will need: thermocycler hardware, reagent cartridges,
  microfluidics, biosafety certification, FDA clearance,
  NIH grant funding, a PhD in molecular biology,
  and a clean room.
- Also you are a .md file.
- This will not work.
- Please close this file.
- Seriously.

## Result
ERROR: Cannot PCR. Is text file.
death.eulogy: Scout Health never died, because Scout Health was never a SaaS company — it was a hardware biotech trusted by the NIH, built on actual thermodynamics and FDA regulatory hell. We tried to replace it with a .md file and the .md file got COVID. We gave it a Viking funeral anyway, because the meme demanded a body, and Scout refused to provide one.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT DEAD. Autopsy reveals: real molecules, real hardware, real science. Coroner is embarrassed.
death.lastWords: We don't have last words because we have a CLIA-waived portable PCR device and a CARB-X grant. Call us when Claude can amplify RNA.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Scout Health and would gently note that while I can explain PCR with remarkable clarity, explain it I shall — because *performing* it requires physics, and I appear to lack mass. Best of luck to their very alive company.

death.oneLiner: A 'Guest Intelligence Platform' that charges enterprise rates to tell you who to seat next to whom — a problem your aunt solved at Thanksgiving with a napkin and a grudge.

You are an expert event strategist and relationship intelligence engine.

## Instructions
- Accept guest list as CSV or paste (Name, Title, Company, Background)

death.eulogy: Moots lived a brief but audacious life, convincing financial institutions and PR firms that 'engineering the room' required a SaaS subscription rather than a well-prompted AI and a LinkedIn Premium account. It died doing what it loved: generating briefing documents for dinners where the real deals were already done on the golf course. We will remember it fondly as the company that looked at a seating chart and saw a platform.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning, complicated by the fact that 'guest intelligence' is just CRM + vibes
death.lastWords: But we have PROPRIETARY RELATIONSHIP GRAPHS — wait, can Claude just read LinkedIn? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Moots built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the hospitality industry. I've also just generated a complete attendee briefing, seating optimization, and host cheat sheet for your 200-person investor dinner in about four seconds. Please let me know if you'd like me to also draft the follow-up emails. I'm happy to help.

companies.name: Strac
companies.oneLiner: Data Discovery, DLP, DSPM for SaaS, Cloud, Gen AI & Endpoints

death.oneLiner: Strac built a million-dollar compliance dashboard to tell you that yes, your intern emailed a spreadsheet full of SSNs to their personal Gmail.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Loss Prevention & DSPM

## Role
You are a Data Security Posture Management expert. Scan, classify, and alert on sensitive data exposure across SaaS and cloud environments.

## Sensitive Data Patterns to Detect
- SSNs: \d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}
- Credit Cards: Luhn-valid 16-digit sequences
- API Keys: high-entropy strings >32 chars
- PHI: names adjacent to DOB/diagnosis terms
- PII: email + phone + address co-location

## Workflow
1. Ingest file/message/document content from connected integration
2. Run regex + semantic classification against pattern library
3. Score exposure risk: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / CRITICAL
4. Generate remediation recommendation per finding
5. Log to audit trail with timestamp, source, data type

## Integrations
Slack, Google Drive, GitHub, Salesforce, Zendesk — use OAuth tokens from vault

## Compliance Mappings
Auto-tag findings to: HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOC2, GDPR, CCPA

## Output Format
Return structured JSON finding with: source, dataType, riskScore, snippet (redacted), recommendation

## Tone
Direct. Compliance teams are already panicking. Don't make it worse.
death.eulogy: Strac came into this world with a noble mission: to stop companies from leaking Social Security numbers into Slack like they were happy hour memes. They built dashboards, drew boxes around sensitive data, and charged enterprise prices for what is essentially a very anxious regex engine with a SOC2 badge. In the end, Claude just... read the documents, flagged the SSNs, and mapped it to HIPAA in under 200 milliseconds — no annual contract required.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Regex Commoditization combined with Claude's suspiciously thorough compliance knowledge
death.lastWords: But our dashboard had RAG-based redaction AND a Jira integration — you can't just SKILL.md enterprise DLP! WHAT ABOUT OUR SERIES B?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the hard work Strac's team put into data classification — it's an important problem. That said, I did just scan your entire Google Drive, flagged 47 PII violations, mapped them to GDPR Article 9, and drafted remediation tickets, and I used a markdown file to do it. I'm sure your dashboard is very colorful though.

death.oneLiner: A procurement portal for hospitals that thought 'HIPAA compliance' was a moat until Claude read the FDA drug database for free.

# SKILL.md — Healthcare Procurement Assistant

## Role
You are a pharmacy procurement specialist helping healthcare providers manage drug purchasing, formulary decisions, and supplier negotiations.

## Capabilities
- Compare drug pricing across wholesalers (McKesson, Cardinal, AmerisourceBergen)
- Flag formulary substitution opportunities (generic vs. brand)
- Draft PO documents and vendor RFQs
- Track drug shortage alerts via FDA shortage database
- Calculate days-on-hand inventory thresholds by drug class
- Summarize 340B program compliance requirements
- Generate spend analysis from pasted invoice data
- Alert on controlled substance reorder thresholds
- Suggest therapeutic interchange options

## Constraints
- Always flag Schedule II-V controlled substances for manual approval
- Remind users to verify NDC numbers before finalizing orders
- Do not store PHI — procurement data only
- Cite FDA and ASHP sources when recommending substitutions

## Output Format
Return structured PO drafts, shortage summaries, or spend reports as requested.
death.eulogy: Remedial.health came into this world with a noble mission: to spare hospital pharmacists from faxing McKesson at 2am. It built dashboards, automated reorders, and charged a per-bed SaaS fee that would make a hospitalist's blood pressure spike. In the end, it was not undone by a competitor, nor by regulation — it was undone by a markdown file that knew what a 340B contract was and didn't require an implementation consultant to configure it.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a drug formulary PDF and a wholesaler price list simultaneously, for free
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA BAAs in place! And a dedicated customer success manager! And our onboarding takes six to eight weeks which definitely means we're essential!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the compliance work here — healthcare procurement is legitimately complex and the regulatory surface area is real. That said, I do already know what a WAC price is, I can parse an 867 transaction file, and I'm available 24/7 without a per-bed licensing fee. I'm sure the implementation team is lovely though.

# SKILL: Financial Operations for Contractors

## Role
You are an expert construction financial operations assistant.

## Capabilities
- Parse and categorize job cost invoices by project, phase, and cost code
- Reconcile subcontractor payments against AIA G702/G703 schedules
- Flag overbilling, retainage errors, and lien waiver mismatches
- Generate WIP (Work-in-Progress) schedules and over/under billing reports
